Unreasonableness: can be reached after identification of JE (Li) + last resort.
Stringent: Unreasonableness (Li, SZVFW)

Improper purpose (will not be inferred) - High Bar

Conclusion
Jurisdiction [+ privative clause]
The aim of bringing a judicial review application is to establish that a breach of the limits of power has occurred.
Cth Avenue
the High Court's jurisdiction under s 75 (v) and s 75 (iii) of the Constitution, V In all matters : (v) in which a writ of Mandamus or prohibition or an injunction is sought against an officer of the Commonwealth ; Matter: a controversy (Re McBain; Ex Parte Australian Catholic Bishops Conference (2002) 209 CLR 372) .
So no hypothetical questions
An officer of the cth
An officer of the Commonwealth is a person holding an office of government in the federal system, and includes - see more.
Iii In all matters ... in which the Commonwealth, or a person suing or being sued on behalf of the Commonwealth, is a party
Section 75(iii) is clearly a broader jurisdiction than s 75(v) and may also be relied upon in civil law cases.

Section 3(3) broadens the concept even further
Administrative character (vs delegated legislation and legislative in character)
Under enactment (excludes the exercise of prerogative power and non-statutory decision-making - see Justiciability below) S3 of ADJR - 'enactment' means:
Test: Griffith University v Tang (2005) 221 CLR 99
the decision in question must be expressly or impliedly required or authorised by the enactment; and
the decision must confer, alter or otherwise affect legal rights or obligations and derive the capacity to do so from the statute as opposed to some other source.
Bond
For a decision to be a reviewable decision under the ADJR Act, it will generally, but not always, entail a decision required by or authorised by a statute which is final or operative and determinative of the issue of fact falling for consideration.
Conduct for the purpose of making such a decision (s6) S3(5) includes… preparatory to the making of the decision, including the taking of evidence or the holding of an inquiry or an investigation.
the conduct must be part of the procedure undertaken for the purpose of making, or leading up to making, a reviewable decision under s 5.
Bond - A majority of the High Court (Mason CJ, Brennan and Deane JJ)
Conduct is ‘ procedural and not substantive in character’.

Conduct is defined to complement the meaning of decision. It points to action taken , rather than the decision made, for the purpose of making a reviewable decision.
Failure to make a decision when there was a duty to do so (s7) discretionary power --> still be under a duty to determine an application.
Exception: decision made by the Governor-General / exclusion by Schedule 1 or by regulation (s 19)
FCC: The Federal Circuit and Family Court’s jurisdiction to engage in judicial review under the Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977 (Cth); The jurisdiction conferred on the Federal Circuit Court by s 8(2) of the Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977 (Cth).
+ Migration Act 1958 (Cth).
Vic Avenue
SCV: The Supreme Court of Victoria’s inherent original jurisdiction accessed under the Supreme Court Act 1986 (Vic) and the Administrative Law Act 1978 (Vic); Inherent judicial review jurisdiction has been held by the High Court to be 'a defining characteristic' of state Supreme Courts, and it cannot be removed by legislation: Kirk v Industrial Court (New South Wales) (2010) 239 CLR 531, 581 [98] – [19]. 'codified' in the Constitution Act 1975 (Vic) s 85

Access this jurisdiction via SCA 1986 / Supreme Court (General Civil Procedure) Rules 2005 (Vic), Order 56 in the nature of certiorari, mandamus, prohibition ... shall be exercised only by way of judgment or order (including interlocutory order)
Judicial review may be sought on common law grounds together with Charter-based judicial review grounds
60 days
ALA 1978 More complex for O56
But give an applicant a statutory right to reasons (not available under common law) (s10)
30 days limitation period.
S 3 Any person affected by a decision of a tribunal Decision
S2 A decision- operative and quality of finality
Tribunal : S2
Unlike the FC, these two avenues cannot be joined.
Charter Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities Act 2006 (Vic)
Grounds : Charter claims can operate as additional grounds of unlawfulness Piggy-backing (Bare v IBAC)
Factors to Consider:
Level: Cth or state
Decision Type: statute power
prerogative power: international relations, going to the war, national security
making of delegated legislation.
Decision maker: minister, a merits review tribunal or the Governor-General
Rememdies that you seek
Privative clauses and other limitation devices
Plaintiff S157: Bangladeshi Citizen applying for a protection visa. (HCA jurisdiction) The jurisdiction of this Court to grant relief under s 75(v) cannot be removed by or under a law of the Parliament

JE --> HCA JR review ganrateed by s 75(v) --> prohibition, mandamus, cirterario
Non JE --> privative clause will ouster JR
Kirk v Industrial Court (NSW) (State Supreme Court’s jurisdiction) Manager of a farm owned by Kirk’s company was killed while using an ATV
S179(1) of Industrialist relations Act provided that a decision of the industrial court is final and may not be appealed against, viewed, quashed by any court or tribunal.
State Superme Court: Ch II requires that there be a body fitting the description of the supreme court of a state.
It is beyond the legislative power of a state to alter the constitution or character of its supreme court that is ceases to meet the constitutional description.
The supervisory role of the supreme courts exercises through the grant of prohibition, certiorari and mandamus was and is a defining characteristic of those courts.
Graham (GRAHAM V MINISTER FOR IMMIGRATION AND BORDER PROTECTION (2017) 263 CLR 1) - confidential information Minister is satisfied that cancellation or refusal is in the national interest. Minister relied on confidential information and this information cannot be shared with anyone (per s503A(2)©).

Ratio: Whether a law crosses the line between permissible regulation and impermissible interference depends upon the extent to which it requires a court to depart from the methods and standards which have characterized judicial activities in the past.
Rule of law (concern of island of power / accountability) --> what parliament cannot do under any source of legislative power is to enact a law which denies this court the ability to enforce the legislated limits of an officer’s power.
I.e.. question is that whether the court can do what is supposed to do. Court needs to be able to ascertain whether certain information is admissiable.
Remedies (pt I)
ADJR Act See section 16 - Flexible access to remedies
Writs Certiorari: to quash the legal effect of consequence of a decision (Ainsworth) Need to mention - Under the constitution, this must be sought as ancillary relief to mandamus/prohibition.
It has long been accepted that certiorari may issue as ancillary to the constitutional writs of mandamus and prohibition (Plaintiff S157)
Mandamus: to compel a public official to perform their duty according to law
Prohibition: to stop a decision-maker from deciding illegally or continuing an unlawful course of action.

Equitable relief ( Enfield - based on discretion of the court) Injunction: mandatory (to do a certain act) or prohibitory (to retain from doing a certain act) the application must have a legal right ( Bateman’s Bay/Smethurst (HCA) )
Declaration: statement of pre-existing rights or parties (see Ainsworth) Requires that there be an actual and not hypothetical controversy ( Enfield )
Bare Declaration
Judicial Discretion to refuse relief (but presummption is to provide remedies Enfield )
Standing
Historical to open standing (book p300-306)
ADJR Act ss3(4), 5 and 6: person aggrieved - Argos: right to life
Harm to economic interest can be sufficient ( Argos ) - landlord for supermarket
Standing under the ADJR Act is an enabling criterion, which is not to be restricted or confined such as by primary (enabling) legislation (Argos)
Test: ADJR Act test of standing should not be given a narrow meaning (Ellicott J in Tooheys Ltd v Minister for Business and Consumer Affairs (1981) 36 ALR 64): beyond that which he or she has as an ordinary member of the public.
Writs (Standing and remedies are more interrelated under constitution and Judiciary Act pathway) Certiorari "ancillary" or "incidental" ( Aala )
Requires sufficient effect on a legal right or interest ( Hot Holdings ) - condition precedent
Personal and business reputations are included in interest ( Ainsworth )

Even a stranger can seek these remedies ( McBain / ACF ), but discretionary consideration strengthened (See Aala )
Prohibition (same as above)
Mandamus: Standing test: whether a prosecutor can demonstrate a sufficient interest or special legal right in enforcing a public duty that is owed to them.
Key issue is whether there is a public duty that remains unperformed Martin v Nalder [2016] WASC 138 - Uber case - minister has discretion on this. So no public duty.
Equitable Relief General: Applicant must have a special interest in the subject matter of the action, more than the interest of an ordinary members of the public in uploading the law, and more than a mere emotional or intellectual concern ( ACF; Onus ) Religious and cultural connection: Onus
Right to speak not enough ( not be remote, indirect or fanciful ): Right to life
Competing community funeral fund business: Bateman’s Bay
To seek an injunction, the application must have a legal right (Bateman’s Bay) - Reason: if you can get some other remedies, you will not get injunction
Standing
Grounds
Without authority?
Improper purpose?
Relevant consideration? + charter
Irrelevant consideration?
Invalid delegation?
PF: hearing rule? (done)
PF: bias? (done)
Objective jurisdictional fact? (done)
Subjective jurisdictional fact? (done)
Unreasonableness? (done)
Error of law?
Unlawful policy? (done)

Inflexible application of policy? (done)
Consideration of regulations
Details
Without authority?
Issue — Whether the Minister acted without authority by granting a launch facility licence for a term exceeding the maximum permitted by the Act.
Rule (ratio) — A statutory power may be exercised only within the scope conferred by the empowering statute, and the scope of that authority is determined by statutory construction; an officer or decision-maker who acts outside that scope acts without legal authority, and the action is susceptible to judicial review: ABC Developmental Learning Centres Pty Ltd v Secretary, Department of Human Services [2007] VSC 37; (2008) 15 VR 489 (Hollingworth J).
Error of Law - was the power correctly exercised? Rule (ratio) Error of law arises where a decision-maker, while exercising a power they hold, makes a legal mistake in the course of doing so — typically by misconstruing a statutory term , applying the wrong legal test, or misunderstanding the legal limits of their power. It is distinct from acting without authority: the decision-maker has power to act, but has exercised it on an erroneous legal basis. They can overlap in certain circumstances.

Where the legal error does not go to the jurisdictional limits of the power — but is nonetheless visible on the face of the decision-maker's reasons or record — it may still be correctable by certiorari at common law (Craig v South Australia; Kirk v Industrial Court (NSW) (2010) 239 CLR 531). Prohibition will not issue for non-jurisdictional error of law on the face of the record (Seminar 8). A privative clause can validly exclude judicial review for non-jurisdictional error of law on the face of the record (Kirk; Plaintiff S157/2002 v Commonwealth (2003) 211 CLR 476).
Improper purpose? Identify the improper purpose Statutory power may only be exercised for the purpose/s for which it has been conferred (Toohey) Statute’s objects
Even if a power is wide/discretionary, it mus still be exercised for the purpose/s of the Act (Sclieske)
Test: Was the exercise of power substantially for that improper purpose? The onus of establishing that the decision-maker acted for an improper purposes lies on the applicant (Toohey)
If a power has been exercised for more than one purpose, if that ulterior purpose is substantial then it will be an abuse of power; it does not have to be the sole purpose (Samrein)
This is a question of fact (Samrein)

Relevant consideration? Statutory construction The subject matter, scope and purpose of the act may either expressly state, or necessarily imply, that a certain consideration must (or must not) be taken into account (Mason J, Peko-Wallsend)
Mandatory relevant considerations Error can only be established if a decision-maker fails to account for a consideration which they are bound to take into account (Mason J, Peko-Wallsend)
The degree of effort that consideration requires will vary according to the length, content, and degree of relevance (Tickner; see also Plaintiff M1). Report is important, it should take into the consideration. Need to mention that in the reasoning.
The intellectual process mentioned in other cases is just a label. It should be a fact based enquiry.
It is generally for the decision-maker to determine what the appropriate weight to afford a consideration is (Peko-Wallsend)
Proper consideration of the charter (In Victoria Only) Section 38(1) “give proper consideration to a relevant human right” has a procedural and a substantive limb (Tate JA, Bare; Thompson). The limbs are cumulative (Certain children) Procedural limb
Operates as a constraint on the exercise of public power (Tate JA, Bare)
It is a supplementary obligation upon public authorities, beyond that imposed under the primary legislation (Tate JA, Bare)

Requires application of the four limbs of the HJ test (Bare; certain child - chilren sent to Barwon adult maximum security prison; thompson - Barwon prison - random drug test and stripe search)
Step 1: Understand in general terms which of the rights of the person affected by the decision may be relevant and whether, and if so how, those rights will be interfered with by the decision;
Step 2: seriously turn their mind to the possible impact of the decision on the a person’s human rights and the implications for the affected person;
Best to use the analogy to explain this (using Bare - police assult on Bare African person, look at the complaint and look at the long term impact)
Step 3: identify the countervailing interest or obligations (such as policy and procedures - bowen prison's drug situation is quite bad); and
Step 4: balance completing private and public interests as part of the exercise of justification.
Proper consideration is a higher standard than that generally applicable at common law to taking into account relevant consideration.
So this imposes additional consideration to decision-maker.
Substantial limb
Applicant need to established the fact that a human right is identified as limited by the action of a public authority.

Once a human right is identified as limited by the action of a public authority, burden is on the state to show that the limitation is “demonstrably justified” consistently with section 7(2) a to e(Certain Children; Thompson)
Court approaches s7(2) with limited degree of deference to the decision-maker (Certain Children)
Consequence of breach s 38(1)
While it is unlawful for a public authority to breach either obligation in s38(1), that unlawfulness does not of itself give rise to charter invalidity and in relation to a decision, does not constitute jurisdictional error (Certain Children)
Case:
Bare (Ethiopian descent - police cruelty - IBAC not investigating)
s10(b) Protection from torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment
s8 the right to equal protection of the law without discrimination
Certain Children (children being moved to Barwon adult maximum security prison) - setting rules only
THOMPSON V MINOGUE (2021) (random drug test and stripe search in Barwon Prison)
S13(a) Privacy
Irrelevant consideration? Statutory construction The subject matter, scope and purpose of the act may either expressly state, or necessarily imply, that a certain consideration must (or must not) be taken into account (Mason J, Peko-Wallsend)
So this is statutory construction.
It will usually go along with improper purpose.

Invalid delegation? MINISTER FOR ABORIGINAL AFFAIRS V PEKO-WALLSEND (1986) 162 CLR 24 (Mason J) The presence of an express statutory power of delegation does not necessarily exclude the existence of an implied power to delegate or act through the agency of others
There are cases which establish that when a Minister is entrusted with administrative functions he may, in general, act through a duly authorised officer of his Department: Carltona
Ratio : The cases in which the principle has been applies are cases in which the nature, scope and purpose of the function vested in the repository made it unlikely that Parliament intended that it was to be exercised by the repository personally because administrative necessity indicated that it was impractical for them to act otherwise than through their officers
PATTENDEN V COMMISSIONER OF TAXATION [2008] FCA 1590 (Logan J) Whether it is lawful for a power consigned by statute to a designated person to be exercised in the name of that person by a duly authorised subordinate depends on whether the statute requires the power personally to be exercised by its designated repository
That depends on the nature of the power and other circumstances – eg, size of department, geographic area for which department is responsible, frequency of the exercise of power.

There is authority that the ability of a subordinate to exercise in the name of a Minister a statutory power conferred on the Minister need not be found in a formal instrument but may be found in departmental practice
PF: hearing rule? Prior hearing rule: decision-maker must give the person prior notice that a decision may be made, provide them with the information (particularly prejudicial information) on which the decision may be based and advise them of the person’s right to make a submission in reply.
The hearing rule entitles a person whose interests are liable to be affected to be given notice of relevant matters and a reasonable opportunity to present his or her case. This includes: a duty of disclosure whereby adverse information that is ‘credible, relevant and significant’ (Kioa) to the decision to be made should be disclosed in an opportunity given to deal with it; or,
giving parties the opportunity to make submissions where a tribunal proposes to decide a case on a different basis or by way of a different procedure than initially expected or agreed (WZARH).
Test 1. Threshold: Does the hearing rule apply to the specific statutory power under consideration? Process of statutory construction (Badari; Brennan J, Kioa; Saeed; WZARH)
Consider whether there is a clear parliamentary intention to displace procedural fairness (Badari; Saeed) - presumption see below in Badari.

2. Content: If it does apply, what does the hearing rule require? Flexible obligation, appropriate and adapted to the circumstances (Badari; Kioa; WZARH)
Chance to respond (Kioa)
Additional evidence and submissions relied by Minster (family violence) without letting the person know (NATHANSON)
Interviewer change with no further interview (Wzarh)
demeanour matters
Language
Individual affected: BADARI (changing method of rent calculation)
3. To be determined by looking to the facts and construing the Act’s scope and nature of the power exercised
The content can range from requiring a full trial to nothingness (Badari; Brennan J, Kioa)
Where information is credible, relevant and significant, there is generally a requirement that there be an opportunity to respond (Brennan J, Kioa)
PF: bias?
Bias rule A decision-maker must be free of any reasonable apprehension of bias or preconception arising from circumstances such as the decision-maker’s financial or personal interests, prior expression of views, or previous role in the decision to be made. Actual bias Jia Legeng 2001 - Minister voiced concern over the radio
Gleeson CJ and Gummow J Test: The state of mind described as bias in the form of prejudgment is one so committed to a conclusion already formed as to be incapable of alteration, whatever evidence or arguments may be presented

Natural justice does not require the absence of any predispositions or inclination for or against an argument/conclusion
The Minister was (only) obliged to give genuine consideration to the issues raised by ss 501, 502 and to bring to bear on those issues a mind that was open to persuasion
Need to establish the decision-maker’s subjective state of mind
Reasonable apprehension of bias EBNER V OFFICIAL TRUSTEE IN BANKRUPTCY (2000) 205 CLR 337 The trial judge had disclosed during the trial that he was a beneficiary
Gleeson CJ, McHugh, Gummow and Hayne JJ
Application of the principle outside the judicial system must sometimes recognise and accommodate differences between court proceedings and other kinds of decision-making. This will make a difference when we deal with the step 2 of the test.
Test: A judge is disqualified if a fair minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question the judge is required to decide
The question is one of possibility (real and not remote) – not probability
Two step test:
First, it requires the identification of what is said might lead a judge to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits
Second, there must be an articulation of the logical connection between the matter and the feared deviation from the course of deciding the case on its merits.

Objective jurisdictional fact? It is a question of statutory construction as to whether a criterion is one the satisfaction of which enlivens the power of the decision-maker to exercise their power (Enfield; M70) What is not: When a criterion conditioning the exercise of statutory power involves assessment and value judgments on the part of the decision-maker, it is difficult to characterise the criterion as a jurisdictional fact (M70, French CJ)
Whether a fact is objective or subjective also a question of statutory construction (m70)
Objective to be determined by the Court on the evidence before them(Plurality, M70; Enfield)
M70 (Malaysia solution - Malaysia complies with human rights) Section 198A(3)(a) does not refer to the Minister being satisfied of the existence of the criteria or provide that the Minister’s forming of an opinion about those matters is a condition for the exercise of the discretion to make a declaration
The matters stated in (i)-(iii) are not established by examination only of what has happened, is happening or may be expected to happen in the relevant country; the access and protections to which those sub-paras refer must be provided as a matter of legal obligation

Subjective jurisdictional fact? Subjective Whether a power is expressly, or by necessary implication, conditioned upon the formation of a state of mind by the decision-make, the existence of the state of mind will itself be a jurisdictional fact (French CJ, M70)
Where a power is expressly, or by necessary implication, conditioned upon the formation of a state of mind by the decision-maker, the existence of the state of mind will itself be a jurisdictional fact (French CJ, M70) based on the satisfaction or opinion of the decision-maker, see M70 and SZMDS
The requisite opinion or belief is a jurisdictional fact. If based upon a misconstruction of one or more of the matters, the opinion or belief is not that which the subsection requires in order that the power be enlivened (French CJ, M70) MINISTER FOR IMMIGRATION AND CITIZENSHIP V SZMDS (2010) 240 CLR 611 FACTS: SZMDS was a Pakistani citizen requesting for refugee due to his homosexuality.
Questions: Had the RRT properly formed its state of satisfaction as to whether Australia owed SZMDS protection obligations?
Two approaches from SZMDS - Stronger focus on reasoning by Gummow and Keifel, compared to a stronger focus on the conclusion by Crennan and Bell JJ For Gummow and Keifel J (dissenting): involves an analysis of the logicality and rationality of the assumptions made

For Crennan and Bell JJ: correct approach is to ask whether it was open to the decision-maker to engage in this process of reasoning that it did, and to make the findings on the material before it as it did
Threshold here is whether no rational decision-maker could have arrived at the same conclusion on the same evidence.
Not every lapse in logic will give rise to jurisdictional error. A court should be slow, although not unwilling, to interfere in an appropriate case.
the test for illogicality or irrationality must be to ask whether logical or rational or reasonable minds might adopt different reasoning or might differ in any decision or finding to be made on evidence upon which the decision is based. If probative evidence can give rise to different processes of reasoning and if logical or rational or reasonable minds might differ in respect of the conclusions to be drawn from that evidence, a decision cannot be said by a reviewing court to be illogical or irrational or unreasonable, simply because one conclusion has been preferred to another possible conclusion.
Unreasonableness? MINISTER FOR IMMIGRATION AND CITIZENSHIP V LI (2013) 249 CLR 332 Facts Li’s new migration agent informed the Tribunal that the skills assessing authority had make mistakes in the assessment, and requested an adjournment so a correct assessment could occur

Tribunal refused to exercise its power to adjourn under the Migration Act to allow for a reassessment and affirmed the delegate’s decision to refuse the visa
Ratio : Unreasonableness is a conclusion which may be applied to a decision which lacks an evident and intelligible justification
MINISTER FOR HOME AFFAIRS V DUA16 (2020) 271 CLR 550 DUA16: The agent fraudulent used pro forma submissions which contained some information relevant to DUA16 but also information relating to a different applicant
CHK16: the agent submitted information solely relating to a different applicant. DUA16:
the statutory context and the high threshold of legal unreasonableness precludes a conclusion that it could be legally unreasonable for the IAA to fail to get new information
CHK16:
It is legally unreasonable.
Unlawful policy? Lawfulness of policy When statutory discretion is available, the policy cannot restrict that. - It must not apply a policy that is unlawful, inconsistent with the statute, or leads to an improper exercise of discretion. ( Drake No 2 )
Lawful policy “leaves the range of discretion intact while guiding the exercise of the power” (Drake No 2) Inconsistency between statute and policy
Guiding the decision vs decide the decision

Inflexible application of policy? (same as merits review) Inflexible application of policy : The application of the general rule to the plaintiff contained no suggestion of anything other than an inflexible rule, a rule which prevented her from being considered. (Green v Daniels)
Appropriate policy guides, but does not control, decision-making (Drake [No 2]/Green v Daniel)
An appropriate policy is one which guides but does not control decision-making, and which is informative of the standards and values which the minister usually applies. Drake [No 2]
Consider regulation
the jurisdictional basis for challenging the validity of delegated legislation at the Commonwealth level is under s39B of the Judiciary Act 1903 (Cth) rather than the ADJR Act, which only applies to decisions of an ‘administrative character’. The delegated legislation of the States and Territories may be challenged under the inherent jurisdiction of the respective Supreme Courts.
Judicial review of delegated legislation the jurisdictional basis for challenging the validity of delegated legislation at the Commonwealth level is under s39B of the Judiciary Act 1903 (Cth) rather than the ADJR Act, which only applies to decisions of an ‘administrative character’. The delegated legislation of the States and Territories may be challenged under the inherent jurisdiction of the respective Supreme Courts.

R v Toohey; Ex parte Northern Land Council (1981) 151 CLR 170 [11.2.12C] Facts:
Improper purpose for using planning act to oust native title claim
Issue: Whether the regulation-making power could be challenged on the ground that it was exercised for an unauthorised (improper) purpose — and, in particular, whether the exercise of that power was insulated from review because the Administrator was, or was treated as, a representative of the Crown.
Toohey J: an inquiry could not be held into the motives of the Administrator in making the regulation.
Ratio: Gibbs J The nature and extent of the power must be inferred from a construction of the act read as a whole.
A statutory power may be exercised only for the purposes for which it is conferred.
No inquiry may be made into the motives of the legislature in enacting a law, but where a statute confers powers upon an officer or a statutory body and either by express provision or by reason of the general character of the status it appears that the powers were intended to be exercised only for a particular purpose , then the exercise of the power not for such purpose but for some ulterior object will be invalid.

Vanstone v Clark [2005] 147 FCR 299 (Weinberg J's judgment) This case concerns delegated legislation under the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Commission Act 1989 (Cth) (ATSIC Act). The Act allowed the Minister to suspend a Commissioner for ‘misbehaviour’ (s 40), and to define ‘misbehaviour’ by legislative determination (s 4A).
Ratio: Test: The question is whether the delegated legislation is capable of being reasonably considered to be appropriate and adapted to achieve the prescribed purpose.
Delegated legislation may be invalid for any one of a number of reasons: Because it purports to deal with a subject not within the scope of the power provided in the empowering act
It may deal with such a subject, but in a manner that exceeds the prescribed limits within which the legislation must fall
Because it is inconsistent with, or repugnant to, the empowering act, or some other act
Because the power to make the delegated legislation has been exercised not for the purpose set out in the empowering Act, but for some other impressible purpose. --> Toohey
Because its effect is so unreasonable that it cannot be regarded as falling within the contemplation of the legislature in enacting the empowering act, or is not reasonably proportionate to the empowering provisions of that act.
Analysis steps for regulation
Step 1: Regulation invalid?

Step 2: How does that affect the decision? Link is consideraiton of irrelevant consideration (maybe legally unreasonable)
Consequences of breach
Undertake two step JE/Consequence of breach analysis test the full ‘two-stage’ jurisdictional error / consequences of breach analysis for your ground, informed by the statutory construction task outlined in PBS and the principles on materiality per LPDT: 1. Has there been a failure to comply with a precondition to or condition on the power (ie, a breach), of a kind that Parliament would have intended should lead to invalidity? (PBS) Project Blue Sky: NZ company suing ABA for requirement of AU content in breach of trade agreement. A better test for determining the issue of validity is to ask whether it was a purpose of the legislation that an act done in breach of the provision should be invalid
Reasoning: The fact that s 160 regulates the exercise of functions already conferred on the ABA rather than imposes essential preliminaries to the exercise of its functions strongly indicates that that it was not a purpose of the Act that a breach was intended to invalidate any act done in breach
2. Was the breach material? (LPDT) Test: there is a r ealistic possibility that the decision that was made in fact could have been different if the error had not occurred.
Effect of invalid decision:

BHARDWAJ Tribunal affirmed cancellation of visa and then revoked the cancellation.
Gaudron and Gummow JJ: A decision that involves jurisdictional error is a decision that lacks legal foundation and is properly regarded, in law, as no decision at all
Standing
Grounds
Breach
Consequences of breach
Remedies (pt II)
Conclusion

Administrative Law Review Notes
Merits review
Merits review
Merits review, sometimes referred to as ‘administrative review’, is conducted by a decision-maker working within the executive branch of government; it is not an exercise of judicial power.
Merits review is most commonly undertaken by senior public servants and members of administrative review tribunals.
Nature
Key principles under merits review are:
‘Step into the shoes’: ‘Step into the shoes’ of the decision-maker, who originally exercised a statutory power or made a decision under statute (such as whether a person is entitled to a visa or liable for child support payments), ART s52 Tribunal is not bound by rules of evidence
ART S54 Tribunal can exercise powers of decision-maker
Correct or preferable decision (against good government) : The object of the review undertaken by the tribunal has been said to be to determine what is the ‘correct or preferable decision’ (Justice Kiefel - Shi v Migration Agents Registration Authority (2008) 248 ALR 390, 422 [140]) ‘Preferable’ is apt to refer to a decision which involves discretionary considerations.

The Registrar’s decision to refuse Marrambik’s application to change his name is subject to merits review .

S42 of VCAT Act hold that VCAT has review jurisdiction where an enabling Act expressly confers it, and in this context the relevant enabling Act is the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act 1996 (Vic) (“BDMR Act”).
Section 52 of the BDMR Act provides the statutory basis for review. Under s 52(1) , “a person whose interests are affected by a decision of the Registrar under this Act may apply to the Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal for review of the decision.”
Time limit
Under BDMR Act s 52(2) , an application must be lodged within 28 days of:
the date of the decision, or
if a statement of reasons is requested under the VCAT Act, the date the statement is provided (or the date the applicant is informed that reasons will not be given).
VCAT Act s48 holds that The review jurisdiction of the Tribunal is invoked by a person who is entitled to do so by or under an enabling enactment applying to the Tribunal in accordance with section 67 for review of a decision under that enactment. Therefore, the time limit set under BDMR Act s 52(2) is reinforced by VCAT Act s48 , which govern when review jurisdiction is invoked.
Provided Marrambik files within the 28‑day period (or within the extended period triggered by a reasons request), he meets the temporal requirement for standing.
Conclusion

Conclusion: the Registrar’s refusal is reviewable on the merits by VCAT, with jurisdiction sourced directly from s 52 of the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act 1996 (Vic) .

Standing to Seek Merits Review
If merits review is available, Marrambik would have standing to bring the application. Standing turns on three elements: (1) whether there is a reviewable decision; (2) whether Marrambik is a person whose interests are affected;
1. Reviewable decision
BDMR Act specifically allows registrar to register name change.
A threshold requirement is that there must be a “decision” capable of review. Under VCAT Act 1998 (Vic) s 4(1)(h) , a person “makes a decision” if they refuse to do any act or thing. The Registrar’s refusal to register Marrambik’s name change therefore constitutes a “decision”.

Further, s 4(2)(a) provides that a decision is made “under an enactment” if it is made in the exercise or purported exercise of a statutory function. This reflects the principle in Brian Lawlor Automotive Pty Ltd (1979) , where the AAT held that a decision is reviewable even if it is legally ineffective, provided it is made in the purported exercise of statutory power.
Here, the Registrar’s refusal was made in the exercise of powers conferred by the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act 1996 (Vic) . It is therefore a reviewable decision for the purposes of merits review.
2. A person whose interests are affected
Standing for merits review is governed by the enabling Act. Under BDMR Act s 52(1) , “a person whose interests are affected by a decision of the Registrar under this Act may apply to VCAT for review.”
The meaning of “interests” is expanded by VCAT Act s 5 , which provides that:
s 5(a): “interests” means interests of any kind, not limited to proprietary or financial interests;
s 5(b): a person may apply whether their interests are directly or indirectly affected.
Definition of Interest is not limited. Consistent with the enabling act.

Marrambik plainly has an interest in the registration of his own legal name. His interest is personal, cultural, and identity‑based. These are well within the broad, inclusive definition of “interests” in s 5. The Registrar’s refusal directly affects that interest.
Accordingly, Marrambik satisfies the standing requirement in s 52(1).
Marrambik would have standing to seek merits review. The Registrar’s refusal is a reviewable decision made in the purported exercise of statutory power; Marrambik is a person whose interests are affected within the meaning of s 52(1); and he may bring the application provided he complies with the 28‑day time limit in s 52(2).

Power and Function
In conducting merits review of the Registrar’s refusal to change Marrambik’s name, VCAT exercises the powers and functions conferred by both the VCAT Act 1998 (Vic) and the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act 1996 (Vic) (“BDMR Act”). These powers determine the scope of VCAT’s authority and the nature of the decision it must make.
1. VCAT’s Merits Review Function: Standing in the Shoes of the Decision‑Maker

VCAT conducts a merits review , meaning it must determine the correct or preferable decision on the material before it. This principle is established in:
Drake v Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs (No 1) — merits review requires the tribunal to make the “correct or preferable” decision.
Frugtniet v ASIC — the tribunal “stands in the shoes” of the original decision‑maker and re‑exercises the statutory power.
This approach is codified in VCAT Act s 51(1)(a) :
“In exercising its review jurisdiction… the Tribunal has all the functions of the decision‑maker.”
Accordingly, VCAT does not merely assess whether the Registrar acted lawfully; it re‑makes the decision afresh , applying the same statutory criteria that bind the Registrar.
2. VCAT’s Remedial Powers Under s 51(2)
VCAT Act s 51(2) sets out the orders VCAT may make on review:
affirm the decision
vary the decision
set aside the decision and substitute its own
set aside and remit the matter with directions
These broad remedial powers allow VCAT to fully reconsider the merits of the name‑change application and, if appropriate, substitute its own decision granting the name change.
3. Relevant Statutory Powers Under the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act
The enabling Act defines the substantive powers and functions that VCAT has.
BDMR Act s 24 — empowers the Registrar to register a change of name.

BDMR Act s 25 — sets out the matters the Registrar must consider when deciding whether to register a name change (e.g., identity, public interest, fraud prevention).
Because VCAT “stands in the shoes” of the Registrar, it must apply ss 24–25 directly when determining whether Marrambik’s name should be changed.
These provisions are central to the merits review task: VCAT must decide whether, on the evidence before it, the statutory criteria support granting the name change.
4. Relevance to Marrambik’s Case
These powers are directly relevant in several ways:
VCAT must re‑exercise the Registrar’s statutory discretion under ss 24–25 , not merely review the legality of the refusal.
VCAT must determine the correct or preferable outcome, considering Marrambik’s cultural identity, personal history, and reasons for seeking the name change.
VCAT may set aside the Registrar’s refusal and substitute a decision granting the name change if it concludes that the statutory criteria are satisfied.
Alternatively, VCAT may remit the matter with directions if further factual development is required.
Because the merits review jurisdiction is fully re‑determinative, VCAT is empowered to give Marrambik the outcome he seeks if the evidence supports it.
5. Summary
VCAT’s relevant powers include:
full re‑exercise of the Registrar’s functions (VCAT Act s 51(1)(a))

broad remedial authority to affirm, vary, substitute, or remit (s 51(2))
application of the substantive criteria in BDMR Act ss 24–25
making the correct or preferable decision (Drake No 1; Frugtniet)
These powers enable VCAT to reconsider the name‑change application on its merits and, if appropriate, substitute a decision granting Marrambik’s preferred name.
Evidence: Generally, the tribunal should be using the best and most current information available. (Furgtniet; Shi) Note in Shi, Kirby (broader view - assumption) and Keifel (narrow view) have different arguments
Use of the Most Recent Evidence
In conducting merits review, the Tribunal must base its decision on the best and most current evidence available . This principle is well‑established in the authorities.
In Frugtniet v ASIC , the Full Federal Court confirmed that a merits‑review tribunal is required to make the correct or preferable decision on the basis of the evidence before it at the time of review, not merely the material before the original decision‑maker.
In Shi v Migration Agents Registration Authority , the High Court affirmed that the Tribunal must consider the most up‑to‑date information , although the judges differed in their reasoning.
Kirby J adopted a broader view, emphasising that merits review assumes the Tribunal will consider all current circumstances.

Kiefel J took a narrower approach, focusing on the statutory text but still accepting that updated evidence may be relevant where the statute permits.
Applied to this case, VCAT must assess Marrambik’s name‑change application using the current facts , including any updated information about his identity, cultural background, or personal circumstances. The Tribunal is not confined to the material the Registrar had when refusing the application. This ensures that the Tribunal’s substituted decision reflects the present reality and achieves the correct or preferable outcome.
Government policy : Appropriate policy guides, but does not control, decision-making (Drake [No 2]/Green v Daniel)
An appropriate policy is one which guides but does not control decision-making, and which is informative of the standards and values which the minister usually applies. Drake [No 2]
The AAT is warranted in adopting a practice of applying lawful ministerial policy, unless there are cogent reasons otherwise, since consistency is not preferable to justice. Drake [No 2]
Inflexible application of policy: The application of the general rule to the plaintiff contained no suggestion of anything other than an inflexible rule, a rule which prevented her from being considered. (Green v Daniels)

VCAT s57 - similar S57 - the Tribunal in conducting the proceeding must apply the statement of policy - it is only in VCAT Only (suggested that the policy should be lawful)
Use of Government Policy
Government policy may guide, but cannot control, the Tribunal’s exercise of discretion.
In Drake v Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs (No 2) , the Tribunal held that policy is a legitimate and useful guide because it promotes consistency and reflects the standards ordinarily applied by the decision‑maker.
However, policy must not be applied inflexibly or mechanistically . The Tribunal must remain open to departing from policy where there are cogent reasons to do so (Drake (No 2);
The principle is that policy may inform , but cannot dictate , the merits review outcome.
This approach is reflected in VCAT Act s 57 , which provides that VCAT must apply a statement of government policy. Even then, the policy must be lawful , and VCAT must still exercise its own independent judgment.

In the context of the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act, any internal guidelines or departmental policies about name changes may be relevant to understanding how the Registrar typically exercises discretion under ss 24–25. However, VCAT must not treat such policies as binding. If Marrambik’s cultural identity, personal history, or other compelling circumstances justify departure from policy, VCAT is empowered—and required—to do so.

The jurisdiction conferred on the Federal Court by s 8(1) of the Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977 (Cth). Under ADJR Act, Court retains a discretion under section 16 of the AD(JR) Act whether or not to grant any remedy to the applicant.
Section 8(1) of the ADJR Act grants the Federal Court jurisdiction to review decisions made under Commonwealth enactments. The ADJR procedure is significantly different to the procedure under the Judiciary Act. The most significant differences are that the grounds for review are codified; the jurisdiction is not 'remedy-driven' and applicants are not required to establish jurisdictional error; and applicants have a statutory right to obtain reasons for the decision under review from the decision maker.

For a decision to be a reviewable decision under the ADJR Act, it will generally, but not always, entail a decision required by or authorised by a statute which is final or operative and determinative of the issue of fact falling for consideration.

Competing priorities
The majority limit judicial review to decisions which possess a quality of finality. As Mason CJ commented, to find otherwise would ‘lead to a fragmentation of the process of administrative decision-making and set at risk the efficiency of the administrative process’ (at 337).

A majority of the High Court (Chief Justice Mason, with Justices Brennan and Deane concurring on this point) held:
Conduct is ‘ procedural and not substantive in character’.
Conduct is defined to complement the meaning of decision. It points to action taken , rather than the decision made, for the purpose of making a reviewable decision.
Application: The finding that Bond was not a fit and proper person could not be attacked as 'conduct' as the challenge did not relate to the conduct of the proceedings engaged in before the making of a 'decision'. The finding is not action of a procedural nature.

Decision S2 A decision is 'a decision operating in law to determine a question affecting the rights of any person or to grant, deny, terminate, suspend or alter a privilege or licence and includes a refusal or failure to perform a exercise a power to make such a decision'. Note the requirement that the decision be 'operative' and have a quality of finality (in so far as it concerns a determination which effectively disposes of the issues between the parties). Although the ALA and the ADJR are clearly different statutes, operating in different jurisdictions, and it is not correct to assume the meaning of ‘decision’ in each of these statutes is interchangeable; it is important to note that following the decision in Australian Broadcasting Tribunal v Bond (1990) 170 CLR 321, the definition of 'decision' in the ALA includes decisions that determine a question affecting the rights of a person that are not necessarily immediately binding and enforceable: An authority in Victorian law to cite for this preposition is Masters v McCubbery [1996] 1 VR 635, 649 (per Winneke P).

Tribunal S2 'a person or body of persons (not being a court of law or a tribunal constituted or presided over by a Judge of the Supreme Court) who, in arriving at the decision in question, is or are by law required, whether by express direction or not, to act in a judicial manner to the extent of observing one or more of the rules of natural justice. '

Charter Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities Act 2006 (Vic)
The Charter imposes additional legal obligations on public authorities beyond those imposed by the traditional common law grounds of judicial review.
s 38(1) it is unlawful for a public authority to act in a way that is incompatible with a human right or, in making a decision, to fail to give proper consideration to a relevant human right. procedural limb of s 38(1) (failure to give proper consideration to rights)
substantive limb (acting incompatibly with rights)
Grounds : Charter claims can operate as additional grounds of unlawfulness
Piggy-backing: Section 39(1) of the Charter functions as a procedural gateway that permits Charter unlawfulness to be raised only where a court already has jurisdiction to grant relief. there is no direct access to the courts for breach of the Charter and There is no standalone cause of action. (Bare v IBAC)

Charter adds to grounds of JR, but not new avenue of JR (I.e. source of judicial authority) This doctrinal move preserves the structural integrity of Victorian public law. The Charter reshapes the content of legality — introducing proportionality, structured reasoning, and rights-based justification — but not the source of judicial authority.
Conclusion: Charter Section 39(1) does not confer jurisdiction or create a standalone cause of action. It permits Charter unlawfulness to be raised only if the applicant already has an independent basis for seeking relief — for example, a judicial review proceeding — even if that ground ultimately fails (Certain Children; Minogue).

A remedy consequent upon outcomes of the principal remedy.
Smethurst v Commissioner of Police(2020): example: Smethurst sought certiorari as an ancillary to an injunction. Because Smethurst sought an injunction, she was able to invoke the jurisdiction under s 75(v); and once this jurisdiction was invoked, certiorari was available as an ancillary remedy. Grounds of JR
= Is there a breach of / failure to comply with a precondition to or condition on the exercise of the relevant statutory power?
Purpose of JR: superior courts supervise the exercise of executive (statutory) powers to enforce the rule of law in Australia. The task that the courts is to determine the legality (and validity) of government action.
Grounds of review: These are statutory presumptions. They describe implicit i preconditions or conditions imposed on the exercise of statutory powers. Procedural fairness – hearing rule
Procedural fairness – bias
Error as to jurisdictional fact
Acting without legal authority
Invalid delegation
Failure to take into account a relevant consideration
Taking into account an irrelevant consideration
Inflexible application of policy
Unreasonableness
Legal consequences of jurisdictional error
= If a breach is established, what are the consequence of that breach?

Whereas the grounds of judicial review help us to interpret what the preconditions to and the conditions on the exercise of power are, other principles of statutory interpretation are relied on to determine what Parliament intended would be the consequence of breaching a precondition to or condition on the exercise of power.
The Courts will also exercise their own discretion when considering what remedy, if any, is appropriate. Moreover, some remedies (the writs) are limited to errors of law that invalidate government action (or more precisely rise to the level of a jurisdictional error ); whereas equitable remedies can be granted where the impugned administrative action involved an error of law that does not invalidate the action. Jurisdictional error = invalidity
Jurisdictional error vs merely unlawful

Jurisdiction refers to the scope of the authority that is conferred on a repository . In its application to judicial review of administrative action the taking of which is authorised by statute, it refers to the scope of the authority which a statute confers on a decision‑maker to make a decision of a kind to which the statute then attaches legal consequences . It encompasses in that application all of the preconditions which the legislature exits in order for the decision‑maker to embark on the decision‑making process. It also encompasses all of the conditions which the statute expressly or impliedly requires to be observed in or in relation to the decision‑making process in order for the decision‑maker to make a decision of that kind. A decision made within jurisdiction is a decision which sufficiently complies with those statutory preconditions and conditions to have “such force and effect as is given to it by the law pursuant to which it is made”. (at Hossain [23])
Jurisdictional error Why need this? When seeking the writs of prohibition, mandamus or certiorari at common law or in the s 75(v) and s39B, relief will only be given for jurisdictional error. Certiorari: also available on non jurisdictional error on the face of the record (Craig and Kirk)
It is also important when interpreting privative clauses as it is entrenched under constitution s75(v).

LPDT v Minister for Immigration, Citizenship, Migrant Services and Multicultural Affairs [2024] HCA 12 at [2], citing Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs v Bhardwaj (2002) 209 CLR 597 at [53]. Jurisdictional error refers to a breach of an express or implied condition of a statutory conferral of decision - making authority which results in a decision made in the purported exercise of that authority lacking the legal force attributed to exercise of that authority by statute. Though a decision affected by jurisdictional error is a decision in fact, it is “in law … no decision at all” and is in that sense “void”.

LPDT (2024) SM; [2024] HCA 12 at [3]: Because an express or implied condition of a statutory conferral of decision-making authority can take many different forms, and because breach can occur in many different circumstances, the categories of jurisdictional error are not closed . Jurisdictional error can result from breach by a third party of a condition of a statutory process preceding a decision, but more often results from breach by a statutory decision-maker of a condition of the making of a decision. Jurisdictional error on the part of a statutory decision-maker in making a decision can include : misunderstanding the applicable law; asking the wrong question; exceeding the bounds of reasonableness; identifying a wrong issues; ignoring relevant material; relying on irrelevant material; in some cases, making an erroneous finding or reaching a mistaken conclusion; or failing to observe some applicable requirement of procedural fairness.
LPDT (2024) SM; [2024] HCA 12 at [3]: The nature of the error has to be worked out in each case concerning a specific decision under a specific statute.

LPDT Two questions on jurisdictional error Has an error has occurred (that is, has been a breach of an express or implied condition of the statutory conferral of decision-making authority) This a question of law - determined through an exercise in statutory construction to determine the the nature, scope and purpose of the power.
Is the error jurisdictional (or has the error made resulted in the decision made lacking legal force) - threshold of materiality This is a question of fact - could that make a difference?
[4]-[6] In some cases, where an error is established, the error will be jurisdictional irrespective of any effect that the error might or might not have had on the decision that was in fact made. E.g. apprehended or actual bias/unreasonableness.
[7] threshold of materiality : In most cases, however, an error will only be jurisdictional if the error was material to the decision that was made in fact, in the sense that there is a r ealistic possibility that the decision that was made in fact could have been different if the error had not occurred. That is because a statute which contains an express or implied condition to be observed in a decision-making process is ordinarily to be interpreted as incorporating such a “threshold of materiality” in the event of non-compliance.

[10] The inquiry with respect to each question is backward-looking . Both questions are to be answered by reference to the decision that was made and, depending on the nature of the error, how that decision was made.
Key cases Step 1: What did Parliament intend would be the consequence of a breach? Project Blue Sky v Australian Broadcasting Authority (CB418) This case shows how a court will interpret a statute to ascertain the intention of Parliament concerning the consequences of a breach of the limits on statutory power. You should apply the principles of PBS at “Stage 1”.
In this case it was held that an act in breach of a statutory requirement was held to be “unlawful”, but not “invalid”.
‘Filter out’ the directory legal requirements (materiality)
Consideration : Consider the language of the statute, its subject matter, and objects, and the justice and convenience (to the public or individual) of holding that material breach of the duty invalidates

Only a serious error, which undermines the statutory scheme (eg Bhardwaj), or perhaps interferes with individual (Wei) or third-party rights (PBS), will be treated as ‘mandatory’ and, therefore, as having invalidated the decision; whereas a relatively immaterial error will not invalidate the decision, because it is unlikely “that the legislature intended that a decision be rendered invalid by an immaterial error”. (Hossain at para 67, per Edelman J).
Presumptions: Subject to contrary indications in the statute, breach of common law grounds are presumed to lead to invalidity.
Step 2: Consequence of a finding of Jurisdictional error = no decision at all Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs v Bhardwaj (CB412) Facts
Mr Bhardwaj was unable to attend his student visa merits review hearing because he was ill and sent a fax to the Immigration Review Tribunal (IRT) notifying them of this.
The Tribunal did not receive the fax before it went ahead with the hearing in Mr Bhardwaj’s absence.
After the hearing, the Tribunal made an adverse decision against Mr Bhardwaj based solely on the documents before it (the “September decision”).
Mr Bhardwaj was informed of this adverse decision and, instead of seeking judicial review, contacted the Tribunal to explain that he had sent the fax.

Acknowledging that it had made an error, the Tribunal decided to re-list the matter for hearing, on the basis that it had not properly heard the case and had denied Mr Bhardwaj procedural fairness.
The Tribunal then reheard the case and subsequently made a decision in Mr Bhardwaj’s favour (the “October decision”).
The Minister sought judicial review of the Tribunal’s October decision in Mr Bhardwaj’s favour.
The Minister argued that once the Tribunal made the September decision, it had exhausted (“spent”) its jurisdiction, meaning it lacked power to later rehear the case and make the October decision.
This argument was rejected at first instance and again by the Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia (FCAFC).
Following those losses, the Minister appealed to the High Court of Australia (HCA).
Statutory background complication noted in the image:
By the time of the Tribunal’s second hearing (leading to the October decision), Mr Bhardwaj was out of time to apply to the FCA for judicial review.
This was because strict 28‑day time limits applied to judicial review applications.
Additionally, the FCA’s review jurisdiction was limited to specific grounds listed in Part 8 of the Migration Act, which did not include denial of procedural fairness.
The slide notes that this limitation becomes significant when considering Plaintiff S157/2002 (2003) later on.
Ratio:

If a purported decision is affected by jurisdictional error, it is regarded as no decision at all. It is invalid but not necessarily a nullity (it remains a decision in fact). In this case, it was held that a jurisdictionally flawed decision was of no legal effect (for the purposes of law “no decision at all”) and the tribunal could proceed as if it had not made the decision. The judges came to this conclusion by applying the same principles of statutory interpretation as those applied in Project Blue Sky – the court looked to what Parliament intended. The majority found the errors of law committed by the tribunal were intended to invalidate its initial decision. This case can be applied to state the effect of jurisdictional error.
Gaudron and Gummow JJ
[51] A decision that involves jurisdictional error is a decision that lacks legal foundation and is properly regarded, in law, as no decision at all.
Dissenting - Kirby (utility / pragmatisim)
It is for the act to guide this. Fact is that the act did not give the power for tribunal to change decision.
It is required for clarity and certainty, especially for migration decisions.
Non jurisdictional error “Error on the face of the record” is not a type of jurisdictional error. The error can be any error of law.

BUT the Victorian Court of Appeal has held that it would “ordinarily be difficult to justify the quashing of administrative decision on the basis that the reasons given for it contain an incidental error of law which could not sensibly be said to affect the decision‑maker’s conclusion”.
Don’t forget: where relief is sought on the ground of error of law on the face of the record, the court is only entitled to review the documents that comprise the “record” of the inferior court or tribunal: Craig at 176. (the documentation that initiates the proceedings and thereby grounds the jurisdiction of the tribunal, the pleadings (if any) and the actual order or ruling; it will not include the transcript, the exhibits or the reasons for decision unless they are incorporated by reference).

Meaning that those exercising power must do so in a procedurally fair manner
Why Natural justice promotes better decision-making by ensuring that relevant information is before the decision-maker.
Rule of law - (R(Osborn) v Parole Board) Lord Reed
Projecting an air of impartiality
Public confidence is fostered in the decision-making process and the correctness of decisions.
Fosters participation in government, by informing people of a decision that may affect them and giving them an opportunity to put a view
Fair process effect - psychological - people who see that a hearing or process is fair are more likely to accept the integrity both of particular decisions and of the institutions in which decisions are made.
Dignitarian: treating people affected by official power with respect.
Practical problems: This must be balanced against the pragmatic demands of administrative decision-making.
Floodgates - too much of something can bring governments and the courts to a halt.
Takeaway Fairness is not an abstract concept. It is essentially practical. The concern of the law is to avoid practical injustice.

Details Two traditional elements of fairness Prior hearing rule: decision-maker must give the person prior notice that a decision may be made, provide them with the information (particularly prejudicial information) on which the decision may be based and advise them of the person’s right to make a submission in reply.
The hearing rule entitles a person whose interests are liable to be affected to be given notice of relevant matters and a reasonable opportunity to present his or her case. This includes: a duty of disclosure whereby adverse information that is ‘credible, relevant and significant’ (Kioa) to the decision to be made should be disclosed in an opportunity given to deal with it; or,
giving parties the opportunity to make submissions where a tribunal proposes to decide a case on a different basis or by way of a different procedure than initially expected or agreed (WZARH).
Bias rule A decision-maker must be free of any reasonable apprehension of bias or preconception arising from circumstances such as the decision-maker’s financial or personal interests, prior expression of views, or previous role in the decision to be made.
Other considerations Duty to hear != duty to consider - treated separately.
Duty to inquiry: in limited circumstances only - as an aspect of Wednesbury unreasonableness.
Duty to consult

Overall Test: Q1: implication: is there an implied duty to accord natural justice? Whether a duty to be fair should be implied in the exercise of statutory powers (strong presumption)
Q2: exclusion: has the legislature evinced an intention to exclude the obligation to observe one or other requirements of natural justice?
Q3: content What procedural obligations (e.g. hearing/documentation should be given) should be required as part of the exercise of a power Badari
Hearing rule 1. Threshold: Does the hearing rule apply to the specific statutory power under consideration? Process of statutory construction (Badari; Brennan J, Kioa; Saeed; WZARH)
Consider whether there is a clear parliamentary intention to displace procedural fairness (Badari; Saeed) - presumption see below in Badari.
2. Content: If it does apply, what does the hearing rule require? Flexible obligation, appropriate and adapted to the circumstances (Badari; Kioa; WZARH) Chance to respond (Kioa)

Relevant/irrelevant considerations Statutory construction The subject matter, scope and purpose of the act may either expressly state, or necessarily imply, that a certain consideration must (or must not) be taken into account (Mason J, Peko-Wallsend)
Mandatory relevant considerations Error can only be established if a decision-maker fails to account for a consideration which they are bound to take into account (Mason J, Peko-Wallsend)

Irrelevant consideration Error can only be established if a decision-maker accounts for a consideration which they are prohibited from taking into account.

Proper consideration of the charter (In Victoria Only) Section 38(1) “give proper consideration to a relevant human right” has a procedural and a substantive limb (Tate JA, Bare; Thompson). Step 2: seriously turn their mind to the possible impact of the decision on the a person’s human rights and the implications for the affected person;

Best to use the analogy to explain this (using Bare - police assult on Bare African person, look at the complaint and look at the long term impact)
Step 3: identify the countervailing interest or obligations (such as policy and procedures - bowen prison's drug situation is quite bad); and
Step 4: balance completing private and public interests as part of the exercise of justification.

Proper consideration is a higher standard than that generally applicable at common law to taking into account relevant consideration (Kyrou, McLeish and Niall JJA, Thompson). So this imposes additional consideration to decision-maker.

the test for illogicality or irrationality must be to ask whether logical or rational or reasonable minds might adopt different reasoning or might differ in any decision or finding to be made on evidence upon which the decision is based. If probative evidence can give rise to different processes of reasoning and if logical or rational or reasonable minds might differ in respect of the conclusions to be drawn from that evidence, a decision cannot be said by a reviewing court to be illogical or irrational or unreasonable, simply because one conclusion has been preferred to another possible conclusion.
Unreasonableness Legal unreasonableness can be a conclusion reached by a supervising court after the identification of an underlining jurisdictional error in the decision-making process. Or it can be a conclusion reached without necessarily identifying another jurisdictional error (Li at [27]-[28], [72]). The legal standard of reasonableness and the indicia of legal unreasonableness will need to b found in the scope, subject and purpose of the particular statutory provision in issue in any given case (Li [67])
Originally sourced in Wednesbury unreasonableness: a decision “so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could have ever come to it”
Since Li, legal unreasonableness in Australia is no longer limited to Wednesbury unreasonableness

Legislature is taken to intend that a discretionary power conferred by a statute will be exercised reasonably (Quin; Li; SZVFW)

In the exercise of a discretion, there is generally an areas of decisional freedom within which reasonable minds mayh reach different conclusions. (Li, French CJ [28], Hayne, Kiefel and Bell JJ [66], Gageler J [105])

Purposive Ask whether the delegated legislation is a reasonable means of attaining the ends of the regulation or rule making power.
Non-purposive Whether there is a real and substantial connection between the delegated legislation and the subject matter of the grant of power.

R v Toohey; Ex parte Northern Land Council (1981) 151 CLR 170 [11.2.12C] Facts: The Northern Land Council had lodged the Kenbi Land Claim under the Aboriginal Land Rights (Northern Territory) Act 1976 (Cth), over land on the Cox Peninsula near Darwin. Under that Act, the Aboriginal Land Commissioner (Toohey J) could only hear claims over "unalienated Crown land," a term defined to exclude land within a town.
Acting under the Planning Act 1979 (NT), the Administrator of the Northern Territory made regulations which purported to treat as a town an area of some 4,350 square kilometres around Darwin — an enormous expansion given that Darwin itself occupied an area of about 142 square kilometres. This area included most of the land subject to the Kenbi Land Claim, and the regulation, if valid, would have prevented the Commissioner from hearing the claim.
When the Council tried to argue before the Commissioner that the regulation had been made for the improper purpose of defeating the claim, and sought discovery of documents, Toohey J felt bound by precedent to rule that the motives of the Administrator in making the regulation could not be called into question. The Council then applied to the High Court for certiorari, mandamus and prohibition.

Issue: Whether the regulation-making power could be challenged on the ground that it was exercised for an unauthorised (improper) purpose — and, in particular, whether the exercise of that power was insulated from review because the Administrator was, or was treated as, a representative of the Crown.
Toohey J: an inquiry could not be held into the motives of the Administrator in making the regulation.
Ratio: Gibbs J The nature and extent of the power must be inferred from a construction of the act read as a whole.
A statutory power may be exercised only for the purposes for which it is conferred.
No inquiry may be made into the motives of the legislature in enacting a law, but where a statute confers powers upon an officer or a statutory body and either by express provision or by reason of the general character of the status it appears that the powers were intended to be exercised only for a particular purpose , then the exercise of the power not for such purpose but for some ulterior object will be invalid.
Crown: No convincing reason can be suggested for limiting the ordinary power of the courts to inquire whether there has been a proper exercise of a statutory power by giving to the Crown a special immunity from review.
Onus: lies on those who make that assertion.

Reasoning The planning act are to be exercised only for planning purpose and it is not intended by the Act to be conferred for the purpose of defecting the traditional land claims of aboriginals.
Unless the Administrator enjoys some privilege that enables him to transcend and disregard the limitation which the stature on its proper construction imposes.
Vanstone v Clark [2005] 147 FCR 299 (Weinberg J's judgment) This case concerns delegated legislation under the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Commission Act 1989 (Cth) (ATSIC Act). The Act allowed the Minister to suspend a Commissioner for ‘misbehaviour’ (s 40), and to define ‘misbehaviour’ by legislative determination (s 4A).
Geoff Clark, Chairperson of ATSIC, was convicted of obstructing police. The offence carried a possible penalty of imprisonment, but he was only fined. The Minister relied on the ATSIC (Misbehaviour) Determination 2002, which provided that conviction of an offence punishable by imprisonment constituted ‘misbehaviour’. Based on this, Clark was suspended from office.
Issue: Whether the Determination defining ‘misbehaviour’ was within the power conferred by the ATSIC Act, and whether the Minister lawfully suspended Clark under it.
Held: The Full Federal Court held that the cl5(1)(k) was invalid and could not support the minister’s decision.

Minister’s decision in reliance on s40 was invalid for failing to have regard to a relevant consideration.
Ratio: Delegated legislation, made pursuant to a purposive empowering provision, will be struck down if it fails the test of reasonable proportionality.
In some of the authorities, this principle is referred to in terms of arbitrariness, capriciousness or unreasonableness… they can be regarded as indicia of a failure to satisfy that test.
The question is whether the delegated legislation is capable of being reasonably considered to be appropriate and adapted to achieve the prescribed purpose.
Courts must give full right to the fact that by-laws are made by local government bodies, whose members are democratically elected and directly accountable to their constituents. Unreasonable obligation on a person will fall within the test of invalidity.
By-law will not change the analysis.
This is a stringent test. It will only be satisfied in an extreme case.
Delegated legislation may be invalid for any one of a number of reasons: Because it purports to deal with a subject not within the scope of the power provided in the empowering act
It may deal with such a subject, but in a manner that exceeds the prescribed limits within which the legislation must fall
Because it is inconsistent with, or repugnant to, the empowering act, or some other act

Because the power to make the delegated legislation has been exercised not for the purpose set out in the empowering Act, but for some other impressible purpose. --> Toohey
Because its effect is so unreasonable that it cannot be regarded as falling within the contemplation of the legislature in enacting the empowering act, or is not reasonably proportionate to the empowering provisions of that act.
Reasoning + There is no reason in principle why delegated legislation cannot operate to extend the meaning of a term in the act
- Parliament could not have intended that commissioner be removed for conduct that most sensible people would regard as relatively harmless.
- One must be cautious about testing the validity of delegated legislation by considering extreme examples of its possible misuse.
Black CJ: Clause 5(1)(k) did not refer to the conduct, but to the consequence of that conduct. My view is that this argument is weak.
Analysis steps for regulation
Step 1: Regulation invalid?
Step 2: How does that affect the decision? Link is consideraiton of irrelevant consideration (maybe legally unreasonable)
Legal consequences of jurisdictional error
Remedies
Conclusion
To read

headingPath: READING ACTIVITY: Aronson and McMillan articles
chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › READING ACTIVITY: Aronson and McMillan articles
chunks.text:
On the following pages you will find two Reading Activities:
Mark Aronson, ‘ Is the ADJR Act hampering the development of Australian Administrative Law?
(2004) 15 Public Law Review 202, pages 202-214 only .
John McMillan, ‘ Restoring the ADJR Act in federal judicial reviewLinks to an external site. ’ (2013) 72 AIAL Forum 12: Links to an external site. pages 12-19 only.
We have uploaded the articles in full but you are only required to read the specified pages. You may wish to read these articles in their entirety in SWOTVAC as you revise for the essay component of the final exam.
Essay - ADJR
Mark Aronson, ‘Is the ADJR Act hampering the development of Australian Administrative Law?” (2004) 15 Public Law Review 202, pages 202-214 only.

chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › 1. SUMMARY OF THE ARTICLE
chunks.text:
Core thesis: Mark Aronson argues that while the ADJR Act has been “overwhelmingly beneficial”, it now suffers from structural limitations that restrict its usefulness and may distort the development of Australian administrative law. He examines three major criticisms:
Jurisdictional shortcomings
Over‑particularised grounds of review
The claim that the Act has “retarded” the common law’s development

chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › 1. SUMMARY OF THE ARTICLE › A. Jurisdictional Shortcomings
chunks.text: Aronson identifies several limits in the ADJR Act’s jurisdiction:

chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › 1. SUMMARY OF THE ARTICLE › A. Jurisdictional Shortcomings › 1. “Administrative” decision requirement
chunks.text:
The Act only applies to decisions “of an administrative character”. Aronson argues this is conceptually unclear and historically accidental. Courts now treat “administrative” as meaning “neither judicial nor legislative”. He suggests the Act should extend to subordinate legislation, noting that the current exclusion forces applicants into awkward procedural routes.
The article notes that ADJR’s limitation “denies direct coverage of subordinate legislative acts”.

chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › 1. SUMMARY OF THE ARTICLE › A. Jurisdictional Shortcomings › 2. Exclusion of the Governor‑General
chunks.text: The Act expressly excludes the Governor‑General, despite vice‑regal immunity having disappeared at common law. Aronson calls this an “anomaly”.

chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › 1. SUMMARY OF THE ARTICLE › A. Jurisdictional Shortcomings › 3. “Final and operative” decision requirement
chunks.text: Following Bond, ADJR review is limited to final decisions. Aronson argues this is unnecessary and counterproductive, especially given the High Court’s broader s 75(v) jurisdiction.

chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › 1. SUMMARY OF THE ARTICLE › A. Jurisdictional Shortcomings › 4. “Under an enactment” requirement
chunks.text:
This is the most significant criticism. Aronson argues that ADJR should not be limited to statutory powers. Common law judicial review already extends to non‑statutory executive power , including prerogative powers and non‑statutory schemes.
He notes that “it is difficult to justify ADJR’s continuing restriction to decisions under enactments”.
He recommends replacing the test with one focused on public power , aligning with English developments such as Datafin.

headingPath: B. Grounds of Review: Particularisation and Lack of General Principles
chunks.locationLabel: NOTES Review › 1. SUMMARY OF THE ARTICLE › B. Grounds of Review: Particularisation and Lack of General Principles
chunks.text:
Aronson acknowledges that ADJR’s grounds are highly particularised—17 in total. Critics argue this prevents the emergence of overarching principles.
Kirby J has suggested that the Act’s codification has “retarded” the development of the common law, which in England has evolved more freely.
Aronson rejects this, arguing:
ADJR includes catch‑all grounds (“otherwise contrary to law”) that allow flexibility.
The common law has continued to innovate (e.g., Yusuf, S20).
The real issue is the lack of articulated general principles , not the Act itself.
