Seizure (s 50 warrant). The seizure of the four breeding cats depends on a s 50 warrant, which required reasonable grounds to suspect the cats were "the subject of an offence" against the Act. The only candidate offence is breeding without approval (s 32); but by reg 15 Ms Anand's approval continued in force until her renewal application was lawfully determined, and if the refusal and cancellation are invalid (so that there has been no lawful determination, and no valid cancellation) she remained an approved breeder , so no offence was committed and there was no jurisdictional foundation for the warrant. This argument must, however, confront a temporal objection . Section 50 conditions the warrant on the justice's satisfaction of "reasonable grounds for suspecting" an offence, assessed on the material before the justice at the time of issue — and on 22 May the cancellation had not been set aside and appeared operative. The Council will say a suspicion can be reasonable even if its factual premise is later falsified. The answer lies in Bhardwaj : a decision affected by jurisdictional error is "in law, no decision at all", so the cancellation never had legal effect and Ms Anand was at all times an approved breeder by force of reg 15 — there was never an offence to suspect. But Bhardwaj nullity is not absolute (a purported decision may have practical and even some legal consequences until set aside), and the safer pleading is twofold: first, that the suspicion was not objectively reasonable even on the material presented (the warrant application necessarily rested wholly on the Council's own assertion of cancellation, advanced by the same conflicted officer); and secondly, that whatever the warrant's validity at issue, no continued impoundment can survive once the cancellation is quashed — so the order for the return of the cats does not depend on quashing the warrant at all. That distinction matters for the respondent set: the issuing justice should be named as a respondent to the certiorari claim but would be expected to submit save as to costs ( R v Australian Broadcasting Tribunal; Ex parte Hardiman (1980) 144 CLR 13 ), while the return of the four cats is sought against the Council as the facility operator holding them. This is a discrete and important head of relief, distinct from the quia timet protection of Maximus.

Conclusion
The cancellation should be separately quashed (and is, if anything, more vulnerable than the refusal); the s 50 warrant and seizure are challengeable, and relief should include an order for the return of all four seized cats, not merely the protection of Maximus.
4.15 Grounds Considered and Not Separately Pursued
For completeness, three further matters were considered and are not separately pursued. Bad faith : although the sister's competing cattery, the PawPrint post and the "would do as the Councillor required" note could support a bad-faith argument, bad faith carries a very high threshold and is rarely necessary where improper purpose (4.2) and bias (4.7) are available; it is not separately run. Legitimate expectation : not pursued — the concept provides no basis for determining whether, or with what content, procedural fairness is owed ( Kiefel, Bell and Keane JJ, WZARH ; see also Re Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs; Ex parte Lam (2003) 214 CLR 1 ), and Ms Anand's procedural entitlements are fully vindicated at 4.6. Failure to make obvious inquiries : the failure to read Dr Lim's assessment, or to check the anonymous allegations, could support a narrow failure-to-inquire argument; it is best run as reinforcing unreasonableness (4.10) rather than as a separate ground.
5. The Strongest Ground — Comparative Assessment

Before turning to breach and relief, the grounds should be ranked: the choice of lead ground determines what must be proved (in particular, whether materiality must be shown at all), who the necessary respondents are, and what relief follows. The grounds are not of equal strength. Assessing them by the certainty of the breach, resistance to counter-argument, whether they void the whole decision, the materiality burden, and the value of the resulting relief:
Unauthorised decision-maker / invalid delegation (4.5) is, with bias, the strongest. If Mr Boyle lacked authority under s 40, the decision is a nullity regardless of its merits, and no materiality need be shown. The only real obstacle is the s 42(1) "employees" counter-argument, which is answerable (s 40(1) is the sole, specific delegation power, confined to the CEO, and there is no written instrument to Mr Boyle).
Apprehended bias (4.7) is equally powerful: the facts (a competing-cattery sibling; investigator-and-decider; a prior public condemnation) make the fair-minded-observer test easy to satisfy, and bias is jurisdictional irrespective of materiality (LPDT). It voids the entire decision.

Acting without authority / error of law (4.1, 4.1a) and the related no-evidence ground (4.8a) are clean and largely incontestable on the face of the reasons (reasons 4–5 outside s 34(2); the non-existent "conviction"; the ultra vires reg 12). The Council will argue that, because s 34(2) requires only "one or more" grounds, a single valid and independently sufficient ground (reason 1) can support the refusal notwithstanding the invalid surplus; that reflects the orthodox severance principle, but it fails here because reason 1 ("fit and proper") is itself vitiated (no evidence, bias, ignored evidence), so no valid ground survives.
Failure to consider relevant considerations (4.3) and reliance on irrelevant considerations (4.4) are strong and mutually reinforcing, and feed unreasonableness (4.10) .
Ultra vires delegated legislation (4.12) is strong as to reg 12 and decisively removes reason 3.
Improper purpose (4.2) is unusually well-evidenced but remains fact-sensitive; subjective jurisdictional fact (4.9) faces the high SZMDS threshold — though, as argued at 4.9, this case satisfies even the stricter outcome-focused formulation — and is best pleaded alongside the no-evidence and consideration grounds; the Charter (4.13) adds an additional badge of unlawfulness but must piggy-back and does not itself establish jurisdictional error.

The ground chosen for the full consequences-of-breach analysis is the unauthorised-decision-maker ground (4.5). It is chosen because the breach is established on undisputed documentary facts (there is simply no instrument of delegation to Mr Boyle), it admits of no evaluative reply, and it voids the decision wholesale; apprehended bias (4.7) is maintained as the alternative lead. The two-stage analysis at section 6 is undertaken for that ground, with the others as alternatives.

Is any one ground sufficient to obtain the relief sought? Yes. Either the unauthorised-decision-maker ground or the apprehended-bias ground, if accepted, is independently sufficient to quash the entire decision (and neither requires proof that the outcome would have differed). Pleading order matters : the delegation ground should be put first, because if Mr Boyle was never the repository of the power the Court need not enter the contested factual territory of bias and purpose at all — though bias must be maintained in the alternative, since if the delegation argument fails (s 42 "employees") the bias case becomes the lead ground; and conversely, if delegation succeeds, the bias findings would attach to a nullity and the Court may decline to decide them. The first filing should be the interlocutory injunction application for Maximus , supported by the usual undertaking as to damages, with the originating motion to follow within the Order 56 period. The remaining grounds provide robust, mutually reinforcing alternatives and support the consequential relief (the injunction protecting Maximus, the return of the four seized cats, and the declarations). The cancellation (s 35) is, if anything, more vulnerable than the refusal, and the s 50 seizure falls with the cancellation (4.14).
6. Breach and Consequences of Breach
Issue

Whether the established errors are jurisdictional, so that the decision is invalid.
Rule (ratio)

Jurisdictional error is "a breach of an express or implied condition of a statutory conferral of decision-making authority which results in a decision made in the purported exercise of that authority lacking the legal force attributed to exercise of that authority by statute" ( LPDT ). Consistently with the approach required for the exam, the full two-stage analysis is undertaken for the chosen lead ground — the unauthorised decision-maker (4.5) — with the remaining grounds as alternatives. Step 1 (Project Blue Sky Inc v Australian Broadcasting Authority (1998) 194 CLR 355): whether it was a purpose of the legislation that an act done in breach of the relevant condition should be invalid. Step 2 (materiality, LPDT v Minister for Immigration, Citizenship and Multicultural Affairs (2024) 280 CLR 321): in most cases an error is jurisdictional only if material, in the sense that there is a realistic possibility the decision could have been different had the error not occurred — a backward-looking inquiry, answered by reference to the decision that was in fact made and how it was made ( LPDT ) (a low threshold); a threshold of materiality is ordinarily to be implied as a matter of statutory construction, it being unlikely that Parliament intended a decision to be invalidated by an immaterial error ( Hossain v Minister for Immigration and Border Protection (2018) 264 CLR 123 ). The contrary view — that materiality should not be a criterion of jurisdictional error at all, because Parliament cannot be taken to intend that decision-makers need comply with the law only where non-compliance would change the result ( Nettle and Gordon JJ, Minister for Immigration and Border Protection v SZMTA (2019) 264 CLR 421 ) — has not prevailed, and LPDT now states the orthodox position. Errors of certain kinds — notably apprehended or actual bias and legal unreasonableness , which deprive the decision of a characteristic the statute requires — do not call for a separate materiality inquiry ( LPDT at [16]–[17] ). A decision affected by jurisdictional error is "in law, no decision at all" ( Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs v Bhardwaj (2002) 209 CLR 597 ).
Application

Step 1 (lead ground): Section 40 is not a provision that merely regulates the exercise of functions already conferred (the feature that saved the provision in Project Blue Sky ); it is an essential precondition to the existence of authority in anyone other than the Council itself — Parliament identified, exhaustively and formally (writing; absolute majority; a register), the only person to whom the power could be devolved. A purported exercise by anyone else is precisely what the provision exists to prevent, and Parliament must be taken to have intended it to be invalid. The same is true, in the alternative, of s 34(2)'s exhaustive grounds, the implied obligation of procedural fairness, and the rule against bias: each is a condition on the existence of the authority, not a direction as to its manner of exercise, for a decision affecting livelihood, reputation and property. Step 2 (lead ground): the unauthorised-decision-maker ground requires no materiality inquiry — if Mr Boyle lacked power, there is no "decision" whose hypothetical alternative content could be assessed; the same is true of bias and legal unreasonableness (no separate materiality inquiry: LPDT). For the consideration and procedural-fairness grounds, materiality is plainly satisfied: had the Council read Dr Lim's assessment and put the false allegations to Ms Anand, there is a realistic possibility (indeed a likelihood) the decision would have been different. The errors are therefore material where materiality is required.

Conclusion
The decision is affected by jurisdictional error and is, in law, no decision at all ( Bhardwaj ); it is void and liable to be quashed.
7. Remedies (Part II) — Likely Availability
Given the strength of the grounds, the remedies sought are likely to be granted:
Certiorari to quash the 15 May 2026 decision (the decision being void: Bhardwaj).
Mandamus to compel a lawful redetermination of the renewal application by an authorised, unbiased decision-maker on the relevant considerations. Mandamus issues for actual or constructive failure to perform a public duty — and an unauthorised decision-maker means the Council has constructively failed to determine the application at all. It will compel determination according to law, not a grant: the residual "may" in s 34(2) means even a lawfully constituted decision-maker retains a discretion. But the practical position on redetermination is strong: none of the s 34(2)(a)–(f) grounds is capable of being lawfully made out on the true facts, and where no ground is satisfied the "only if" precondition for any refusal is absent — refusal would simply be unavailable, and renewal must follow. The discretion preserved by "may" is a discretion not to refuse despite satisfaction of a ground; it is not a freestanding discretion to refuse.

A prohibitory (interlocutory and final) injunction to restrain the destruction of Maximus. Given the irreversible harm (destruction of a living animal that, on the facts, is not "dangerous" within s 31(2)), the balance of convenience strongly favours urgent interlocutory relief; Ms Anand has the necessary legal/proprietary interest ( Bateman's Bay ).
An order for the return of the four seized cats and relief against the s 50 warrant: if the cancellation is invalid Ms Anand remained an approved breeder, so the cats were not "the subject of an offence" and there was no foundation for the warrant (4.14).
Declarations that the decision is invalid and that regulation 12 is ultra vires ( Enfield : an actual controversy exists).
The Court always has the discretion to refuse relief ( Enfield ). The presumption is that the Court will grant the remedies that are available and appropriate ( Enfield ).
8. Overall Conclusion (Question 1)

Ms Anand has strong prospects of success . The decision of 15 May 2026 is affected by multiple jurisdictional errors, of which the unauthorised-decision-maker and apprehended-bias grounds are each independently decisive. The application should be brought in the Supreme Court of Victoria under Order 56 (within the 60-day period), seeking certiorari, mandamus, a prohibitory injunction to protect Maximus, and declarations (including that reg 12 is invalid). Friends of Felines Inc has only an arguable standing, so Ms Anand should be the principal applicant. The Charter provides an additional ground (failure to give proper consideration to her rights to expression, property and privacy/reputation), to be run with the established grounds under s 39.
QUESTION 2 — Effect of a Privative Clause (hypothetical s 57)
Issue
How the analysis would differ if the Act contained s 57, providing that a decision "is final and conclusive, and must not be challenged, appealed against, reviewed, quashed or called into question in any court, and is not subject to prohibition, mandamus, injunction, declaration or certiorari, on any ground."
Rule (ratio)

a State privative clause cannot oust the Supreme Court of Victoria's supervisory jurisdiction to review for jurisdictional error : that jurisdiction is a defining characteristic of a State Supreme Court entrenched by Chapter III of the Constitution and cannot be removed by State legislation ( Kirk v Industrial Court (NSW) (2010) 239 CLR 531 ; cf. Plaintiff S157/2002 v Commonwealth (2003) 211 CLR 476 , s 75(v), at the federal level; and Graham v Minister for Immigration and Border Protection (2017) 263 CLR 1 : Parliament cannot deny a court the ability to enforce the legislated limits of an officer's power). The clause is read down so as not to apply to decisions affected by jurisdictional error. By contrast, a sufficiently clear clause may validly exclude review for non-jurisdictional error of law on the face of the record ( Kirk; Craig v South Australia ). A privative clause is also relevant to Project Blue Sky Step 1 , as a statement of Parliament's intention about the consequences of non-compliance.
Application

Section 57, however widely drawn, cannot prevent the Supreme Court of Victoria from reviewing the Council's decision for jurisdictional error . Critically, the grounds identified in Question 1 are, in the main, jurisdictional : an unauthorised decision-maker (s 40), apprehended bias, denial of procedural fairness, acting without authority/excess of power, failure to consider mandatory considerations, and legal unreasonableness are all classic jurisdictional errors. As a threshold point, the decision arguably fails even the first Hickman proviso: a decision made by an unauthorised officer, under dictation, infected by apprehended bias and substantially actuated by the purpose of punishing criticism is not a "bona fide attempt" to exercise the s 34 power, so s 57's protection is never engaged. Beyond that, the construction technique is textual, not merely doctrinal: s 57 protects "a decision of a local government under this Act ". A purported decision affected by jurisdictional error is not a decision "under this Act" at all — it is made outside the authority the Act confers — so it falls outside the clause's own description of what it protects ( Plaintiff S157; Kirk ); such a decision is "in law, no decision at all" ( Bhardwaj ). The clause and the grounds of review are reconciled, not collided. Two further textual points sharpen the position: s 57 speaks only of decisions of a local government , so it does not touch the challenge to reg 12 , which is an instrument of the Governor — the declaration of the regulation's invalidity proceeds wholly unaffected; and the warrant is a decision of a justice , not of a local government, so the certiorari claim against the warrant is likewise untouched. Section 57 therefore does not save the decision against the principal grounds, and certiorari, mandamus, prohibition, injunction and declaration remain available in the Court's inherent jurisdiction. There is also a distinctively Victorian obstacle the Council must overcome before Kirk is even reached: s 85(5) of the Constitution Act 1975 (Vic) provides that a provision repealing, altering or varying the Supreme Court's jurisdiction is of no effect unless the Act states that intention expressly and the prescribed parliamentary statements are made. Nothing suggests s 57 was enacted in compliance with that manner-and-form requirement, so it may be wholly inoperative as a matter of Victorian constitutional law without any need to read it down.

The clause would have two narrower effects. First , to the extent any error is characterised as a non-jurisdictional error of law , review survives only if the error appears on the face of the record — and here the applicant is doubly constrained. At common law the record does not include the reasons unless incorporated ( Craig ); the statutory cure, ALA s 10 (deeming reasons part of the record), is unavailable because the ALA's 30-day period has expired — and s 57 would in any event present a separate question whether the ALA route survives a privative clause at all. The s 37 notice arguably is incorporated in the record as the instrument recording the decision, but the prudent course is the one already taken: frame every error-of-law argument as jurisdictional (misconstruction of the s 34(2)(d) and (e) preconditions goes to the limits of the power: Craig; Kirk ), so that nothing turns on the record. Second , at Project Blue Sky Step 1 , the Council would invoke s 57 as evidence that Parliament did not intend non-compliance to invalidate; but Kirk confines that argument to non-jurisdictional matters, and cannot convert a jurisdictional error into a permissible one. The Charter analysis is unaffected (s 57 does not touch the Court's supervisory jurisdiction, and Charter unlawfulness is in any event raised via s 39 alongside the surviving grounds).
Conclusion

Section 57 would not materially change the advice. The Supreme Court of Victoria's supervisory jurisdiction over jurisdictional error cannot be ousted ( Kirk ; s 85 Constitution Act 1975 (Vic)), and the principal grounds are jurisdictional. At most, s 57 would bar review of any purely non-jurisdictional error of law on the record — which is a reason to plead the error-of-law points as jurisdictional. The remedies (including the injunction protecting Maximus and the declaration of reg 12's invalidity) remain available.
— End of Memorandum —
E. Matters Flagged for Your Review
Isbester v Knox City Council (2015) 255 CLR 135 is the leading apprehended-bias authority directly on point (a council officer who investigated and then decided an animal-destruction matter). It is not in the supplied course materials , so it is flagged 'FOR REVIEW' in the answer (ground 4.7) and should be confirmed before use; the answer's bias analysis stands on Ebner and Jia Legeng regardless.
A handful of other authorities relied on for completeness (for example Pattenden; Samrein; Carltona; Re McBain) should be confirmed as within your course list; the core analysis rests on the listed cases.
The hypothetical is a practice instrument authored for this exercise; it is not a past exam paper.
F. Structural Note (this version)

At the principal's direction, the Strongest Ground — Comparative Assessment has been moved front and centre as section 5, immediately after the grounds and before the breach-and-consequences analysis, because the choice of lead ground drives what must be proved (including whether materiality is required at all), the respondent set, and the relief. All cross-references were renumbered and re-verified. The exam time guide (9:00am start, strongest ground at 1.5x) now appears on the first page of this document.

Title: Clive's Inquiries Re RHOA Licence Application
Issue (a): Can Clive apply for merits review?
Answer:
Clive can apply for merits review, as detailed below:
Jurisdiction
Rule:
Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal Act 1998 (Vic) ('VCAT Act') s42(1) holds that VCAT has review jurisdiction where an enabling Act expressly confers it, and the relevant enabling Act is the RHOA.
RHOA s42(1) provides the statutory basis for review when a person’s application for a licence is refused.
RHOA s42(3)(a) also provides that an application must be lodged within 28 days after the decision. The time limit set under RHOA s42(3)(a) is reinforced by VCAT Act s48, which governs when the review jurisdiction is invoked.
Application:
VCAT has review jurisdiction for Clive’s case. Under RHOA s3, licence means a licence to operate a rooming house. Clive received the outcome on 21 February 2026 for his application of licence to operate a rooming house and the advice date for this memo is 23 February 2026. Therefore he also meets the temporal requirement.
Standing
Rule:
Clive would need to have standing to bring the application.
VCAT Act s48(a) allows a person to bring an application to VCAT if the person is entitled to do so by an enabling enactment.

Standing is governed by RHOA s42(1), providing specifically that “A person whose application for a licence or for renewal of a licence is refused may apply to VCAT…”
Application:
Clive has his licence application refused. Accordingly, Clive satisfies the standing requirement in RHOA s42(1).
Furthermore, the RHOA ss 9–11 set out other requirements, including age and formality. On the facts, Clive appears to satisfy these threshold criteria.
Conclusion
Clive may bring the merits review application to VCAT provided he complies with the 28-day time limit in RHOA s42(3)(a). In the event when Clive does not meet this temporal requirement, he can still consider requesting VCAT to extend a time limit fixed by RHOA s42(3)(a), in pursuant to VCAT Act s 126(1).
Issue (b) What are Clive’s prospects of success?
Answer:
Clive’s prospects of success are high for the following reasons:
Nature and function
Merits review is carried out by tribunals within the executive branch, not involving judicial power. In Victoria, VCAT performs this function.
When VCAT conducts a merits review, it “stands in the shoes” of the original decision-maker and determines the “correct or preferable decision” on the material before it. This approach is also codified in VCAT Act s 51(1)(a). High Court also held in Frugtniet and Shi that VCAT should base its decision on the best and most current evidence available.

Power and Function
In conducting the merits review, VCAT exercises the powers and functions conferred by both VCAT Act and RHOA. These powers determine the scope of VCAT’s authority and the nature of the decision it must make. VCAT remakes the decision afresh, applying the same statutory criteria that bind the Authority. Therefore, VCAT must apply RHOA s16 directly when determining whether a licence should be granted.
Rule:
RHOA s16(1)(a) mandates that if the Authority is satisfied that the applicant is a fit and proper person, the authority must grant a licence. RHOA s16(2) also mandates that (a) “the Authority must refuse to grant a licence if the Authority is satisfied that the applicant is not a fit and proper person” or (c) “any information required under section 14 has not been provided within the time specified by the Authority”. RHOA s16(2)(c) does not operate here as Clive is still within the period to provide information. So the only question here is around fit and proper person.
Relevantly, RHOA s3 defines that fit and proper person means that “none of the relevant persons meets any of the licence disqualification criteria”. RHOA s3 further holds that relevant person means that natural person.
Application:

VCAT needs to engage in statutory construction on the criteria set out in RHOA s17. It requires VCAT to determine the meaning of the statute by taking text, context and purpose approach.
VCAT must adopt the interpretation that best achieves the purpose of the statute, consistent with purposive interpretation mandated in Interpretation of Legislation Act 1984 (Vic) s35(a). Decision-makers may consult extrinsic materials.
VCAT also needs to apply the same statutory criteria that bind the authority. This is upheld in Frugtniet in which Keifel CJ, Keane and Nettle JJ stated that “AAT is not at large. It is subject to the same general constraints as the original decision-maker...”
Therefore, what VCAT needs to determine here is whether Clive is a fit and proper person. This needs to be reviewed against licence disqualification criteria, defined under RHOA s17, relevantly, the following (s17(1)(a), s17(1)(b) and s17(1)(f)(viii)):
s17(1)(a) within the preceding 10 years, a relevant person has been convicted or found guilty of an offence involving … drug cultivation … that was punishable by a term of imprisonment of 3 months or more…

The facts are not clear with regards to drug cultivation. The key is to understand if there is any conviction that was punishable by a term of imprisonment of 3 months or more. Barney’s notes state that “he has never been convicted or found guilty of an offence against any section of any of the laws listed in the request for information”. Therefore, it is unlikely that Clive fell into this category for his hydroponic tomatoes episode.
The counter argument is that given Clive’s credibility of his statement (especially on that he wanted to do rooming house service to help the vulnerable and on the other hand, he planned to use his tactics to collect debt which is likely to be illegal - see my analysis under S17(f)(viii) below, further enquiry should be made on relevant conviction in the past 10 years that might impact Clive’s chance for his application.
The better view is that further enquiry is required. For the rest of the assignment, I will assume that Clive has not been convicted under legislations relevant under RHOA s17(1)(a).
s17(1)(b) a relevant person is an insolvent under administration
This section is stated in a present tense (i.e. is) which suggests that it is the person’s current status that matters. Barney’s notes indicated that Clive was “an insolvent…All clear now.”. As Clive is no longer an insolvent now, this is unlikely to be an issue for Clive.

Clive is a debt collector and his employees ‘park a car outside their home with its hazard lights on and a big sign saying “debt collection in process’. this action is highly likely to be prohibited under s45(2)(e) and (f) of the Australian Consumer Law and Fair Trading Act 2012 (Vic) because this action (car with hazard light on and having a big sign saying debt collection in progress) intimidates the debtor by creating public pressure and exposes the debtor to ridicule by signalling to neighbours that they are in debt. Even though Clive did not do it himself, under common law principles, Clive might be held liable through vicarious liability for his employee's prohibited conduct. Under equitable principles, Clive may also be liable if he authorised, encouraged, knowingly permitted, or failed to prevent the conduct.
The counter argument is that Clive has not been found convicted or found guilty of an offence under this. Clive is not personally involved in this type of prohibited activities. So this should not be taken into consideration as RHOA s17(1)(f)(viii) is clearly referring to Clive as a natural person.
The better view is that because of no facts showing Clive’s past conviction relevant under s17(1)(f)(viii) and VCAT needing to make decision based on statutory requirements, Clive should be fine under this requirement.
Conclusion:

VCAT Act s 51(2) sets out the orders VCAT may make on review, including affirming, varying and setting aside the decision aside the decision. RHOA s4(a) and s16(1) further define the substantive powers and functions that the original decision-maker has to determine (i.e. grant or refuse) applications for licences.
Therefore, these broad remedial powers allow VCAT to fully reconsider the merits of licence application and, if appropriate, substitute with its own decision granting the licence.
Based on the analysis above, VCAT is highly likely to find Clive a fit and proper person for RHOA purposes.
Therefore, VCAT is highly likely to grant Clive the licence.
Frugtniet v Australian Securities and Investments Commission [2019] HCA 16, [51] (Bell, Gageler, Gordon and Edelman JJ).

ADMINISTRATIVE LAW — PART B
Model Essays for Past Examination Questions (2012–2025)
Seventeen essays covering thirty-three questions · Method: contention → both sides → preferred position
Prepared for Raymond · 7 June 2026 · QA-reviewed to High Distinction standard (one rework cycle + verification)
Question → Essay Map
Essay 1: Materiality & jurisdictional error — 2025, 2022, 2020, 2019, 2013 (SZMDS), 2023 (no-materiality errors)
Essay 2: Indigenous peoples & migrants — 2025 (institutional terra nullius), 2024
Essay 3: Adequacy of judicial review — 2025, 2024 (s 75(v)), 2016 (Quin & individual justice)
Essay 4: Limits of JR: legality, merits & materiality — 2024
Essay 5: Victorian Charter — 2024 (higher bar), 2023, 2022, 2014
Essay 6: Ombudsman & non-judicial accountability — 2021, 2020, 2018, 2013
Essay 7: Legal unreasonableness after Li — 2023, 2016, 2015
Essay 8: Statutory construction as the engine of JR — 2021
Essay 9: Procedural fairness, Kioa to Badari — 2022, 2012 (hearing rule)
Essay 10: Separation of powers — 2020
Essay 11: ADJR, Bond & the statutory gateways (with the ALA) — 2019, 2018, 2016, 2015, 2017 (ALA)
Essay 12: The right to reasons — 2019
Essay 13: JR, merits review & Ombudsman compared — 2016, 2018 (who vs what); absorbs the untexted 2024 MR entry
Essay 14: Standing & justiciability — 2018, 2017 (technicalities)

Essay 15: Apprehended bias: realism & Jia — 2017
Essay 16: Privative clauses, S157/Kirk & privatisation — 2017, 2014 (privative), 2014 (outsourcing)
Essay 17: The rule of law & control of discretion (Kitto J, Ipec-Air) — 2021
Note: every case cited is drawn from your seminar slides or notes (audited against an allow-list of both). The only exception is R v Anderson; Ex parte Ipec-Air, quoted in the 2021 question itself and used only in Essay 17. The compiled question list contains a '2024 MR' heading with no question text — flag for checking against the original paper.
How to Use These Model Essays
Each essay follows the required method: a clear contention stated at the outset; the case for the proposition; the case against ; and a reasoned preferred position . Each is written to 20-mark Part B scale (roughly 35–40 minutes' writing). A "Variants" note shows how to re-angle the same material to each year's phrasing. Authorities are drawn from the course materials; the handful flagged as enrichment should be verified before use.
Essay 1 — Materiality and Jurisdictional Error

Covers: 2025 ("materiality has exacerbated the tension in the proper boundaries of judicial review"); 2022 (SZMTA (Notes) dissent — "wrong turn"); 2020 (to what extent do you agree with Nettle and Gordon JJ); 2019 (unpredictability of consequences-of-breach law); 2013 ("not every lapse in logic will give rise to jurisdictional error"); 2023 (some errors jurisdictional irrespective of effect).
Contention. The majority position — that most errors are jurisdictional only if material — is a defensible construction-based settlement, but Nettle and Gordon JJ's dissent in SZMTA identifies a real cost: materiality relocates part of the lawfulness inquiry from the statute to the counterfactual judgment of the reviewing court, and to that extent the boundaries of judicial review have genuinely been strained. The preferred view is that LPDT (Sem 11–12) has now stabilised the doctrine and answered the worst of the dissent's objections, so the "wrong turn" charge ultimately fails — but only narrowly.

The case for the majority position. Materiality is anchored in statutory construction, the orthodox foundation of jurisdictional error: a statute is "ordinarily to be interpreted as incorporating a threshold of materiality" because it is unlikely that Parliament intended decisions to be invalidated by errors that could not realistically have changed anything ( Hossain (Notes) ; SZMTA , majority; confirmed in LPDT (2024) 280 CLR 321). Jurisdictional error is "a breach of an express or implied condition of a statutory conferral of decision-making authority" ( LPDT ), so the materiality threshold is not judicial invention but an implication of the legislature's own intention — the same technique as Project Blue Sky (Sem 11 s9–11) 's question whether breach was intended to invalidate. It also serves remedial common sense: certiorari is discretionary, and relief that could change nothing is futile. LPDT further disciplined the test: the threshold is "realistic possibility", a low bar; the onus dispute is reduced because the inquiry is backward-looking, "answered by reference to the decision that was made in fact"; and a category of errors — apprehended or actual bias and legal unreasonableness — never requires a separate materiality inquiry because such errors deprive the decision of a quality the statute demands regardless of outcome ( LPDT [4]–[6]; the 2023 question states exactly this).

The case against (Nettle and Gordon JJ). The dissent's three objections are formidable. First, principle: a person "is entitled to... a decision made under, and in accordance with, the terms of the statute, not the terms of the statute subject to some level of materiality" — materiality dilutes the legislature's own conditions. Secondly, institutional integrity: requiring the applicant to prove the decision could have been different invites the court to reconstruct the decision-making — "a form of merits review" that would "fundamentally change the nature of judicial review", trespassing on the legality/merits line drawn in Quin (Sem 21 s9) . Thirdly, incentive and onus: the burden falls on the person wronged, who must speculate about an administrative process they did not control; Nathanson (Sem 13 s16–17) shows how close this comes to re-running the hearing (saved there only by Gageler J's reminder that the opportunity to be heard matters most where it is assumed nothing could have been said). The 2019 charge of unpredictability also bites here: when everything turns on implication-by-construction, like cases can be decided unlike.

Preferred position. The majority view, as consolidated in LPDT , should be preferred — but for reasons that concede much to the dissent. The dissent is right that materiality involves the court in counterfactual judgment; LPDT answers this not by denying it but by domesticating it: a deliberately low "realistic possibility" threshold, a backward-looking factual inquiry rather than speculative reconstruction, and the carve-out for errors (bias, unreasonableness) where the statute's demand is unconditional. SZMDS (Sem 20 s7–11) 's caution that "not every lapse in logic will give rise to jurisdictional error" reflects the same impulse on the irrationality ground: review polices the boundaries of authority, not the quality of reasoning as such. What remains of the critique is real but tolerable: a residue of unpredictability inherent in any construction-based doctrine, and a modest evidential burden on applicants. On balance, materiality is better understood not as a wrong turn but as the price of taking Project Blue Sky seriously — Parliament's intention governs not only what conditions exist, but what follows from their breach.

Variants. For 2025 ("exacerbated the tension in the proper boundaries"), lead with the Quin boundary and present materiality as managed strain rather than rupture. For 2020/2022, structure expressly around the SZMTA majority/minority exchange. For 2019, foreground Project Blue Sky and Bhardwaj (Sem 11 s12–13) and argue stability has improved post- LPDT . For 2013, centre SZMDS (two approaches: Crennan and Bell JJ's "open on the evidence" test versus Gummow A-CJ and Kiefel J's process-focused logicality) and connect to the later materiality settlement.
Essay 2 — Indigenous Peoples, Migrants and Administrative Power
Covers: 2025 ("institutional terra nullius"); 2024 ("Indigenous Australians and migrants are especially disserved by existing doctrines").
Contention. The proposition is substantially correct: the structural features of Australian administrative law — its statutory positivism, deference to executive evaluation, and remedial formalism — bear hardest on those most exposed to discretionary power and least able to invoke it. But the critique must be qualified: the same body of doctrine has also produced some of the most significant vindications of Indigenous and migrant interests, and the deficiency lies less in judicial review's design than in what it was never designed to do.

The case for the proposition. The migration jurisdiction supplies the starkest evidence. The most aggressive privative clause litigated in Australia was a migration provision ( Plaintiff S157 (Sem 23 s8–10) ); materiality doctrine itself was forged almost entirely in migration appeals ( Hossain; SZMTA; Nathanson; LPDT (Sem 11–12) ), meaning the doctrinal "price" of error falls disproportionately on visa applicants; and the high thresholds of SZMDS (Sem 20 s7–11) irrationality and SZVFW (Sem 21 s9) unreasonableness operate in a context of acute vulnerability, language barriers and credibility assessment — where, as Gageler J observed in Nathanson , the danger is assuming nothing the person said could have mattered. For Indigenous peoples, the record discloses a different pathology: executive power exercised about them with consideration obligations honoured thinly. Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16) required the Minister to consider the most recent and accurate information about Aboriginal land claims precisely because the department had failed to; Tickner (Sem 17 s17–19) v Chapman demanded an "active intellectual process" because the Minister had not read the representations protecting Ngarrindjeri heritage; R v Toohey (Sem 17 s7–8) concerned executive power deployed to defeat an Aboriginal land claim. That these are the canonical authorities is itself the indictment: the leading cases on ignored considerations are cases about ignoring Indigenous voices — a doctrinal echo of what the 2025 question calls "institutional terra nullius". Standing doctrine adds a layer: Onus (Sem 9 s6–8) v Alcoa succeeded, but only by establishing a "special interest" exceptional to the general rule that cultural concern is insufficient ( ACF (Sem 9 s5–6) ).

The case against. The same cases read the other way. Peko-Wallsend , Tickner , Toohey and Onus are victories: the grounds proved capable of compelling attention to Indigenous interests, and Kioa (Sem 13 s8–10) itself — the foundation of modern procedural fairness — vindicated a migrant facing deportation, as did Saeed (Sem 13 s11–12) , WZARH (Sem 13 s13–15) , Nathanson and Plaintiff M70 (Sem 20 s7–11) (which stopped the "Malaysia Declaration" on jurisdictional-fact grounds). Section 75(v) has proved structurally incorruptible ( Plaintiff S157 ), and it is migrants who have been its chief beneficiaries. On this view the doctrines are facially neutral; the disproportionate litigation burden reflects the volume and intrusiveness of the statutory schemes Parliament enacts, not bias within review doctrine — and the remedy lies with Parliament, the Ombudsman and merits review (the ART; formerly the AAT), not with reshaping judicial review.

Preferred position. The proposition should be accepted in qualified form. Judicial review has been an indispensable shield, but it is a shield with structural gaps that fall along predictable lines: it cannot question the merits ( Quin (Sem 21 s9) ); it polices consideration but not empathy or weight ( Peko-Wallsend ); its remedies return the decision to the same repository ( Bhardwaj (Sem 11 s12–13) logic); standing and special-interest rules discount communal and cultural injury ( ACF / Onus ); and materiality now asks the excluded to prove that inclusion could have mattered. Those gaps are facially neutral but not neutrally distributed. The honest conclusion is that Indigenous peoples and migrants are disserved less by the doctrines' terms than by their limits — and that filling the gap is the work of the legislature, charters of rights, and the non-judicial integrity institutions, as much as of the courts.
Variants. For 2025, anchor the "narrative of exclusion" in Toohey , Tickner and Onus and engage the "terra nullius" metaphor directly — the executive treating the Indigenous interest as an empty field. For 2024, give migrants equal billing through the Kioa – LPDT line.
Essay 3 — The Adequacy of Judicial Review in Protecting Individuals

Covers: 2025 ("can common law judicial review adequately protect individuals from harm by the executive"); 2024 ("does judicial review under s 75(v) ensure adequate accountability and protection"); 2016 (Brennan J in Quin (Sem 21 s9) — justice in the individual case).
Contention. Judicial review cannot, and does not pretend to, adequately protect individuals from executive harm — and that is by design. Its constitutional function is to enforce the limits of power, with individual justice an "occasional consequence" ( Quin ). Adequacy therefore has to be assessed at the level of the system: judicial review is the indispensable but deliberately incomplete core of a larger accountability architecture.

The case that protection is adequate. Three features give review real protective force. First, entrenchment : s 75(v) (and, for the States, the supervisory jurisdiction confirmed in Kirk (Sem 23 s11–12) ) cannot be removed by Parliament, so a minimum of judicially enforceable legality survives even the most determined privative clause ( Plaintiff S157 (Sem 23 s8–10) ; Graham (Sem 23 s13–15) : Parliament cannot deny the court the ability to enforce the limits of an officer's power). Secondly, reach : the modern grounds protect interests, not just rights — procedural fairness presumptively attaches to any power apt to affect rights or interests adversely ( Kioa (Sem 13 s8–10) ; Badari (Sem 14 s6–9) ), bias doctrine guards impartiality prophylactically ( Ebner (Sem 15 s10–12) ), and unreasonableness condemns decisions lacking evident and intelligible justification ( Li (Sem 21 s9–14)). Thirdly, practical efficacy : quashing and mandamus actually undo unlawful decisions ( Bhardwaj (Sem 11 s12–13) : a decision affected by jurisdictional error is no decision at all), and the cases show individuals — often the least powerful — succeeding against the Commonwealth ( Kioa; Aala; M70; Nathanson (Sem 13 s16–17) ).

The case that protection is inadequate. The structural objections are equally real. Review is confined to legality: the court must not "trespass on the merits" ( Quin ), so a harsh, unwise or unjust decision that is lawful is untouchable — the gap the 2021 Ombudsman question calls "lawful but wrong". Its remedies are weak in personal terms: the matter is remitted to the same decision-maker, who may lawfully reach the same outcome; no damages flow from invalidity as such. Its thresholds are forbidding: unreasonableness is "necessarily stringent" ( SZVFW (Sem 21 s9) ), irrationality demands that no logical route led to the satisfaction ( SZMDS (Sem 20 s7–11) ), and materiality requires the applicant to show a realistic possibility of a different outcome ( LPDT (Sem 11–12) ). Its gateways exclude: standing demands a special interest ( ACF (Sem 9 s5–6); Onus (Sem 9 s6–8) ), and costs, delay and complexity make the Supreme Court or High Court a remote prospect for most people actually harmed by government. And it is reactive: review corrects single decisions after the fact; it cannot reform a defective administrative culture.

Preferred position. The honest answer is "adequate for its purpose; inadequate as the sole protection — and rightly so". Brennan J's framing in Quin is not a confession of failure but a statement of constitutional role: under the separation of powers, the courts' legitimacy in declaring invalidity depends precisely on their not re-deciding merits. The gap is real but is deliberately filled elsewhere: merits review (the ART/VCAT) supplies the "correct or preferable" decision standing in the shoes of the decision-maker ( Drake (Sem 5 s9–13); Shi (Sem 5 s14–15) ); the Ombudsman investigates defective administration beyond legality; reasons obligations (ADJR s 13; ART; ALA s 8) discipline decision-making before litigation; and the Victorian Charter adds a rights-compatibility obligation (s 38). Judicial review's adequacy should be judged as the keystone of that arch — irreplaceable ( Kirk ; S157 ) but never sufficient alone. The strongest version of the critique is therefore not that review fails at its task, but that access to it — cost, standing, complexity — undermines even the task it sets itself; that is where reform belongs.

Variants. For 2024 (s 75(v)-specific), foreground entrenchment, the Hickman (Sem 23 s9)/S157 construction technique, and Graham , and weigh them against the merits/remedies/access limits. For 2016, lead with the Quin dictum itself — review "often does" remedy injustice, but that is "an occasional consequence", not the rationale, which is "simply to enforce obedience to the law" — then map the "other developments" limb across merits review, the Ombudsman, reasons obligations and the Charter.
Essay 4 — The Limits of Judicial Review: Legality, Merits and Materiality
Covers: 2024 ("judicial review ought to be concerned strictly with lawfulness... rather than the relative significance of legal errors; when concerned with the latter, judicial review approaches merits review").
Contention. The proposition is half right. The legality/merits boundary is fundamental and constitutionally compelled — but the claim that weighing the "relative significance" of errors breaches the separation of powers misunderstands materiality: assessing whether breach of a statutory condition was intended to invalidate is itself an exercise in legality, not merits.

The case for the proposition. Quin (Sem 21 s9) draws the line: the court's duty is to declare and enforce the law, "and no further"; the merits, "to the extent that they can be distinguished from legality", belong to the repository of the power. Materiality seems to cross that line: it asks the court to consider what the decision-maker would realistically have done absent the error — a counterfactual evaluation of the decision-making process itself. Nettle and Gordon JJ in SZMTA (Notes) made exactly this charge: requiring the applicant to prove the consequences of error "would become a form of merits review" and "fundamentally change the nature of judicial review". The separation-of-powers anxiety is genuine: a court that grades errors by significance looks like a court supervising quality, not legality.

The case against. The objection proves too much. The "relative significance" of an error has always been part of legality analysis: Project Blue Sky (Sem 11 s9–11) asks whether Parliament intended breach of the particular condition to produce invalidity — necessarily a judgment about the provision's significance within the scheme; the discretion to refuse relief for futility ( Enfield (Sem 8 s13) line) has always involved asking whether the error could have mattered; and certiorari for non-jurisdictional error of law on the face of the record ( Craig (Notes) ) has always discriminated between errors that count and errors that do not. Materiality, as LPDT (Sem 11–12) settles it, is statutory construction: the threshold exists because Parliament is taken not to intend invalidity for errors with no realistic possibility of affecting the outcome. The court is still measuring the decision against the statute — the Quin task — not substituting its view of the correct outcome. And the doctrine itself preserves the boundary: where the statute's demand is unconditional (bias; unreasonableness), no materiality inquiry arises at all ( LPDT ).

Preferred position. The proposition should be rejected in its strong form but honoured in its caution. Materiality is legality work, done with construction tools, and LPDT 's backward-looking, low-threshold formulation keeps the counterfactual inquiry modest. But the caution earns its place: the more elaborately a court reconstructs what the decision-maker would have done, the closer it sails to the merits — which is why the threshold is "realistic possibility", not probability, and why the inquiry attends to the decision actually made rather than to an idealised re-decision. The separation of powers is maintained not by refusing to assess significance, but by the discipline with which significance is assessed.
Variants. This essay pairs with Essay 1; for the 2024 phrasing, lead with Quin and the separation of powers, and use Essay 1's materiality material as the second movement.
Essay 5 — The Victorian Charter and Judicial Review
Covers: 2024 ("s 38(1) sets a higher standard than common law"); 2023 ("the Charter adds little to the Supreme Court's inherent jurisdiction"); 2022 ("no significant difference to the traditional grounds"); 2014 ("administrative law fails to ensure human-rights-compatible decision-making").

Contention. The Charter adds something real but bounded: s 38(1) imposes obligations that are genuinely more demanding than the common law grounds — but its remedial architecture (s 39 piggy-backing; no invalidity per Certain Children (Sem 18 s11–14) ; no entitlement to damages, s 39(3)) means the addition is more behavioural than curial. "Adds little" is wrong as to standards and largely right as to remedies.

The case that the Charter adds little. Three features support the sceptics. First, s 39's "piggy-backing" design: Charter unlawfulness can be raised only where the applicant already has an independent ground of relief — it confers no standalone cause of action ( Bare (Sem 18 s7–10) v IBAC ). Secondly, the consequence of breach is deliberately muted: breach of s 38(1) "does not of itself give rise to invalidity and... does not constitute jurisdictional error" ( Certain Children (No 2) ) — so a Charter win may produce a declaration and little more. Thirdly, much of s 38(1)'s work is replicated by the traditional grounds: failing to consider rights resembles failing to consider a relevant consideration ( Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16) ); acting incompatibly with rights in a gross case will often be legally unreasonable ( Li (Sem 21 s9–14)); and s 38(2) excuses authorities who could not reasonably have acted differently under their statute — preserving primary legislation's primacy as Momcilovic (Sem 4 s9–12) preserved it for s 32 interpretation.

The case that the Charter adds much. The standard is higher, and the cases say so expressly. Section 38(1) has two cumulative limbs — procedural and substantive (Tate JA, Bare (Sem 18 s8); Certain Children (Sem 18 s12)). The procedural limb is a supplementary obligation that constrains the exercise of power (Tate JA, Bare (Sem 18 s9)), and its content is the four-limb HJ test : (1) understand which rights may be affected and how; (2) seriously turn the mind to the impact on the affected person; (3) identify the countervailing interests; and (4) balance the competing interests ( Thompson (Sem 18 s18); summarised Bare; Certain Children; Thompson at Sem 19 s8). That this exceeds the common law is stated in terms: "'proper'... must be higher than that generally applicable at common law" ( Thompson (Sem 18 s17)), with the court applying "a greater degree of scrutiny... than in a conventional judicial review proceeding" ( Thompson (Sem 18 s18)). The common law, by contrast, polices only whether a mandatory consideration was ignored and leaves weight to the decision-maker ( Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16)). On the substantive limb, once a right is limited the onus shifts to the State to show the limit is "demonstrably justified" under s 7(2) (nature of the right; purpose; extent; relationship to purpose), the court showing only limited deference ( Certain Children (Sem 18 s12–13); Sem 19 s9). Nothing in the traditional grounds reverses an onus or requires proportionality. And the Charter reframes who is accountable: "public authority" reaches functional, not merely institutional, government (s 4), addressing part of the privatisation gap.

Preferred position. Both halves of the debate are right about different things. As a standard of conduct, s 38(1) is a genuine ratchet: it requires a quality of engagement with the affected person's rights that Peko-Wallsend never did, and s 7(2) subjects limitation to a justificatory discipline the common law lacks. As a source of curial outcomes, the Charter is deliberately weak: s 39 tethers it to existing grounds, Certain Children denies it jurisdictional-error status. The preferred contention for the 2024 phrasing is therefore: the proposition is correct — s 38(1) does set a higher bar — while the 2022/2023 scepticism is best answered "true of remedies, false of obligations". The deeper point is that the Charter was designed to change administration, not litigation: a dialogue model in which the courts' contribution is declaratory and the real work is done inside public authorities.
Variants. For 2014, broaden: the common law protects rights only interstitially (the principle of legality; PF), merits review can consider rights, and the Charter is the structural answer — then assess its limits as above.
Essay 6 — The Ombudsman and Non-Judicial Accountability

Covers: 2021 ("lawful doesn't mean it isn't wrong"); 2020 ("collaboration without capture"); 2018 (Ombudsman promotes fairness and dignity, "just as important" as judicial review); 2013 (McMillan — a new culture in public administration).
Contention. The Ombudsman is not a lesser substitute for judicial review but a different instrument aimed at a different failure: defective administration rather than unlawful administration. The "just as important" claim is therefore defensible — provided importance is measured by systemic effect on administrative culture rather than by coercive power, where the Ombudsman is deliberately weak.

The case for the Ombudsman's importance. Judicial review's jurisdictional limit is the Ombudsman's starting point: a decision can be perfectly lawful yet "wrong" — harsh, mistaken on the facts, rude, slow, or simply unfair — and Quin (Sem 21 s9) puts all of that beyond the courts. The Ombudsman investigates precisely that territory: action that is contrary to law, or unreasonable, unjust, oppressive or improperly discriminatory, or simply "wrong" (the grounds set out in the Ombudsman's enabling legislation), with recommendations rather than orders. Its comparative advantages are structural: it is free, informal and accessible (no standing rules, no costs risk — answering the access critique in Essay 3); it can investigate own-motion and systemically, addressing patterns rather than single decisions; it can look at merits, manners and maladministration alike; and its currency — publicity, parliamentary reporting, persuasion — produces the cultural change McMillan describes: three decades of administration "more attuned to the rights of the public". Neave's "collaboration without capture" captures the method: effectiveness depends on working relationships with agencies (collaboration) while preserving the independence that gives findings credibility (without capture) — an institutional analogue of the bias rule's insistence on both engagement and impartiality.

The case against. The Ombudsman's weaknesses mirror its strengths. It cannot quash, compel or bind: where an agency digs in, the citizen still needs the courts ( Bhardwaj (Sem 11 s12–13) -style nullity is available only judicially). Its independence is fragile in exactly the way Neave concedes — budgetary dependence, reliance on agency cooperation, and the risk that collaboration shades into capture; "resistance to oversight" is the norm Neave reports, not the exception. Its jurisdiction typically excludes ministers and policy, the very apex where the most consequential discretion sits. And its justice is administrative, not adjudicative: no hearing, no enforceable rights, outcomes contingent on goodwill. On this view the 2018 proposition overstates: the judge's writ runs whether government likes it or not ( S157 (Sem 23 s8–10); Kirk (Sem 23 s11–12) ); the Ombudsman's influence runs only as far as persuasion.

Preferred position. The preferred view treats the comparison as a category error resolved by the system view: the rule of law requires both obedience to law (courts) and quality of administration (integrity bodies), and each institution is "most important" within its own register. The Ombudsman's lack of coercive power is not a defect but the condition of its access to government's inner workings — agencies open files to an investigator who recommends that they would resist with a litigant who demands. "Collaboration without capture" is thus the correct operating principle, but it must be underwritten by structural safeguards (tenure, parliamentary reporting lines, adequate funding) because capture is a standing risk, not a hypothetical. The 2021 proposition should be embraced: "lawful but wrong" is a real and large category, and recognising it is the strongest justification for a multi-institutional accountability system in which the Ombudsman, merits review and judicial review are complements, not rivals.

Variants. For 2020, organise around the Neave quotation: collaboration (effectiveness), capture (risk), and the administrative-law principles (accountability, transparency, fairness, rule of law) that the balance serves. For 2013, foreground McMillan's culture claim and evaluate with examples of systemic investigation; for 2018, run the judge/Ombudsman comparison front and centre with Quin as the hinge.
Essay 7 — Legal Unreasonableness after Li
Covers: 2023 ("Li is irreconcilable with the merits/legality distinction in Quin (Sem 21 s9)"); 2016 ("does not depend on definitional formulae"); 2015 ("Li increased the risk that courts will be seen to be engaging with the merits").
Contention. Li (Sem 21 s9–14) loosened Wednesbury (Sem 21 s9)'s straitjacket without abandoning the Quin boundary: "evident and intelligible justification" polices the rationality of the path to a decision, not the merits of its content. The risk of merits creep is real and acknowledged — but the post- Li cases ( SZVFW (Sem 21 s9); DUA16 (Sem 22 s7–8) ) show the High Court actively disciplining the doctrine back within the boundary, so "irreconcilable" overstates.

The case that Li crossed the line. Wednesbury demanded a decision "so unreasonable that no reasonable repository could have made it" — a formula whose very extremity guaranteed distance from the merits. Li replaced certainty-by-absurdity with evaluative judgment: a decision lacking "an evident and intelligible justification" invites the court to assess the adequacy of justification, which is uncomfortably close to assessing quality; and where reasons are unexpressed, the court may infer unreasonableness from outcome — reasoning from result, the signature of merits review. French CJ's invocation of disproportion, the plurality's resort to an area-of-decisional-freedom metaphor, and Gageler J's caution that the test remains "stringent", all betray the anxiety. The 2015 proposition that the "opening up" increased the perception of merits engagement is on this view plainly right: perception follows method, and the method is now evaluative.

The case that Li is reconcilable with Quin. Three disciplines hold the line. First , the presumption frame: the legislature is taken to intend that a statutory discretion be exercised reasonably ( Quin; Li; SZVFW ) — so unreasonableness review is the enforcement of a statutory implication, squarely the Quin task of policing limits. Secondly , the threshold: the test is "necessarily stringent" ( SZVFW , Kiefel CJ), an appellate court must not substitute its own view of what is reasonable, and the standard is breached only where no intelligible path connects material to outcome — not where the court would have weighed things differently (weight remains for the decision-maker: Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16) ). Thirdly , the case law's own trajectory: SZVFW reversed a judge who had treated his disagreement as unreasonableness; DUA16 declined to brand even a negligent agent-error context unreasonable in DUA16's own case while finding CHK16's clear; the doctrine condemns the unexplained and inexplicable , not the unattractive. The 2016 proposition supplies the jurisprudential key: unreasonableness is a conclusion, not a formula — "does not depend on definitional formulae" — an area-of-judgment concept whose content is supplied by the particular statute, which is why it cannot be reduced to, or impeached by, any single verbal test.

Preferred position. Li is best understood as relocating unreasonableness from absurdity to accountability-for-justification , while Quin continues to mark the outer boundary. The reconciliation is achieved by the standard of review, not the standard of conduct: the question is never "was this decision right or wise" but "is there an intelligible justification within the statute's purposes" — and the High Court's post- Li corrections show the boundary being enforced in practice. The honest concession to the 2015/2023 critiques is that the doctrine now requires judicial self-restraint as a virtue rather than guaranteeing it by formula; the safeguard is the stringency insisted on in SZVFW and the refusal to find unreasonableness where reasons disclose a rational, if debatable, path. Properly applied, Li is not irreconcilable with Quin ; it is Quin 's implication — that power must stay within the limits Parliament intended — applied to the implied condition of reasonableness.
Variants. For 2016, lead with the "no definitional formulae" point and use Li/SZVFW/DUA16 as illustrations of context-specific application. For 2015, focus on perception versus doctrine and the post-Li disciplining cases.
Essay 8 — Statutory Construction as the Engine of Judicial Review

Covers: 2021 ("not everything required to supervise the legality of administrative power can be reduced to principles of statutory construction").
Contention. The proposition is correct but narrowly: construction is the dominant and legitimating technique of modern review — yet at least three load-bearing elements (the constitutional entrenchment of review itself, the common law's procedural-fairness presumptions, and equitable remedial discretion) cannot honestly be reduced to construction without fiction.

The case for construction's sufficiency. The modern law systematically converts grounds into implications of the statute: jurisdictional error is breach of an "express or implied condition of the statutory conferral" ( LPDT (Sem 11–12) ); whether breach invalidates is the Project Blue Sky (Sem 11 s9–11) purpose question; materiality is an implied threshold ( Hossain (Notes) ); reasonableness is an implied condition ( Li; SZVFW (Sem 21 s9) ); mandatory considerations are found in subject-matter, scope and purpose ( Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16) ); jurisdictional facts are identified by construction ( Enfield; M70 (Sem 20 s7–11) ); procedural fairness applies subject to displacement by plain words ( Saeed (Sem 13 s11–12); Badari (Sem 14 s6–9) ); even privative clauses are dissolved by construction ( Hickman; S157 (Sem 23 s8–10) ). The attraction is constitutional legitimacy: if every ground is Parliament's implication, review never trespasses on merits ( Quin (Sem 21 s9) ) — the court only ever enforces the statute.

The case against sufficiency. First, the foundations are not statutory. Section 75(v) and the Kirk (Sem 23 s11–12) principle entrench supervisory jurisdiction against statutes; the technique that defeats privative clauses is construction in form but constitutional necessity in substance — the clause is read down because it must be, not because Parliament meant it. Secondly, the common law supplies content that no realistic Parliament enacted: the "strong presumption" of procedural fairness ( Badari; Kioa (Sem 13 s8–10) ) and the bias rule ( Ebner (Sem 15 s10–12) ) are judicial creations attributed to legislative intention by courteous fiction — Parliament never "intended" the fair-minded lay observer. Thirdly, remedies: the discretion to refuse relief (delay, futility, third-party prejudice — Enfield line) and the equitable remedies (injunction, declaration) operate on principles owing nothing to the particular statute. Fourthly, non-statutory executive power exists, and its supervision (justiciability of prerogative and contractual power) cannot be construction of an Act at all.

Preferred position. Construction is the grammar of review, not its source . The preferred account is a two-level one: at the surface, courts legitimately express every ground as statutory implication, and this is more than rhetoric — it disciplines review to each scheme and honours parliamentary supremacy; beneath, the implications are drawn from a common law and constitutional bedrock (legality presumptions, fairness, entrenched supervision) that persists whatever any statute says. The High Court's own practice betrays the duality: S157 simultaneously "construes" the privative clause and announces that review for jurisdictional error cannot be ousted. The proposition should therefore be accepted — with the addendum that the irreducible residue is not an embarrassment but the constitutional point: some things bind because of what courts are, not because of what Parliament said.
Essay 9 — Procedural Fairness from Kioa to Badari
Covers: 2022 ("the principles governing procedural fairness remain as strong now as when first articulated in Kioa (Sem 13 s8–10)"); 2012 ("the fair hearing rule is one of the most important safeguards of individual rights").

Contention. The principles are stronger than in 1985 in their foundations and reach, but their bite has been qualified at the remedial end by materiality. Kioa 's framework — presumptive application, flexible content — has not merely survived; Badari (Sem 14 s6–9) has entrenched it. The accurate statement is: stronger threshold, steadier content, weaker consequences.

The case that the principles remain strong. The threshold presumption has hardened: from Mason J's "common law duty to act fairly" in Kioa to Saeed (Sem 13 s11–12) 's insistence that only plain words of necessary intendment displace fairness, to Badari [2025] HCA 47 confirming a "strong common law presumption" wherever a power is apt to affect rights or interests adversely. Content doctrine matured rather than weakened: disclosure of credible, relevant and significant adverse material ( Kioa , Brennan J) — the very obligation enforced in Nathanson (Sem 13 s16–17) , where undisclosed family-violence material vitiated the decision; the opportunity to respond when the basis of decision shifts ( WZARH (Sem 13 s13–15) ); and the abandonment of "legitimate expectation" ( WZARH ) was a strengthening, removing an unstable gatekeeper so that fairness turns on interests, not expectations. The bias limb likewise: Ebner (Sem 15 s10–12) 's two-step has proved adaptable from judges to administrators, extending to social-media prejudgment ( Hendry (Sem 15 s13) ). The hearing rule remains, as the 2012 question says, the paradigm safeguard: it protects before harm occurs, costs the state little, and improves accuracy — Nathanson (Gageler J): the opportunity matters most exactly when it is assumed nothing could have been said.

The case that the principles have weakened. Three qualifications. First, materiality : since SZMTA and LPDT (Sem 11–12) , a denial of fairness yields invalidity only where a realistic possibility of a different outcome is shown — Kioa 's generation asked whether fairness was denied, not whether it would have mattered; the dissent's warning that this shifts the burden to the wronged applies with most force here. Secondly, statutory codes : Parliament increasingly prescribes exhaustive procedures (the Migration Act's codes considered in Saeed ), shrinking the common law's field even as its presumption strengthens. Thirdly, content flexibility cuts both ways: "from a full trial to nothingness" ( Badari ; Brennan J in Kioa ) licenses minimal process at the policy end — Badari itself distinguishes formulating general policy (minimal fairness) from individual application — and flexibility can become a vocabulary for dilution.

Preferred position. The 2022 proposition is correct with one honest amendment: the principles are as strong — arguably stronger, given Saeed and Badari — but their consequences are more conditional than in 1985. The materiality qualification is best defended on Kioa's own logic: fairness exists to protect the practical opportunity to affect outcomes, so an error that could not realistically have affected anything sits oddly with invalidity — yet Nathanson shows the courts policing the qualification so that it does not become a licence to ignore people who "would have lost anyway". For the 2012 phrasing, the hearing rule's claim to be the most important safeguard rests on its breadth (all rights and interests), its timing (before the harm), and its constitutional resilience (only clear words displace it) — credentials no other ground matches.
Variants. For 2022, lead with the Kioa-to-Badari arc and the materiality qualification. For 2012 ("most important safeguard"), lead with the safeguard credentials (breadth, timing, resilience) and use Kioa/Nathanson as the two required cases.
Essay 10 — Separation of Powers and Administrative Law
Covers: 2020 ("our study of administrative law calls into question the idea that Australia has a strong separation of powers").

Contention. Administrative law simultaneously proves and disproves a strong separation: the judicial/executive boundary is rigorously policed (its strongest wall), while the legislative/executive boundary is porous by design. The accurate conclusion is a strong but selective separation — strong where Ch III operates, weak everywhere else, with administrative law itself built to compensate for that weakness.
The case that separation is weak. The executive routinely exercises quasi-legislative power (delegated legislation — the very subject of Toohey (Sem 17 s7–8) and Vanstone (Sem 24 s8–11) v Clark ) and quasi-judicial power (tribunals determining rights, merits review re-deciding cases — permissible precisely because the ART/VCAT are not Ch III courts and apply "correct or preferable" rather than judicial method: Drake (Sem 5 s9–13) ). Parliament, dominated by the executive, can confer discretions of breathtaking width ("the Minister may, if satisfied..."), attempt to oust review (privative clauses), and validate breaches prospectively. Responsible government — the supposed political check — depends on the executive's own majority. On this view the textbook tripartite separation is, outside the courts, more diagram than description.

The case that separation is strong. Where the judicial power is concerned, the separation has real teeth — its source the Chapter III entrenchment of the courts' supervisory role recognised in Plaintiff S157 (Sem 23 s8–10) and Kirk (Sem 23 s11–12) — and administrative law is its sharpest expression. Parliament cannot withdraw s 75(v) review ( Plaintiff S157 ) nor deprive State Supreme Courts of supervisory jurisdiction ( Kirk ); it cannot enact a law that denies a court the ability to enforce the limits of an officer's power ( Graham (Sem 23 s13–15) ). Conversely, the courts police themselves out of the executive's province : the legality/merits line ( Quin (Sem 21 s9) ), the refusal to re-weigh considerations ( Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16) ), the stringency of unreasonableness ( SZVFW (Sem 21 s9) ) — these are separation-of-powers doctrines operating against judicial overreach. Even the materiality debate (Essays 1, 4) is at bottom a separation debate. The entire architecture of review — grounds expressed as statutory implications, remedies that remit rather than re-decide — is the separation of powers made procedural.

Preferred position. The study of administrative law refines rather than refutes the strong-separation thesis: Australia maintains a genuinely strong separation of judicial power (in both directions — courts protected from ouster, executive protected from judicial merits intrusion) and a deliberately weak separation between legislature and executive, with administrative law functioning as the compensating mechanism. The non-judicial accountability institutions (Ombudsman, merits review, reasons obligations) exist precisely because the political branches blur; the judicial doctrines remain rigid precisely because Ch III does not. The proposition is therefore right about the system's design but wrong about its most examinable feature: nothing in the course suggests the Ch III separation is weak — S157 , Kirk and Graham suggest it is the strongest structural commitment in Australian public law.
Essay 11 — The ADJR Act, Bond and the Statutory Gateways (with the ALA)
Covers: 2019 (common law/constitutional underpinnings "overshadowed" by statutory mechanisms); 2018 (ADJR and ALA "different but equally flawed"); 2016 ("trapped in 1977"); 2015 ("the wrong turn happened with Bond (Sem 10 s5)"); 2017 ("does the ALA continue to serve any useful purpose").

Contention. The ADJR Act was the great rationalising reform of Australian administrative law, and its gateways have aged into its principal defect: "decision of an administrative character made under an enactment", as glossed by Bond and Tang (Notes) , now excludes much of how modern government acts. But "trapped in 1977" and "the wrong turn was Bond" each tell only half the story: the deeper cause is the renaissance of the constitutional avenues (s 75(v); s 39B; Kirk (Sem 23 s11–12) ), which made the ADJR's limits matter less — and the ALA's survival is justified by exactly one feature, its records/reasons provisions.

The case for the critiques. The gateway trilogy bites in sequence. Bond confined "decision" to acts that are final, operative and determinative, relegating intermediate findings to "conduct" — protecting administration from fragmentation but excluding much real harm done along the way. Tang required the decision to be authorised by the enactment and to derive its capacity to affect rights from the statute — excising government action sourced in contract, funding agreements and corporate instruments at the very moment government began acting through them (the privatisation gap; cf Essay 16). The Act's schedules exclude swathes of sensitive decision-making; its list of grounds froze a 1977 snapshot while the common law moved ( Li (Sem 21 s9–14)'s reasonableness; materiality; Badari (Sem 14 s6–9) ); and the reasons obligation (s 13) is hedged with exceptions. The ALA is "equally flawed" differently: its gateway — a "decision" by a "tribunal" required to observe natural justice (ss 2–3) — is narrower and more technical still ( Masters v McCubbery (Notes) ), its 30-day limit is harsh, and it adds little to Order 56.

The case against. The critiques undervalue what the statutes did and still do. The ADJR Act invented systematic reasons-on-request (s 13) — the cultural reform McMillan credits with transforming administration (Essay 6) — codified intelligible grounds, and made review accessible in a single Federal Court application; Bond itself was a defensible reading protecting decision-making from interlocutory siege — and the common law shows the flexibility the ADJR text lacks: certiorari can reach even a preliminary recommendation with a discernible legal effect on rights ( Hot Holdings (Sem 10 s7) v Creasy ). The constitutional avenues' renaissance (the 2019 quotation's "overshadowing" in reverse) means the gateways are no longer outcome-determinative: what ADJR excludes, s 39B/s 75(v) catches — S157 (Sem 23 s8–10) guarantees the floor. And the ALA earns its keep through s 8 (reasons on request) and especially s 10: reasons become part of the record, resurrecting certiorari for non-jurisdictional error of law on the face of the record in Victoria — a genuine, distinctive advantage over Order 56 review ( Craig (Notes); Kirk ).

Preferred position. The preferred contention is that the statutory gateways are flawed but the system is not: Australia effectively runs a two-track design in which the statutory tracks provide amenity (reasons, codified grounds, simpler procedure) and the constitutional/common law tracks provide the guarantee ( S157; Kirk ). On that view, Bond was less a wrong turn than a turn whose costs were absorbed elsewhere; "trapped in 1977" is true of the ADJR's text but not of the system's capacity; and the ALA "continues to serve a useful purpose" — but only just, and only because of ss 8 and 10. The reform implication is modernisation of the gateways (decision/enactment definitions; reasons exceptions), not abandonment of the statutory model that made review usable.
Variants. For 2019, frame around the "overshadowing" quotation and argue the pendulum has swung back to the constitutional core. For 2015, organise around Bond's finality requirement; for 2017 (ALA), make ss 8/10 and the 30-day limit the spine; for 2018, run ADJR and ALA in strict parallel.
Essay 12 — The Right to Reasons
