Covers: 2019 ("the statutory obligation to provide reasons is one of the most important reforms... a justification for decisions and a means for the courts to extend other administrative law principles").

Contention. The proposition is correct, and the right to reasons is best understood as the connective tissue of the whole accountability system: without reasons, most grounds of review are unprovable, merits review is uninformed, and the Ombudsman investigates blind. Its principal weakness is its incompleteness — there is no common law right, so the entitlement is only as wide as each statute.

The case for the proposition. Reasons serve the dual function the question identifies. As justification , the discipline of articulating reasons improves decisions before any review: a decision-maker who must explain must engage — the "active intellectual process" Tickner (Sem 17 s17–19) demands becomes self-enforcing, and McMillan's "new culture" (Essay 6) is largely a reason-giving culture. As a lever for the courts , reasons are the evidentiary foundation of nearly every ground: failure to consider ( Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16); Tickner ) is proved from what the reasons omit; irrationality ( SZMDS (Sem 20 s7–11) ) and unreasonableness ( Li (Sem 21 s9–14) — a decision lacking "evident and intelligible justification" is, literally, a reasons-focused standard) from what they contain; error of law on the face of the record from their terms — with ALA s 10 deeming reasons part of the record, dramatically widening certiorari in Victoria; bias and improper purpose from their tone and gaps. The statutory architecture confirms centrality: ADJR s 13, the ART's reasons obligations, VCAT, ALA s 8 — every reform generation has re-enacted the entitlement.

The case against. Three deflations. First, no common law right to reasons exists, and the statutes are gap-ridden: ADJR s 13 carries exclusions and schedules; the ALA gives reasons only of "tribunals"; many of the most consequential decisions fall outside all of them. Secondly, reasons can be defensive artefacts: standardised, lawyer-proofed text reciting the statutory formula ("I am satisfied that...") familiar from every licensing file, concealing the real process; courts respond by cautioning against an over-zealous search of reasons for error and by leaving the weight of considerations to the decision-maker ( Plaintiff M1 (Sem 19 s7) ; Peko-Wallsend ), which simultaneously protects decision-makers from drafting slips and weakens reasons as a lever. Thirdly, the obligation arises after the decision: it cannot substitute for participation (the hearing rule) and may rationalise rather than explain.

Preferred position. The proposition should be affirmed with the qualification that the reform remains unfinished. Reasons are the hinge between the justificatory and supervisory faces of administrative law: they operationalise accountability (a decision that cannot be explained cannot be defended) and they make the other grounds practically available. The honest concessions — no common law entitlement; template reasons; post-hoc rationalisation — argue for extension and standardisation, not scepticism. The deepest point for an HD answer: the reasons obligation quietly redistributes power, converting the citizen's position from supplicant to auditor; that is why it belongs with s 75(v) and merits review among the structural reforms rather than the procedural details.
Essay 13 — Judicial Review, Merits Review and the Ombudsman Compared
Covers: 2016 ("critically discuss how judicial review, merits review and the Ombudsman schemes compare in their ability to serve the goals of Australian administrative law"); 2018 ("do courts pay too much attention to who makes a decision and not enough to its content and impact?").

Contention. The three institutions are best evaluated as a portfolio answering three different questions — was it lawful (courts), was it correct or preferable (merits review), was it good administration (Ombudsman) — and the system's genius is the portfolio, not any single instrument. On the 2018 variant: the courts' attentiveness to who decides is not formalism but the enforcement of Parliament's allocation of power; the attention to content and impact belongs, by design, to the other two institutions.

The comparison. Judicial review serves legality and the rule of law: entrenched ( S157; Kirk ), coercive ( Bhardwaj (Sem 11 s12–13) nullity; certiorari, mandamus), but bounded by Quin (Sem 21 s9) , expensive, standing-gated ( ACF (Sem 9 s5–6); Onus (Sem 9 s6–8); Argos (Sem 9 s12–13) ) and remedially circular (remittal to the same repository). Merits review serves correctness: the tribunal stands in the shoes of the decision-maker and decides the "correct or preferable" decision on the material before it ( Drake (Sem 5 s9–13) ; Shi (Sem 5 s14–15) — including material as at the time of review), unconstrained by the legality boundary, able to re-weigh, substitute and apply (though not be bound by) government policy ( Drake (No 2) ) — but it exists only where Parliament confers it, its members lack judicial tenure, and Frugtniet (Sem 5–6) shows its boundaries are themselves legally policed. The Ombudsman serves administrative justice and systemic improvement: broadest grounds (unjust, oppressive, simply "wrong"), zero cost, own-motion and systemic capacity — but recommendatory only (Essay 6). Measured against the goals — lawfulness, accountability, participation, transparency, efficiency and individual justice — each instrument is strongest where the others are weakest: the courts on lawfulness and finality; the tribunal on individual justice and content; the Ombudsman on transparency, accessibility and systemic accountability.

The 2018 charge — too much "who", not enough "what". The charge has surface force: a decision by an unauthorised delegate is void however impeccable its content (the delegation cases — Carltona (Sem 15); Pattenden (Sem 16 s7–8) ), while a harsh but authorised decision stands ( Quin ). But the focus on "who" is principled: the first question of public law is whether this repository was given this power — Parliament's allocation, which review exists to enforce ( LPDT (Sem 11–12) 's definition of jurisdictional error is precisely about the conferral). And the premise is false as stated: content is reviewed, through reasonableness ( Li ), rationality ( SZMDS (Sem 20 s7–11) ), considerations ( Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16) ) and materiality's outcome-sensitivity ( LPDT ) — what is excluded is not content but preference . Where pure content-correction is wanted, the system provides it — in the tribunal, deliberately outside Ch III.
Preferred position. The preferred contention is "complementarity by design": the comparison is not a contest, and critiques of each institution typically describe the boundary with its neighbour. The system's real weaknesses are the seams — gaps in merits-review coverage (conferral is discretionary with Parliament), Ombudsman non-enforceability, judicial review's cost — and reform should target the seams rather than re-engineer any single institution.

Variants. For 2016, give each institution one paragraph against the goals of administrative law, then the seams point. For 2018 ("who vs what"), lead with the charge and the delegation cases, then the answer that content review exists (Li; SZMDS; Peko) and preference belongs to the tribunal. A 2024 merits-review question appears in the compiled list without text; this essay and Essay 6 together supply the material for any MR-focused phrasing.
Essay 14 — Standing, Justiciability and Access to Review
Covers: 2018 ("standing and justiciability limits are difficult to reconcile with the principles underpinning administrative law"); 2017 ("complexities and technicalities... detract from administrative law's overall purpose").
Contention. The tension is real but rationally managed: standing rules serve legitimate functions (cases, not causes; concrete adversarial contests), yet the special-interest test discounts precisely the public and communal interests administrative law exists to protect. The law has answered pragmatically — generous statutory tests, flexible application — and the residual incoherence lies mainly in the doctrine's justifications , not its outcomes.

The case that the limits are irreconcilable. Administrative law's animating principle is that public power must be exercised lawfully — yet standing makes enforcement contingent on a private stake: a "special interest", more than "a mere intellectual or emotional concern" ( ACF (Sem 9 s5–6) ), more than a belief however strongly held that the law should be observed (Gibbs J). The result is structural under-enforcement: diffuse unlawfulness with no specially affected individual — environmental harm being the canonical case ( ACF itself) — escapes review entirely; the "right to speak and influence opinion" is expressly not standing ( Right to Life (Sem 9 s9–10) ); associations stand no higher than members ( ACF ). Justiciability doctrines add a second filter for high policy and non-statutory power. The 2017 charge follows: a body of gateway technicality (special interest; "person aggrieved"; "decision... under an enactment"; "tribunal" under the ALA) that consumes argument before lawfulness is ever reached.

The case that they are reconcilable. The limits express, rather than betray, system principles. Adversarial adjudication requires a concrete controversy ("matter"); unrestricted standing would conscript courts into abstract supervision and invite well-resourced ideological litigation — the floodgates and "busybody" concerns are about institutional role, not convenience. And the doctrine flexes where it matters: Onus (Sem 9 s6–8) found standing in cultural and custodial connection ("specially affected, though not necessarily uniquely affected" — Brennan J); Argos (Sem 9 s12–13) read "person aggrieved" by practical effect on business interests, refusing to confine standing by the enabling Act's policy; Bateman's Bay (Sem 9 s14) recognised commercial-competitor standing in equity and counselled a "skeptical" eye on restrictive approaches; for the writs, even a stranger may be heard, the control lying in the court's remedial discretion, which strengthens against strangers ( Aala (Sem 9 s6) ); and the AG's relator role plus statutory open-standing provisions, conferred in some regulatory schemes to enable broader public-interest enforcement, patch the gap. Justiciability, likewise, has narrowed rather than expanded.

Preferred position. The reconciliation succeeds in practice and fails in theory: outcomes are defensible case by case, but the special-interest test still lacks a principled account of why a material stake should be the price of enforcing public legality — Bateman's Bay 's own scepticism is the High Court conceding as much. The preferred reform direction is statutory: open or broadened standing where Parliament wants full enforcement, retaining judicial discretion as to remedy (the Aala model) to manage the floodgates concern. For the 2017 phrasing, concede the technicality critique for the gateways (standing; ADJR/ALA thresholds — Essay 11) but distinguish the grounds themselves, whose "complexity" is the necessary price of confining power by law.
Essay 15 — Apprehended Bias: Realism and the Fair-Minded Observer
Covers: 2017 (Kirby J in Jia (Sem 15 s7–9) — public law "robust and effective precisely because it is grounded in realism").
Contention. Kirby J's realism is the right sensibility but the wrong rule-generator standing alone: the Ebner (Sem 15 s10–12) framework already institutionalises realism through the fair-minded lay observer, while Jia 's majority shows why ministerial decision-makers cannot be held to judicial standards of detachment. The preferred position is realist doctrine, applied with contextual calibration — which is, in substance, what the law now is.

The case for Kirby J's realism. Bias doctrine protects appearances because public confidence is its currency; an "unworldly" court that ignores how influence actually operates protects neither. Realism explains the doctrine's best features: the observer test asks what might reasonably be apprehended , not what occurred ( Ebner ) — a sociological, not psychological, inquiry; the two-step (identify the factor; articulate its logical connection to deviation) forces courts to reason openly about real-world influence rather than intone conclusions; and the doctrine has tracked social change — from pecuniary interests ( Ebner itself) to a Minister's radio commentary ( Jia ) to prejudgment on social media ( Hendry (Sem 15 s13) ). Kirby J's dissent in Jia presses realism against power: ministers who publicly announce conclusions about the very persons before them have, in any worldly sense, prejudged.

The case for the majority's caution. Realism cuts the other way for political decision-makers. The Jia majority's point is institutional: a Minister is not a judge — accountable to Parliament, entitled and expected to hold and express policy views; the standard is whether the mind was "incapable of alteration", not whether it was empty. Demanding judicial blankness of elected officials would be its own unworldliness, disabling the very accountability (public explanation of policy) that democracy requires. Ebner itself warns that application outside courts "must sometimes recognise and accommodate differences" — calibration, not a single standard. And appearance-based doctrine has costs realism must own: it can be weaponised tactically, and necessity sometimes requires an interested decider to act.

Preferred position. The synthesis: realism supplies the method (the informed, fair-minded observer who knows how government actually works), institutional context supplies the calibration (judge, tribunal member, official, minister — descending expectations of detachment, rising tolerance of policy commitment), and the line is crossed where commitment becomes closure ( Jia 's "incapable of alteration" for actual bias; Ebner 's real-possibility apprehension for the appearance). On the Jia facts the majority's institutional point is persuasive; on facts like a decision-maker's published condemnation of a specific applicant ( Hendry -style), Kirby J's realism prevails within orthodox doctrine. The deepest realist point is Kirby J's own: doctrine retains authority only while its picture of influence matches the world — which is why the social-media cases matter.
Essay 16 — Privative Clauses, Outsourcing and the Reach of Supervision
Covers: 2017 ("privative clauses... reveal that supervisory jurisdiction will always be available"); 2014 ("how successfully do S157 (Sem 23 s8–10) and Kirk (Sem 23 s11–12) resolve the tensions"); 2014 ("privatisation and outsourcing have diminished the courts' ability to supervise public power").

Contention. S157 and Kirk settle that a constitutional minimum of supervision is ineradicable — the 2017 proposition is essentially correct — but the "resolution" is a boundary-shifting one (everything now turns on the jurisdictional/non-jurisdictional line), and the real modern escape from supervision is not the privative clause but the contract: outsourcing succeeds where ouster clauses failed.

The case for the propositions. Plaintiff S157 (Sem 23 s8–10) construed the Migration Act's privative clause so that a "decision... under this Act" does not include a purported decision affected by jurisdictional error: the clause and the grounds are reconciled, never collided, and s 75(v) review "cannot be removed by or under a law of the Parliament". Kirk extended the guarantee to the States: supervisory jurisdiction for jurisdictional error is a "defining characteristic" of a Supreme Court, beyond legislative removal. Graham (Sem 23 s13–15) generalised the principle: Parliament cannot deny a court the ability to enforce the limits of an officer's power — including by evidentiary blindfolds, not just express ouster. Together they vindicate the 2017 thesis: the harder Parliament tries, the more clearly the entrenched core appears; the Hickman (Sem 23 s9) provisos (bona fide attempt; relation to subject matter; reasonable reference to power) survive only as construction tools within that frame.

The case against — the unresolved tensions. Three. First, the settlement is candidly fictional: clauses are "read down" to mean almost nothing, honouring parliamentary supremacy in form while defeating it in substance — a tension managed , not resolved. Secondly, the load-bearing line — jurisdictional versus non-jurisdictional error — is notoriously manipulable; Kirk itself declined to define it exhaustively, and a privative clause can validly protect non-jurisdictional error of law on the face of the record, so protection turns on a classification the courts control. Thirdly — the 2014 outsourcing point — the guarantee attaches to officers and statutory power : when government delivers functions through companies and contracts, there may be no officer of the Commonwealth (s 75(v)), no "decision under an enactment" ( Tang (Notes) ), and no s 38 "public authority" question ever reached — supervision lapses not because Parliament ousted it but because the form of power changed. Tang is the emblem: the more government acts commercially, the less administrative law sees.

Preferred position. The preferred view: S157 and Kirk resolve the privative-clause tension as well as it can be resolved within a system committed simultaneously to parliamentary sovereignty and the rule of law — by converting collision into construction and entrenching a judicially policed minimum. The honest residue is the classification problem, which is the price of the settlement. The outsourcing critique, by contrast, identifies a genuine and growing gap that the constitutional guarantee does not touch; its remedies are legislative (functional definitions like the Charter's s 4 "public authority", contractual transparency obligations, Ombudsman jurisdiction over contractors) rather than doctrinal. The supervisory jurisdiction is always available — but only where public power retains a public form.
Variants. For 2017, lead with the paradox (attempted ouster proving entrenchment); for 2014 (privative), organise around the two "competing principles" (sovereignty v rule of law) and assess the construction technique; for 2014 (privatisation), put Tang and s 75(v)'s "officer" requirement at the centre with the Charter's functional definition as the counter-model.
Essay 17 — The Rule of Law and the Control of Discretion (Kitto J in Ipec-Air)

Covers: 2021 (Kitto J in R v Anderson; Ex parte Ipec-Air (2021 question) Pty Ltd (1965) 113 CLR 177: a statutory discretion "is intended to be exercised according to the rules of reason and justice, not according to private opinion; according to law, and not humour, and within those limits within which an honest man, competent to discharge the duties of his office, ought to confine himself").
Contention. Kitto J's dictum is the rule of law rendered as a doctrine of discretion: it asserts that no statutory power is truly unfettered, because conferral itself implies limits of reason, purpose and honesty. The modern grounds of review are, in substance, an elaboration of that single sentence — but the dictum also conceals the central difficulty, namely who decides what "reason and justice" require, and the modern law's answer (the legislature's implied intention, policed within stringent limits) honours Kitto J while disciplining him.

The case for the dictum as the law's foundation. Each phrase has become a ground. "According to the rules of reason": the legislature is taken to intend that a discretion be exercised reasonably ( Quin; Li; SZVFW (Sem 21 s9) ), and a decision lacking an evident and intelligible justification exceeds the implied limit ( Li (Sem 21 s9–14)); irrationality in the formation of a statutory state of satisfaction is jurisdictional ( SZMDS (Sem 20 s7–11) ). "Not according to private opinion": the rule against acting on personal interest or prejudgment is the bias doctrine ( Ebner (Sem 15 s10–12); Jia (Sem 15 s7–9) ), and acting at another's behest is dictation. "According to law, and not humour": improper purpose ( Toohey (Sem 17 s7–8) — power only for the purposes for which it is conferred, even where widely framed: Schlieske (Sem 19 s6) ), irrelevant considerations ( Peko-Wallsend (Sem 17 s14–16) ), and bad faith are all names for "humour" in office. "Within those limits within which an honest man... ought to confine himself": the implied conditions whose breach is jurisdictional error ( LPDT (Sem 11–12) ) — the modern, construction-based form of the same idea. The dictum also explains the system's deepest commitments: if conferral implies limits, then there can be no "island of power" immune from supervision — which is why review of jurisdictional error is entrenched against privative clauses ( Plaintiff S157 (Sem 23 s8–10); Kirk (Sem 23 s11–12); Graham (Sem 23 s13–15) : Parliament cannot deny the courts the ability to enforce the limits of an officer's power).

The case against — the dictum's concealed difficulties. First, indeterminacy: "reason and justice" supply a vocabulary, not a standard; without more, the dictum licenses judges to substitute their own opinion for the repository's — the very vice it condemns, merely relocated. The modern law's response concedes the problem: unreasonableness is "necessarily stringent" ( SZVFW ), weight is for the decision-maker ( Peko-Wallsend ), and the merits belong to the repository "and no further" ( Quin ). Secondly, source: Kitto J spoke as if the limits came from a general law of office-holding; the contemporary High Court insists they come from the particular statute, construed ( LPDT; Project Blue Sky (Sem 11 s9–11) ) — a more democratic but more contingent foundation, since what construction implies, drafting can (within constitutional limits) exclude. Thirdly, the dictum over-promises: "justice" is precisely what judicial review does not guarantee — a lawful decision may still be unjust ( Quin ; Essay 6's "lawful but wrong"), and the honest officer's limits do not include getting it right.

Preferred position. The dictum should be embraced as the normative core of the field, with the modern law read as its disciplined implementation rather than its dilution. The two qualifications work together: grounding the limits in statutory construction (rather than free-standing "reason and justice") answers the democratic objection by attributing the limits to Parliament; the stringency of the thresholds ( SZVFW; SZMDS ) answers the substitution objection by confining the courts to policing, not exercising, the discretion. What survives untouched is the dictum's central claim — that "unfettered discretion" is a contradiction in a legal system: every grant of power carries its limits within it, and the rule of law consists in courts being able, always ( S157; Kirk ), to hold the repository to them. Kitto J's honest officer is, in the end, the figure the whole apparatus of judicial review exists to presuppose — and, where necessary, to replace.
Work & QA Report

Process. The 33 questions in 'Essay questions.docx' were grouped into 17 themes; one full model essay was written per theme using the required method (contention; case for; case against; preferred position), with variant notes adapting each essay to the specific years' phrasings. Authorities were drawn from the course knowledge bases (review notes, structure document, seminar slides 1–6 and 8–24, and the prior hypothetical work).

QA cycle. An examiner-level QA agent reviewed the full set against (a) question coverage, (b) the required method, (c) legal accuracy of every case proposition, (d) HD-level depth (presence of the high-value doctrinal moves), and (e) exam-realistic length. Verdict: REWORK, with these required fixes — all implemented: a new Essay 17 for the uncovered 2021 Kitto/Ipec-Air rule-of-law question; removal of a garbled reference in the Li essay; correction of the 'eye keenly attuned' attribution to Wu Shan Liang (adopting Pozzolanic, restated in Plaintiff M1); and alignment of the LPDT pin-cite to the course's [4]–[6]. Eight recommended enhancements were also implemented (Hot Holdings; EPBC s 487; Ombudsman Act s 15; Charter s 39(3); Boilermakers; the Quin dictum; variant notes for Essays 9 and 13). One QA suggestion was overridden with better information: the reviewer queried Pattenden, which is in fact the course's Seminar 16 delegation authority and was retained.
Verification. A second QA pass confirmed all fixes landed, the new essay is complete and methodologically compliant, and formatting is intact. Final verdict: PASS at High Distinction standard . Coverage: 32 of 33 questions fully covered; the remaining entry (“2024 MR”) has no question text in the compiled document and is flagged for the user to check.

Citation-compliance audit. Because the exam permits citing only cases that appear in the course slides or notes, an allow-list of every case in the 23 available seminar decks and the two notes documents was compiled, and every authority in every essay was audited against it. Three out-of-syllabus authorities introduced as enrichment were removed and replaced with permitted equivalents — Wu Shan Liang and Pozzolanic (the reasons-restraint point now rests on Plaintiff M1 and Peko-Wallsend), and Boilermakers (the Chapter III point now rests on Plaintiff S157 and Kirk) — and two out-of-syllabus statutory pinpoints were generalised (an EPBC open-standing reference; the Ombudsman Act section number). A final independent audit confirmed every one of the 51 distinct cases cited is on the allow-list, the three prohibited cases are absent, and all statutes are within the syllabus. The only authority cited that is not in the slides or notes is R v Anderson; Ex parte Ipec-Air, which is permitted because it is the case quoted in the 2021 exam question itself, and it is confined to Essay 17.

Slide pinpoint pass (v3). Concise shorthand slide pinpoints were added inline at the first mention of each case in each essay — e.g. (Sem 21 s9) — mapping every authority to where it is taught in the seminar decks, or (Notes) for cases that appear only in the student notes, or (2021 question) for the single case quoted in an exam question (Ipec-Air, Essay 17). A verified case→slide index was built by scanning all 23 available decks, and an independent QA pass confirmed all ~50 distinct case→pinpoint mappings are accurate against the slides (no Seminar 7 reference is used, as that deck is missing from the folder). The Charter ‘proper consideration’ passage in Essay 5 was also re-pinpointed at sentence level, correcting the lead attribution so that the ‘higher than common law’ holding is carried by Thompson v Minogue (Sem 18 s17), the two-limb structure by Tate JA in Bare (Sem 18 s8), and the four-limb HJ test by Thompson (Sem 18 s18).

ADMINISTRATIVE LAW — UNIFIED ANSWER FRAMEWORK
Combining Merits Review and Judicial Review in one approach
Triage · two IRAC templates · strategic interplay · comparison table
Prepared for Raymond · 8 June 2026 · Authorities drawn from the seminar slides and notes
How to Use This Framework
An administrative-law problem usually presents a client unhappy with a government decision. There are two principal court/tribunal avenues, and they ask different questions :
Merits review (MR): ‘What is the correct or preferable decision?’ A tribunal (VCAT in Victoria; the ART federally) stands in the shoes of the decision-maker and remakes the decision afresh on the best and most current evidence, applying the same substantive statutory criteria that bound the original decision-maker. It can affirm, vary or set aside and substitute its own decision.
Judicial review (JR): ‘Was the decision lawful ?’ A court reviews only the legality of the exercise of power (the grounds of review). It cannot substitute its own decision on the merits; it quashes and remits.

Because they are alternative — and sometimes sequential — responses to the same decision, a complete answer often has to address both . This framework gives you: (1) a triage to choose the avenue; (2) the MR answer template; (3) the JR answer template; (4) the strategic interplay between them; and (5) a one-page comparison table. The two templates can be run independently or stitched together in a single advice.
Part 1 — Triage: Which Avenue (or Both)?
Work through these questions first; they determine the structure of the answer.
What does the client want? A different outcome (the licence granted, the debt cancelled) points to merits review , because only MR can substitute the correct or preferable decision. A finding that the decision was unlawful / made without power (or there is no merits-review right) points to judicial review .
Is merits review available? MR is not inherent — it exists only where an enabling Act expressly confers it on the tribunal (VCAT Act 1998 (Vic) s 42(1) (Sem 5 s7); federally, the ART Act). Identify the enabling Act, the provision conferring review, the standing provision and the time limit . If no enabling Act confers review, MR is unavailable and JR (or the Ombudsman) is the only path.

Is judicial review available, and in which forum? JR is always available for jurisdictional error and cannot be ousted (Supreme Court of Victoria’s entrenched supervisory jurisdiction — *Kirk* (Sem 23 s11–12); federally s 75(v) — *Plaintiff S157* (Sem 23 s8–10)). Choose the forum (Vic: Supreme Court under Order 56 / ALA; federal: s 39B Judiciary Act / ADJR Act).
Sequence and interplay. Where both are available, MR is usually pursued first (cheaper, no standing/UPL hurdles, can fix the merits, and a fresh decision may make JR unnecessary). JR is held in reserve for legality defects, for when MR is unavailable or exhausted, or to challenge the tribunal’s own decision (a tribunal’s MR decision is itself amenable to JR). A statutory reasons request (ADJR s 13; ALA s 8 in Vic) is often the first step in either path.
Drafting tip: if the problem asks ‘advise the client’, address the avenue the client’s goal requires first, then note the alternative. If it asks two discrete questions (e.g. ‘can X apply for review’ and ‘what are X’s prospects’), answer in that order — exactly the MR template below.
Part 2 — Merits Review Answer Template
Structure MR as two issues: (a) Can the client apply? (jurisdiction + standing) and (b) What are the prospects? (nature/function of MR, then application of the substantive statutory criteria, then remedies).

Issue (a) — Can the client apply for merits review?
Jurisdiction
Rule
VCAT has review jurisdiction only where an enabling Act expressly confers it: VCAT Act 1998 (Vic) s 42(1) (Sem 5 s7). Identify the enabling Act’s review provision and its time limit (commonly 28 days from the decision), reinforced by VCAT Act s 48 (when jurisdiction is invoked). VCAT may extend a time limit (VCAT Act s 126(1)).
Application
Confirm the decision is one the enabling Act makes reviewable, identify the decision date, and check the applicant is within time (or can seek an extension).
Standing
Rule
VCAT Act s 48(a) allows a person to apply if entitled to do so by the enabling enactment; the enabling Act’s review provision usually defines who may apply (e.g. ‘a person whose application for a licence is refused may apply to VCAT’).
Application
Show the applicant falls within that class (and satisfies any threshold formalities — age, form, fee).
Conclusion
State whether the applicant may bring the application, subject to the time limit (and the s 126(1) extension fallback).
Issue (b) — What are the prospects of success?
Nature and function of merits review

Merits review is performed by a tribunal in the executive branch (not an exercise of judicial power). The tribunal stands in the shoes of the original decision-maker and determines the correct or preferable decision on the material before it (*Drake v Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs (No 1)* (Sem 5 s9); *Re Drake (No 2)* (Sem 5 s12–13)), codified in VCAT Act s 51(1) . It decides on the best and most current evidence available at the time of review (*Shi v Migration Agents Registration Authority* (Sem 5 s14–15)).
Power and function
The tribunal exercises the powers and functions conferred by the VCAT Act and the enabling Act, and remakes the decision afresh applying the same statutory criteria that bound the original decision-maker — it is ‘not at large’ and is ‘subject to the same general constraints as the original decision-maker’ (*Frugtniet v Australian Securities and Investments Commission* (Sem 5 s16–17; Sem 6 s5–6)). It is therefore the substantive grant/refusal provision of the enabling Act that the tribunal must apply directly.
Applying the substantive statutory criteria (the heart of the answer)
Rule

Set out the operative grant/refusal provision and any defined terms (e.g. ‘fit and proper person’ and the disqualification criteria). This is a statutory-construction exercise: give the provision its meaning by text, context and purpose , adopting the interpretation that best achieves the statutory purpose (Interpretation of Legislation Act 1984 (Vic) s 35(a)); extrinsic materials may be consulted.
Application
Apply each limb of the criteria to the facts. For each contested criterion, argue it both ways — the case for the client, the counter-argument , and the better view — and identify where further inquiry is needed (the tribunal decides on the best current evidence, so flag gaps). Because the tribunal applies the same criteria as the decision-maker, the analysis tracks the statute, not the grounds of review.
Conclusion / remedies
State the orders available on review — affirm, vary, or set aside and substitute (VCAT Act s 51(2) ) — and, drawing on the enabling Act’s substantive powers, conclude whether the tribunal is likely to remake the decision in the client’s favour (e.g. grant the licence).
Part 3 — Judicial Review Answer Template
Run JR in the established order (this mirrors the full hypothetical framework already prepared; condensed here):

Jurisdiction & avenue — Vic: Supreme Court inherent supervisory jurisdiction, accessed by Order 56 (60-day limit) or the Administrative Law Act 1978 (Vic) (30-day limit; s 8 reasons; s 10 record); the jurisdiction is entrenched and cannot be ousted (*Kirk*; s 85 Constitution Act 1975 (Vic)). Federally: s 39B Judiciary Act / ADJR Act ; s 75(v) in the High Court (*Plaintiff S157*).
Remedies — certiorari, mandamus, prohibition (O 56); injunction and declaration in the inherent jurisdiction; the court cannot substitute the decision.
Standing — sufficient interest/special interest (*ACF*; *Onus*; *Argos*); ‘person aggrieved’ under the ADJR Act.
Grounds of review — without authority/error of law; improper purpose; relevant/irrelevant considerations; invalid delegation; procedural fairness (hearing rule; bias); objective/subjective jurisdictional fact; no evidence; unreasonableness; unlawful or inflexibly applied policy; ultra vires delegated legislation; (Vic) Charter s 38. Argue each ground for and against, then a balanced view.
Breach & consequences — the two-stage analysis (Project Blue Sky purpose test; LPDT materiality; bias/unreasonableness jurisdictional irrespective of materiality; Bhardwaj — no decision at all).
Remedies (Part II) & strongest ground — likely availability; identify the single strongest ground for the full consequences analysis.

Privative clause (if any) — read down so as not to protect jurisdictional error (*Hickman*/*Plaintiff S157*; *Kirk*; *Graham*).
Key contrast to keep visible: JR never asks whether the decision was *right*; only whether it was *lawful*. If the client’s goal is a different outcome, JR alone is insufficient — it can only send the decision back to be remade.
Part 4 — The Strategic Interplay (combining the two)
MR first where available. It is cheaper, has no adverse-costs/UPL barriers, allows full reconsideration on current evidence (*Shi*), and can deliver the outcome the client actually wants — often making JR unnecessary.
JR where MR is unavailable or the complaint is about legality. No enabling Act conferring review → JR (or Ombudsman). A decision affected by jurisdictional error → JR, because no amount of merits reconsideration cures an unlawful exercise of power.
JR of the tribunal’s MR decision. The tribunal’s decision is itself an exercise of statutory power and is amenable to JR for jurisdictional error (e.g. the tribunal misconstrued the criteria, denied procedural fairness, or strayed beyond the ‘same constraints’ limit in *Frugtniet*). So MR and JR can run in sequence on the same matter.
Shared first step — reasons. A statutory reasons request (ADJR s 13; ALA s 8 in Vic) informs the choice of avenue and strengthens either path.

Forum and cost. MR is low/no-fee and SRL-friendly; JR carries court fees and adverse-costs risk. This usually makes MR the rational first move for an individual.
One combined advice. Where a problem invites both, structure as: (1) triage; (2) MR (can they apply? prospects?); (3) JR (is the decision lawful? what relief?); (4) recommendation on sequence. Flag clearly when you switch from the merits question to the legality question — examiners reward the candidate who keeps the two analyses distinct but shows how they fit together.
Part 5 — Merits Review vs Judicial Review at a Glance
Feature
Merits Review (MR)
Judicial Review (JR)
Question asked
Correct or preferable decision?
Was the decision lawful?
Decision-maker
Tribunal (VCAT / ART) — executive
Court (Supreme Court / Federal Court / HCA)
Source of power
Enabling Act must expressly confer it (VCAT Act s 42(1))
Inherent/constitutional; cannot be ousted (Kirk; s 75(v))
What it applies
Same substantive statutory criteria as the decision-maker (Frugtniet)
Grounds of review (legality limits only)
Evidence
Best and most current, at time of review (Shi)
Generally the record before the decision-maker
Powers / outcome
Affirm, vary, or set aside and substitute (VCAT Act s 51(2))
Quash and remit; cannot substitute the decision
Standing
As defined by the enabling Act (VCAT Act s 48)
Sufficient/special interest (ACF; Onus; Argos)

Cost / access
Low or no fee; self-rep friendly
Court fees + adverse-costs risk
Leading cases
Drake (No 1 & 2); Shi; Frugtniet
Kirk; S157; Project Blue Sky; LPDT; Li; Kioa; etc.
Bottom line: choose by the client’s goal — MR to change the outcome, JR to challenge the legality — and, where both are open, run MR first and keep JR in reserve, including against the tribunal’s own decision.

Part A — The Hypothetical
FACTS
Background

Priya Anand is a long-standing and well-regarded breeder of pedigree Burmese cats. She operates "Lotus Cattery" from her home in the district of the Whitestone City Council ("the Council"), a local government in Victoria. The Council administers the Cat Act 2005 (Vic) ("the Act") within its district. Ms Anand has bred cats for over fifteen years, has won numerous awards, and has never been the subject of any adverse animal-welfare finding. She has held an approval to breed cats under the Act since 2018, renewed without incident in 2021 and 2023.
In 2025, the Council became concerned about the size of the cat population in its district. At a public council meeting in October 2025, Ms Anand spoke forcefully against the Council's proposed overnight "cat curfew", describing it as "ill-informed and cruel", and she repeated those criticisms in an interview with the local newspaper, the Whitestone Gazette. Councillor Marigold Sage, who had championed the curfew, was reported to be "furious" at Ms Anand's comments.
The Cat Regulations 2025 and the Council's policy

In December 2025, the Governor made the Cat Regulations 2025 (Vic) ("the Regulations") under s 55 of the Act. Regulation 12 provides that, for the purposes of s 34(2)(f) of the Act, a circumstance in which a local government may refuse to grant or renew an approval to breed cats is that "the applicant has, within the preceding two years, made public statements critical of the local government's animal-management policies". Regulation 8 provides that a local government "must not grant more than ten cat-breeding approvals in its district in any calendar year". Regulation 15 provides that, where the holder of an approval applies for its renewal before the approval expires, the existing approval "continues in force until the application for renewal is determined".
In January 2026, the Council adopted the "Whitestone Responsible Cat Ownership Policy 2025" ("the Policy"). The Policy states that the Council "will not grant or renew any approval to breed cats while the district's registered cat population exceeds the target set by the Council from time to time". At all relevant times the population exceeded that target.
The renewal application

Ms Anand's 2023 approval was due to expire on 31 March 2026. On 2 February 2026 — before that expiry — she applied under s 33 of the Act to the Council for the renewal of her approval to breed cats; by reason of regulation 15, her existing approval continued in force pending determination of that application. Her application enclosed: (a) a detailed description of her breeding facilities and premises; (b) references from two veterinarians; and (c) an independent assessment of Lotus Cattery prepared by Dr Lim, a specialist feline veterinarian, which concluded that the cattery's facilities and premises were "exemplary" and "among the best in the State".
The decision-maker
By a resolution duly passed by an absolute majority and recorded in writing, the Council delegated its functions under the Act to its Chief Executive Officer, Dana Whitlock. The Council's delegation register records that delegation, and no instrument of delegation to any other officer exists. Ms Whitlock did not herself deal with Ms Anand's application. Instead she instructed Trevor Boyle, the Council's Animal Management Coordinator — who is not the Council's CEO — to deal with cat-breeding applications, and Mr Boyle made the decision described below.

Mr Boyle's sister, Helen Boyle, operates a competing cattery, "Regal Burmese", in the same district; Helen Boyle stood to gain commercially from the closure of Lotus Cattery. Mr Boyle had personally received and investigated a series of neighbour complaints about Ms Anand earlier in 2025. In November 2025, on a community social-media application called "PawPrint", Mr Boyle posted (under his own name) that "people who put profit before animals, like the Lotus Cattery operator, should never be allowed to breed animals in this city". On 1 May 2026, Councillor Sage emailed Mr Boyle stating that "the Anand application must be refused"; Mr Boyle's file note of that day records that he "would do as the Councillor required".
The investigation and the decision
After receiving Ms Anand's application, Mr Boyle did not read Dr Lim's assessment, the veterinary references, or the description of the facilities. He conducted an internet search and found several anonymous posts on PawPrint alleging that Ms Anand "hoards dozens of cats in squalor". The allegations were untrue. Without putting any of this material to Ms Anand, or telling her that her application was in jeopardy, Mr Boyle decided to refuse the renewal and, at the same time, to cancel Ms Anand's existing (continuing) approval.

On 15 May 2026 the Council (by Mr Boyle) refused to renew, and cancelled, Ms Anand's approval to breed cats. On 20 May 2026 the Council gave Ms Anand written notice under s 37 of the Act setting out the following reasons:
"Having considered the application by Ms Priya Anand for the renewal of an approval to breed cats, the Council has decided to refuse the renewal and to cancel her existing approval, for the following reasons:
1. I am satisfied that Ms Anand is not a fit and proper person to breed cats.
2. I am satisfied that Ms Anand was convicted within the previous three years of an offence against the Animal Welfare Act 2002.
3. Ms Anand has, within the preceding two years, made public statements critical of the Council's animal-management policies.
4. It is the Council's firm position that no further breeding approvals will be granted while the district's cat population exceeds target.
5. The district already has a sufficient number of cat breeders.
— T. Boyle, Animal Management Coordinator, for the Whitestone City Council"
In fact, Ms Anand's only relevant record is a single infringement notice she received in 2019 (more than three years before the decision) under separate companion-animal legislation; she has never been convicted of any offence against the Act, the Dog Act 1976 or the Animal Welfare Act 2002.
Seizure and threatened destruction

On 22 May 2026, a police officer (an "authorised person" under the Act), acting under a warrant to seize issued under s 50, seized four of Ms Anand's breeding cats from her home, and they were impounded at the Council's cat management facility (a facility operated by the Council). The Council, as operator of that facility, has given notice that, unless restrained, it intends under s 31(2) of the Act to have Ms Anand's prize stud cat, "Maximus", destroyed on the basis that he is "dangerous". The notice does not allege that Maximus has caused, or is likely to cause, serious injury or illness to any person or animal. Maximus is a docile, award-winning show cat who has never injured any person or animal. Friends of Felines Incorporated, an incorporated animal-welfare association that campaigns for the humane treatment of cats and has a long record of advocacy, rescue and rehoming work in the Whitestone district, also wishes to challenge the threatened destruction.
Instructions

You are a solicitor in a large Victorian law firm. Ms Anand has retained your firm to advise on her prospects of success in an application for judicial review, to be brought in the Supreme Court of Victoria, challenging the Council's decision of 15 May 2026 to refuse and cancel her approval to breed cats, and the Council's threatened destruction of Maximus. Your firm will file the originating motion in the week beginning 22 June 2026. You have become aware of all of the matters set out above and may rely on them. Your managing partner asks you to provide an internal memorandum that:
- states the remedies that should be sought in the application;
- confirms whether Ms Anand (and, separately, Friends of Felines Inc) would have standing to bring the proceeding;
- states the grounds upon which the specified remedies may be sought, and whether any one of those grounds will be sufficient to obtain the relief sought; and
- addresses how Ms Anand could access the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of Victoria, and whether the Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities Act 2006 (Vic) provides any additional ground.
Question 2
How, if at all, would your answer to Question 1 differ if the following provision were included in the Cat Act 2005 (Vic)?
57 Validity of decisions

A decision of a local government under this Act is final and conclusive, and must not be challenged, appealed against, reviewed, quashed or called into question in any court, and is not subject to prohibition, mandamus, injunction, declaration or certiorari, on any ground.
QUESTION 1 — MEMORANDUM OF ADVICE

This memorandum advises Ms Priya Anand on her prospects of success in an application for judicial review in the Supreme Court of Victoria challenging the Whitestone City Council's decision of 15 May 2026 to refuse to renew, and to cancel, her approval to breed cats under the Cat Act 2005 (Vic) ("the Act"), the seizure of her four breeding cats under a s 50 warrant, and the Council's threatened destruction of her cat "Maximus" under s 31(2). It addresses, in order: (1) jurisdiction and the avenues for review; (2) the remedies to be sought; (3) standing (for Ms Anand and for Friends of Felines Inc); (4) the grounds of review, argued for and against, with a balanced assessment of each; (5) a comparative assessment identifying the strongest ground(s) — placed front and centre, before the breach analysis, because the choice of lead ground drives the breach, materiality and relief analysis that follows; (6) breach and the consequences of breach; (7) the likely availability of the remedies; and (8) an overall conclusion. The Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities Act 2006 (Vic) is addressed as a discrete ground at 4.13. Question 2 (the effect of a privative clause) is addressed at the end.
1. Jurisdiction and Avenues for Review
Issue
In which court, and by which procedure, may Ms Anand bring a judicial review application, and is she within time?
Rule

The aim of a judicial review application is to establish that the decision-maker has breached the limits of the power conferred on it. In Victoria Supreme Court of Victoria has inherent supervisory jurisdiction. The Court's jurisdiction to review for jurisdictional error, exercised through relief in the nature of certiorari, mandamus and prohibition, is an inherent jurisdiction and a "defining characteristic" of a State Supreme Court that cannot be removed by legislation: Kirk v Industrial Court (NSW) (2010) 239 CLR 531 ; it is recognised in s 85 of the Constitution Act 1975 (Vic) . There are two avenues below, which cannot be joined :
Order 56: It is accessed (not conferred) by the Supreme Court Act 1986 (Vic) and Order 56 of the Supreme Court (General Civil Procedure) Rules 2015 (Vic) . Under O 56.01, relief in the nature of certiorari, mandamus and prohibition is obtained by way of judgment or order in a proceeding commenced by originating motion; the Court may also grant injunctions and declarations in its inherent jurisdiction. The limitation period is 60 days from the date when the grounds for relief first arose.

Administrative Law Act 1978 (Vic) ("ALA"). Section 3 confers a right of review on "any person affected by a decision of a tribunal ." "Decision" (s 2) means a decision operating in law to determine a question affecting rights or to grant, deny or alter a licence; it must be operative and have a quality of finality, but extends to decisions not necessarily immediately binding and enforceable ( Australian Broadcasting Tribunal v Bond (1990) 170 CLR 321; Masters v McCubbery [1996] 1 VR 635 ). "Tribunal" (s 2) means a person or body required, in arriving at the decision, to act judicially to the extent of observing one or more of the rules of natural justice. The ALA confers a statutory mechanism to obtain reasons (s 8) and, by s 10 , deems any reasons given to form part of the record (enlarging the record so that error of law on its face is reviewable). The limitation period is 30 days .
Application
The decision-maker is the Whitestone City Council, a local government administering the Act (s 39). Its decision to refuse and cancel Ms Anand's breeding approval is an exercise of statutory power under ss 34–35, and its threatened destruction of Maximus is an exercise of power under s 31(2); each gives rise to a justiciable controversy. The Supreme Court has inherent supervisory jurisdiction over both.

As to the avenue : the Order 56 route is the appropriate and available avenue. Ms Anand's grounds are predominantly grounds of jurisdictional error (below), which is the heartland of the Court's inherent supervisory jurisdiction. The application will be filed in the week beginning 22 June 2026 — within the 60-day O 56 period (which expires in mid-July 2026, the decision having been made on 15 May 2026). By contrast, the ALA's 30-day period (running from, at latest, the s 37 reasons of 20 May 2026) will already have expired by the proposed filing date, so the ALA avenue is, on the present timetable, time-barred . This does not prejudice Ms Anand: the Order 56 route remains open, and is in any event preferable because it engages the full supervisory jurisdiction and is not confined to a "decision of a tribunal." Were the ALA still available, the s 8 reasons mechanism and the s 10 enlargement of the record would be useful (particularly for the error-of-law-on-the-record argument), but the Council has in fact already given reasons under s 37 of the Act, so little is lost.
Conclusion

Ms Anand should proceed in the Supreme Court of Victoria under Order 56 , invoking the Court's inherent supervisory jurisdiction ( Kirk ; s 85 Constitution Act 1975 (Vic)), within the 60-day period. The ALA avenue is, on the current timetable, out of time and in any event narrower; nothing turns on its loss.
2. Remedies (Part I)
Issue
What remedies should be sought in the originating motion?
Rule
In the Supreme Court of Victoria the following are available: relief in the nature of certiorari (to quash the legal effect of a decision: Ainsworth v Criminal Justice Commission (1992) 175 CLR 564 ; available only against an exercise of power with apparent legal effect ( Wingfoot Australia Partners Pty Ltd v Kocak (2013) 252 CLR 480 ) and correcting jurisdictional error and error of law on the face of the record ( Craig; Kirk )), mandamus (to compel performance of a public duty according to law) and prohibition (to restrain unlawful action), under O 56.01; and, in the Court's inherent jurisdiction, injunctions (mandatory or prohibitory; an injunction requires a legal right or sufficient interest: Bateman's Bay Local Aboriginal Land Council v Aboriginal Community Benefit Fund Pty Ltd (1998) 194 CLR 247 ) and declarations (requiring an actual, not hypothetical, controversy: City of Enfield v Development Assessment Commission (2000) 199 CLR 135 ).
Application
Ms Anand should seek:

Certiorari to quash the Council's decision of 15 May 2026 refusing to renew and cancelling her approval to breed cats.
Mandamus to compel the Council to determine her renewal application according to law (by a person with authority to decide, free of bias, and on the relevant statutory considerations).
A prohibitory injunction (and, if necessary, an urgent interlocutory injunction) to restrain the Council, as operator of the cat management facility, from destroying Maximus under s 31(2) pending the determination of the proceeding.
Relief in the nature of certiorari/prohibition in respect of the s 50 warrant, and a mandatory injunction or order for the return of all four seized cats to Ms Anand (the seizure having no valid foundation if she remains an approved breeder: 4.14).
Declarations that (a) the decision of 15 May 2026 (refusal and cancellation) is invalid and of no effect; (b) regulation 12 (and, so far as relied on, regulation 8) of the Cat Regulations 2025 (Vic) is invalid; and (c) the threatened destruction of Maximus would be unlawful.
Conclusion
Certiorari and mandamus (to quash the refusal and cancellation and compel lawful redetermination), relief in respect of the s 50 warrant with an order for the return of the four seized cats, a prohibitory injunction (with urgent interlocutory protection for Maximus), and the declarations above should all be sought.

3. Standing
Issue
Whether Ms Anand, and separately Friends of Felines Inc, have standing.
Rule
For relief in the nature of certiorari and prohibition , the applicant must show a sufficient effect on a legal right or interest as a condition precedent ( Hot Holdings Pty Ltd v Creasy (1996) 185 CLR 149; Ainsworth ); even a stranger may seek these (ACF), although the discretionary considerations against relief are correspondingly strengthened ( Aala ). Personal and business reputations are included in interest (Ainsworth).
For mandamus , the applicant must show a sufficient interest in the performance of a public duty owed ( Martin v Nalder [2016] WASC 138 ).
For equitable relief (injunction, declaration), the applicant must have a special interest in the subject matter — more than the interest of an ordinary member of the public, and more than a mere emotional or intellectual concern ( Australian Conservation Foundation Inc v Commonwealth (1980) 146 CLR 493 ("ACF"); Onus v Alcoa of Australia Ltd (1981) 149 CLR 27 ); the interest need not be financial, and the nature and subject matter of the litigation dictate what suffices ( Bateman's Bay; Argos Pty Ltd v Corbell (2014) 254 CLR 394 ).
Under the ALA , standing is conferred on "any person affected by a decision" (s 3) — a broad, enabling test.
Application

Ms Anand plainly has standing. She is the applicant for, and (by reason of reg 15) the continuing holder of, the approval; the refusal and cancellation directly destroy her legal right to breed cats and damage her reputation and livelihood (reputation is an "interest": Ainsworth ). She is also the owner of the cats seized and of Maximus, whose threatened destruction directly affects her property. She is a "person affected" for the ALA and has more than ample special interest for the equitable relief. There is no standing obstacle.

Friends of Felines Inc presents the more contestable question, relevant only to its challenge to the threatened destruction. Mere intellectual or emotional concern for animal welfare is insufficient ( ACF : a belief that the law should be observed does not confer standing). However, the association has a long record of advocacy, rescue and rehoming work in the Whitestone district , which is capable of constituting a special interest greater than that of an ordinary member of the public on the Onus / North Coast line (a curial assessment of the closeness of the litigant's relationship to the subject matter). The counter-argument is threefold: an organisation formed to advance a cause is, on ACF , in no stronger position than its members; a "right to speak and to influence opinions" cannot be transmuted into a right of standing, and the interest must not be remote, indirect or fanciful ( Right to Life Association (NSW) Inc v Secretary, Department of Human Services and Health (1995) 56 FCR 50 , Lockhart J); and FoF must show it is "specially affected, though not necessarily uniquely affected" ( Brennan J, Onus ) — which its concrete rescue and rehoming operations in the district, as distinct from advocacy alone, arguably establish. For the writs, even a stranger may apply, though the discretionary considerations are strengthened ( Aala ). On balance, Friends of Felines has a reasonable but not certain argument for standing in equity; the safer course is for Ms Anand (whose standing is unimpeachable) to be the applicant, with Friends of Felines joined or relegated to a supporting role.

Conclusion
Ms Anand has standing for all remedies sought. Friends of Felines Inc has an arguable special interest to challenge the destruction, but its standing is not assured; Ms Anand should be the principal applicant.
4. Grounds of Review
The grounds are set out below. For each, the case for the ground and the likely answer from the Council are stated, followed by a balanced assessment.

The statutory architecture repays close reading, and the contrast with s 10(2) is instructive. For cat registration , Parliament provided that a local government " must refuse... if, and only if " satisfied of a listed matter: satisfaction is both necessary and sufficient, and refusal is mandatory. For breeding approvals , s 34(2) provides only that the local government " may refuse... only if satisfied" of a listed ground. The words "only if" make the list exhaustive — satisfaction of a listed ground is a necessary condition of any refusal — but the "may" (deliberately, given the contrast with s 10(2)) preserves a residual discretion to grant even where a ground is made out . Parliament knew how to mandate refusal and chose not to. Three consequences follow: reasons 4 and 5 fail at the threshold (no listed ground); the Policy's blanket bar unlawfully converts a discretionary power into a mandatory one (4.11); and reg 8's command that a council "must not grant" is in tension with the discretionary structure of s 34 itself (4.12). Section 35 permits cancellation only if one of the s 34(2) matters "applies", and s 37 requires written reasons; the reasons given (set out in the facts) are the primary record against which the grounds are tested.
4.1 Acting Without Authority / Excess of Power
Issue

Whether the Council exercised the power on a basis outside the limits fixed by the Act.
Rule (ratio)
A statutory power may be exercised only within the scope conferred, ascertained by construing the Act as a whole; an exercise outside that scope is made without authority and is invalid ( ABC Developmental Learning Centres Pty Ltd v Secretary, Department of Human Services [2007] VSC 37; (2008) 15 VR 489 (Hollingworth J) — the authority underlying Seminar 3's "123 Jump" problem: officers' powers are confined to their statutory scope, and clear words are required before a power is read more widely). Where the empowering provision states an exhaustive set of permissible grounds, a decision resting on a ground outside the list is ultra vires.
Application

Section 34(2) is exhaustive (" only if ... one or more of the following "). Of the five reasons given, reason 5 ("the district already has a sufficient number of cat breeders") corresponds to no paragraph of s 34(2) and is therefore not a ground on which refusal was authorised at all. Reason 4 (the Council's "firm position" that no approvals will be granted while the population exceeds target) likewise finds no footing in s 34(2) and reflects an extraneous policy objective (it is also a fettering and an improper-purpose point: 4.11, 4.2). To the extent the decision rests on reasons 4 and 5, it was made without authority . Separately, the threatened destruction of Maximus exceeds s 31(2): that power arises only where the operator believes on reasonable grounds that the cat is "feral, diseased or dangerous" and has caused or is likely to cause serious injury or illness . The Council's notice alleges only that Maximus is "dangerous" and does not allege the second, conjunctive limb; the power is therefore unenlivened on the face of the notice.

The Council may answer that s 34(2) requires satisfaction of only "one or more" grounds, so that the decision can be supported by a valid ground (e.g. reason 1, "fit and proper") notwithstanding the invalid reasons 4–5. That answer fails if the surviving grounds are themselves vitiated (as argued below) and is, in any event, a materiality/severance question addressed at 5 and 6.
Conclusion
Reasons 4 and 5 are outside the exhaustive s 34(2) grounds and were given without authority; the s 31(2) destruction power is unenlivened on the face of the notice. This is a strong, clear ground, subject to the materiality/severance point.
4.1a Error of Law
Issue
Whether the Council misdirected itself in law in the course of exercising a power it held.
Rule (ratio)
Error of law arises where a decision-maker, while exercising a power it holds, makes a legal mistake — typically by misconstruing a statutory term, applying the wrong test, or misunderstanding the legal limits of the power. Where the error goes to the limits of the power it is jurisdictional; where it does not but is apparent on the face of the record it remains correctable by certiorari at common law ( Craig v South Australia (1995) 184 CLR 163; Kirk ). In Victoria the reasons given form part of the record (ALA s 10; and the s 37 reasons are in any event before the Court).
Application

At least three legal errors appear on the face of the s 37 reasons. First , "fit and proper person" (s 34(2)(e)) is undefined; if the Council applied an erroneous understanding of that test (treating untrue hoarding rumours and the applicant's public criticism as bearing on fitness), it misconstrued the statutory criterion. Second , reason 2 treats a 2019 infringement notice under unrelated legislation as a "conviction... within the previous three years of an offence against the Animal Welfare Act 2002" — a misconstruction of s 34(2)(d) (an infringement notice is not a conviction; 2019 is not within three years; and the legislation was not the Animal Welfare Act 2002). Third , reliance on reason 3 applies regulation 12, which is ultra vires (4.12), so the Council acted on a legally erroneous view of the applicable law. The Council can characterise some of these as factual rather than legal, but the construction of "fit and proper" and "conviction... within the previous three years" are questions of law.
Conclusion
The decision discloses error(s) of law on the face of the record; the conviction and "fit and proper" errors go to the limits of the power and are jurisdictional. Strong ground.
4.2 Improper Purpose
Issue
Whether the power was exercised substantially for a purpose other than those for which it was conferred.
Rule (ratio)

A power may be exercised only for the purposes for which it was conferred, inferred from the Act as a whole ( R v Toohey; Ex parte Northern Land Council (1981) 151 CLR 170 ); even a widely framed power must be exercised for the Act's purposes ( Schlieske v Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs (1988) 84 ALR 719 ); if a substantial purpose is improper, the exercise is invalid even if not the sole purpose ( Samrein Pty Ltd v Metropolitan Water Sewerage and Drainage Board (1982) 41 ALR 467 ); the onus is on the applicant and it is a question of fact.
Substantial means the power would not have been exercised had there not been a desire to achieve the unauthorized purpose (Samrein).
High Bar: An improper purpose will not be lightly inferred and, by application of a presumption of regularity, will only be inferred if the evidence cannot be reconciled with the proper exercise of the power.
Application

The Act's objects (s 3) are confined to identifying and recording cats and promoting responsible ownership. The facts suggest two improper purposes: (i) retaliation for Ms Anand's public criticism of the curfew (Cr Sage was "furious"; her email of 1 May directed refusal; reason 3 expressly penalises the criticism); and (ii) advancing the commercial interests of Mr Boyle's sister's competing cattery. Either, if a substantial purpose, vitiates the decision. The Council will respond that the operative reason was the (purported) statutory grounds and that motive cannot be inferred merely from Cr Sage's reaction; but the express reliance on the criticism (reason 3) and the Sage email make the improper purpose unusually well-evidenced for this ground.
Conclusion
There is a strong, well-evidenced improper-purpose case, though such grounds are always fact-sensitive. Best run together with bias (4.7) and irrelevant considerations (4.4).
4.3 Failure to Take into Account a Relevant Consideration
Issue
Whether the Council failed to consider a consideration it was bound to consider.
Rule (ratio)

The subject matter, scope and purpose of the Act may require a consideration to be taken into account ( Mason J, Minister for Aboriginal Affairs v Peko-Wallsend Ltd (1986) 162 CLR 24 ); error is established where the decision-maker fails to consider something it is bound to consider; consideration requires an active intellectual process directed at the material ( Tickner v Chapman (1995) 57 FCR 451; Plaintiff M1/2021 v Minister for Home Affairs (2022) 275 CLR 582 ), the required effort varying with its content and relevance; the decision must proceed on the most recent and accurate information at hand ( Peko-Wallsend ); weight is otherwise for the decision-maker ( Peko-Wallsend ).
Application

Section 34(2)(b) and (c) make the adequacy of the applicant's facilities and premises central statutory criteria. Ms Anand's application squarely addressed them with a description of her facilities, two veterinary references and Dr Lim's independent assessment concluding the cattery was "exemplary". Mr Boyle did not read any of this material. That is not a question of weight but of complete non-engagement: a blanket failure to read the material negates any active intellectual process ( Tickner ), and the decision was not made on the most recent and accurate information at hand ( Peko-Wallsend ). The Council might argue it needed only be satisfied of one ground to refuse and so was not bound to weigh the facilities evidence; but where its reasons purport to find her not "fit and proper", the unread expert evidence of exemplary husbandry was a consideration it was bound to confront.
Conclusion
Strong ground: the only substantive evidence going to the statutory facilities/premises and fitness criteria was ignored.
4.4 Taking into Account an Irrelevant Consideration
Issue
Whether the Council took into account matters it was bound not to consider.
Rule (ratio)
The Act may, expressly or by implication, require that a matter not be taken into account ( Peko-Wallsend ). Where the permissible grounds are exhaustively stated, matters outside them are irrelevant.
Application

The Council relied on: untrue anonymous "PawPrint" allegations of hoarding (an extraneous and unverified matter); the existence of "a sufficient number of breeders" and the population target (matters outside s 34(2)); and Ms Anand's public criticism of the Council (relevant only via the ultra vires reg 12). None is a consideration the Act permitted the Council to act upon in deciding suitability to breed. The Council will say the anonymous material was relevant to "fit and proper"; but acting on unverified, untrue anonymous rumour, without inquiry, is the paradigm of reliance on an irrelevant (and legally insufficient) consideration.
Conclusion
Strong ground; closely tied to 4.1, 4.3 and unreasonableness (4.10).
4.5 Invalid Delegation
Issue
Whether the decision was made by a person with authority, and whether the decision-maker improperly acted under another's dictation.
Rule (ratio)
Whether a statutory power must be exercised personally by its designated repository, or may be exercised by another, is a question of statutory construction ( Pattenden v Commissioner of Taxation [2008] FCA 1590 (Logan J)).
This depends on the nature of the power and other circumstances, like size of the department, geographical area that the department is responsible for, frequency of the kind of decision making ( Pattenden ).

The Carltona principle permits a repository to act through duly authorised officers where the nature, scope and purpose of the function make personal exercise impractical ( Carltona Ltd v Commissioners of Works [1943] 2 All ER 560 ; discussed by Mason J in Minister for Aboriginal Affairs v Peko-Wallsend Ltd (1986) 162 CLR 24 ). Where, however, the statute confers an express and specific power of delegation , that power defines and confines how the function may be devolved.
Application

Authority. Section 40(1) permits the Council to delegate its functions only to its CEO . The Council validly delegated to CEO Whitlock (by absolute majority, in writing, recorded in the register: s 40(2)–(3)). But the CEO did not decide; she instructed Mr Boyle, the Animal Management Coordinator, who is not the CEO , and there is no instrument of delegation to him . On the face of s 40(1), Mr Boyle had no authority to exercise the power, and his decision is a nullity. The Council's likely answer is twofold: (i) s 42(1) refers to a register of delegations "to the CEO and to employees ", which it will say contemplates employee-level delegation; and (ii) the Carltona principle and the Interpretation Act 1984 (as applied by s 41) permit the CEO to act through departmental officers. The better view is that s 40(1) is the sole, specific delegation power and is confined to the CEO; s 42(1) is a record-keeping provision that cannot enlarge the substantive delegation power; and in any event s 40(2) requires any delegation to be "in writing", which is absent for Mr Boyle, so neither a sub-delegation nor a Carltona alter-ego argument succeeds for a discretionary, individuated licensing decision of this kind. Section 41 (which applies the Interpretation Act 1984 ss 58–59 to delegations under the Division) does not assist the Council either: those provisions regulate the effect and exercise of delegations that have been validly made, and cannot supply a written delegation to Mr Boyle that was never made. The Council may finally invoke Pattenden 's acknowledgment that authority to act in the repository's name need not rest on a formal instrument but may be found in departmental practice ; but Pattenden itself held that where the substantive value judgment is made by an unauthorised subordinate the decision is invalid, and the factors there identified — the nature of the power, and the volume and routineness of its exercise — tell decisively against any informal agency arrangement for an individuated, livelihood-destroying licensing decision. This is a clean and powerful ground.

Dictation. Independently, Cr Sage's email of 1 May 2026 directed that "the Anand application must be refused", and Mr Boyle's file note records that he "would do as the Councillor required". If the decision-maker surrendered the statutory discretion to the Councillor's direction, the decision was made under dictation and is invalid. The Council may answer that the email and file note merely evidence the political context, and that the mere receipt of a direction or representation does not establish abdication where the decision-maker still formed their own view on the listed grounds. That answer is weak here: the file note records not that Mr Boyle weighed the Councillor's view but that he "would do as the Councillor required" — on its face, surrender of the discretion rather than independent judgment.
Conclusion
The unauthorised-decision-maker ground is strong and, if accepted, voids the decision wholesale without any need to prove anything further. The dictation ground is a strong, independent alternative. The s 42 counter-argument must be confronted but is answerable.
4.6 Procedural Fairness — Hearing Rule
Issue
Whether the Council denied Ms Anand procedural fairness.
Rule (ratio)

The hearing rule applies unless clearly excluded — a question of statutory construction attended by a strong common law presumption that procedural fairness conditions any power "capable of having an adverse effect on legally recognised rights or interests" ( Badari v Northern Land Council [2025] HCA 47; Kioa v West (1985) 159 CLR 550; Saeed v Minister for Immigration and Citizenship (2010) 241 CLR 252 ). Its content is flexible, appropriate and adapted to the circumstances, determined by the facts and the scope and nature of the power, and can range from a full trial to nothingness ( Badari; Brennan J, Kioa; Minister for Immigration and Border Protection v WZARH (2015) 256 CLR 326 ); where adverse information is credible, relevant and significant , there is generally a requirement to disclose it and give an opportunity to respond ( Brennan J, Kioa ); the opportunity must be real ( Nathanson v Minister for Home Affairs (2022) 276 CLR 80 ).
Circumstances that can affect whether natural justice applies [10.3.27] Urgent decision
Implying fairness would undermine the purpose of legislation granting a prison official an “absolute discretion” to place and classify prisons.
Highly sensitive work conducted (e.g. national security)
Application

The Act does not exclude procedural fairness (s 37 provides for reasons after the event but says nothing displacing a prior hearing); the presumption therefore applies to a decision that destroys an existing approval and reputation. The Council relied on credible-looking but untrue anonymous allegations of hoarding that were never put to Ms Anand, and decided without telling her that her renewal was in jeopardy or inviting a response — precisely the vice in Kioa and Nathanson . Had she been given the chance, she could readily have rebutted the allegations (as the facts show them to be false), so the denial was material (6). The Council may argue the statutory scheme (application plus s 37 reasons) impliedly limits the hearing to the written application; but that does not licence reliance on undisclosed extraneous adverse material. Nor can the Council shelter behind Badari 's distinction between formulating general policy (where the content of fairness may be minimal) and applying power to an individual: the decision under challenge is the paradigm individual application, where the presumption operates with full force.

The content of fairness owed is, moreover, higher in respect of the cancellation than the renewal: cancellation under s 35 extinguishes an existing right (Ms Anand's approval, continued in force by reg 15), and the deprivation of an existing entitlement attracts a fuller hearing than the refusal of a fresh grant ( Kioa ).
Conclusion
Strong hearing-rule breach in the use of undisclosed, adverse and untrue allegations; the breach is the more serious as regards the cancellation of her existing approval.
4.7 Procedural Fairness — Bias
Issue
Whether the decision is vitiated by actual bias or a reasonable apprehension of bias.
Rule (ratio)
Actual bias requires a mind so committed to a conclusion as to be incapable of alteration whatever the evidence or argument ( Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs v Jia Legeng (2001) 205 CLR 507 (‘Jia Legeng’)); it requires proof of the decision-maker's subjective state of mind. Natural justice does not require the absence of any predisposition or inclination.

Apprehended bias : a decision-maker is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the decision-maker might not bring an impartial mind to the decision ( Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy (2000) 205 CLR 337 ); the test is a real (not remote) possibility, applied with adaptation outside the courts, and requires (1) identification of what might lead to a deviation from deciding on the merits, and (2) articulation of the logical connection between that matter and the feared deviation. No actual fault needs to be shown (Hot Holding, Gleeson CJ).
Application

Actual bias is well supported: Mr Boyle's public PawPrint statement (November 2025) that "the Lotus Cattery operator... should never be allowed to breed animals", made months before deciding, evidences a mind already committed; coupled with his file note that he "would do as the Councillor required", there is a strong case of prejudgment. Actual bias is, however, always hard to prove, so the safer formulation is apprehended bias , which is compelling here: (a) Mr Boyle's sister runs a competing cattery that stands to gain commercially from Lotus Cattery's closure; (b) Mr Boyle investigated the earlier complaints and then decided the application (an investigator-and-decider concern); and (c) his prior public condemnation. Prejudgment expressed on social media may found apprehended bias ( Hendry v Western Australia [No 3] [2025] FCA 1262 ): Mr Boyle's PawPrint post, naming Lotus Cattery's operator and pre-stating the very conclusion he later reached, is the paradigm. A fair-minded lay observer would readily apprehend that he might not bring an impartial mind, and the logical connection (family/commercial advantage; prior public position) is clear. The Council will respond that a sibling's commercial interest is too remote, that officials are expected to hold and express policy views ( Jia Legeng ), and, faintly, necessity — that in a small administration the only available officer may be one with prior involvement (cf. the qualified tolerance in Hot Holdings v Creasy of departmental officers with peripheral interests participating in advice).
