Necessity fails on these facts twice over: the statute itself designates the alternative decision-makers (the Council, or its CEO under s 40), so Mr Boyle was never necessary; and Hot Holdings protected peripheral involvement by non-deciders, not a decider with a direct familial commercial interest and a published prejudgment of this very applicant. These answers do not meet the combination present here — a direct family member's competing cattery, a publicly pre-stated conclusion about this very applicant, and the investigator-and-decider overlap — which together comfortably satisfy the real-possibility test. (There is also an internal point: if the delegation ground at 4.5 succeeds, Mr Boyle was never the lawful decision-maker, so the bias attaches to a nullity; the two grounds are best pleaded as alternatives.)

Conclusion
Apprehended bias is a strong, largely knock-out ground (and bias requires no separate materiality inquiry: see 6). Actual bias is arguable but harder.
FOR REVIEW — The leading on-point authority on apprehended bias of a council officer involved in an animal destruction order is Isbester v Knox City Council (2015) 255 CLR 135 (officer who prosecuted the dog owner then sat on the panel ordering destruction). It is directly analogous to Mr Boyle's investigator-and-decider role and to the destruction of Maximus, and should be cited as the lead authority alongside Ebner . It does not appear in the current course materials, so it is flagged here for confirmation before filing.
4.8 Objective Jurisdictional Fact
Issue
Whether a criterion conditioning the power, objectively determinable by the Court, was satisfied.
Rule (ratio)

A jurisdictional fact is a criterion "satisfaction of which enlivens the power of the decision-maker to exercise a discretion"; whether a criterion answers that description is a question of construction ( City of Enfield v Development Assessment Commission (2000) 199 CLR 135; Plaintiff M70/2011 v Minister for Immigration and Citizenship (2011) 244 CLR 144 ); where it is objectively determinable, the Court decides on the evidence whether it exists (plurality, M70 ); where it involves the decision-maker's value judgment, it is harder to treat as an objective jurisdictional fact (French CJ, M70 ).
Application

The argument is that the existence of a "conviction within the previous three years" under s 34(2)(d) is an objective , court-determinable fact: there was no such conviction (a 2019 infringement notice under unrelated legislation is neither a conviction, nor within three years, nor under the named Acts), so the condition for refusing on that basis did not exist. The counter-argument , which must be confronted, is that s 34(2) does not condition the power on the objective facts but on the local government being " satisfied " that a listed matter applies — which points to a subjective jurisdictional fact (the state of satisfaction), reviewable on the SZMDS standard and as a no-evidence error (4.8a, 4.9), rather than an objective jurisdictional fact determined by the court (cf. M70 , French CJ: criteria involving the decision-maker's assessment are difficult to treat as objective jurisdictional facts). The better view is that the primary characterisation is subjective satisfaction / no evidence, with the objective-fact analysis available in the alternative because whether a person has been "convicted" of a specified offence within a fixed period is itself an objectively ascertainable matter on which any rational satisfaction must rest. As to the destruction , s 31(2) is framed on the operator's "reasonable belief", so it is not a pure objective jurisdictional fact; but the existence of reasonable grounds is objectively assessed, and on the conjunctive "serious injury" limb the operator could not have held the requisite belief because that limb was not alleged.

Conclusion
The "conviction" point is best run primarily as a no-evidence error (4.8a) and as an unlawfully-formed satisfaction (4.9), and in the further alternative as an objective jurisdictional fact that did not exist; on either characterisation, refusal on s 34(2)(d) was beyond power. The destruction is best analysed as excess of power (4.1) given the "reasonable belief" standard.
4.8a No-Evidence Ground
FOR REVIEW — A discrete "no evidence" ground does not appear in the seminar checklist (the slides cite Bond only for the ADJR meaning of "decision"). The substance of this argument is fully available within the taught categories — the s 34(2) satisfaction was irrational on the SZMDS standard (4.9) and the decision legally unreasonable (4.10) — so this head is included for completeness and should be deployed through those categories if the examiner's checklist is preferred.
Issue
Whether the Council made critical findings for which there was no probative evidence.
Rule (ratio)
A decision-maker who makes a finding of fact that is a critical step in the decision, in circumstances where there is no probative evidence to support it, commits a reviewable error ( Australian Broadcasting Tribunal v Bond (1990) 170 CLR 321 ). The error must concern a material finding; it is not enough that the evidence was merely thin or that the applicant disagrees with the weight given.
Application

Two findings rest on no probative evidence. First , reason 2 — that Ms Anand "was convicted within the previous three years of an offence against the Animal Welfare Act 2002" — is supported by no evidence at all: her only record is a 2019 infringement notice (not a conviction) under unrelated legislation. Second , the finding (reason 1) that she is "not a fit and proper person" rests solely on untrue, anonymous "PawPrint" allegations of hoarding, which are no probative material, while the only actual evidence (Dr Lim's assessment; the veterinary references) was not read. The Council may answer that "fit and proper" is an evaluative judgment for which some material (the anonymous posts) existed, so the no-evidence ground (which is narrow) is not made out as to reason 1. That answer has force for reason 1 (better run as unreasonableness, 4.10), but it does not save reason 2, for which there is simply no evidence of the asserted conviction.
Conclusion
Reason 2 is a clear no-evidence error ( Bond ); the "fit and proper" finding is better attacked as unreasonable (4.10) than as strictly no-evidence, though it is arguable in the alternative.
4.9 Subjective Jurisdictional Fact
Issue
Whether the Council's state of satisfaction was lawfully formed.
Rule (ratio)

Where a power is conditioned on the formation of a state of mind, the existence of that state of mind is itself a jurisdictional fact ( French CJ, M70 ); if formed on a misconstruction of the relevant matters it is not the satisfaction the statute requires ( M70 ); the satisfaction is reviewable for irrationality, but Minister for Immigration and Citizenship v SZMDS (2010) 240 CLR 611 discloses two approaches: Crennan and Bell JJ (Majority) asked whether the satisfaction was a conclusion open on the evidence — irrationality is shown only if no logical route led to it — while Gummow A-CJ and Kiefel J (Dissenting) focused on illogicality in the process of reasoning .
Application

Section 34(2) is enlivened only "if the local government is satisfied " of a listed ground, and reason 1 records satisfaction that Ms Anand "is not a fit and proper person". That satisfaction was formed without reading the exemplary independent assessment and on the basis of untrue anonymous rumour and legally irrelevant matters; on the SZMDS standard there is a real argument that no rational decision-maker could have been so satisfied on the material, and that the satisfaction rests on a misconstruction of "fit and proper". Ms Anand succeeds on either SZMDS approach: the conclusion was not open (the only probative material — Dr Lim's assessment and the references — pointed uniformly the other way and was unread), and the process was illogical (treating anonymous, uninvestigated and untrue posts as probative of unfitness while declining to read the expert material). That the case satisfies even the stricter, outcome-focused formulation is what lifts this ground above the usual fate of SZMDS arguments. The Council will nonetheless invoke the high threshold and the latitude owed to an evaluative judgment, so the ground is best pleaded together with the no-evidence and consideration grounds rather than alone.
Conclusion

Stronger than the usual SZMDS argument, because the satisfaction fails even the stricter outcome-focused formulation; prudently pleaded alongside the no-evidence and consideration grounds.
4.10 Unreasonableness
Issue
Whether the decision was legally unreasonable.
Rule (ratio)
The legislature is taken to intend that a statutory discretion will be exercised reasonably ( Attorney-General (NSW) v Quin (1990) 170 CLR 1; Li; Minister for Immigration and Border Protection v SZVFW (2018) 264 CLR 541 ). A decision is legally unreasonable if it lacks an evident and intelligible justification ( Minister for Immigration and Citizenship v Li (2013) 249 CLR 332 ); the test is "necessarily stringent" ( Kiefel CJ, SZVFW ; see also Gageler J in Li ), and reasonableness is judged at the time of, and in the particular circumstances of, the exercise of power ( Minister for Home Affairs v DUA16 (2020) 271 CLR 550 ).
Application

The cumulative features — refusing a fifteen-year, award-winning breeder; ignoring uncontradicted expert evidence of exemplary facilities; preferring untrue anonymous rumour without inquiry; relying on a non-existent conviction and on extraneous policy and political matters — together lack any evident and intelligible justification. The Council's real point is Brennan J's caution in Attorney-General (NSW) v Quin (1990) 170 CLR 1 : the court's duty extends to declaring and enforcing the limits of power, "and no further" — the merits of who should breed cats in Whitestone are for the Council. But this challenge stays on the legality side of that line: the complaint is not that the Council weighed the material badly, but that it did not engage with the statutorily mandated criteria at all — an unexplained outcome of the kind from which Li permits the inference of unreasonableness. Ms Anand does not ask the Court to find her fit and proper; she asks it to hold that no intelligible path connected the material before the decision-maker to the satisfaction recorded. Unreasonableness is, moreover, powerfully reinforced by the established failures (4.3, 4.4) and, once those are accepted, follows naturally. Like bias, legal unreasonableness calls for no separate materiality inquiry (6).
Conclusion
A strong reinforcing ground that follows from the consideration grounds.

4.11 Inflexible Application of Policy / Fettering (and Unlawful Policy)
Issue
Whether the Council fettered its discretion by applying a policy as a binding rule.
Rule (ratio)
A policy may guide but must not control the exercise of a statutory discretion; applying it as an inflexible rule that prevents consideration of the individual case is unlawful ( Green v Daniels (1977) 13 ALR 1 ); an appropriate policy "guides but does not control" and must leave the discretion intact ( Re Drake and Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs (No 2) (1979) 2 ALD 634 ); a policy inconsistent with the statute is itself unlawful ( Drake (No 2) ).
Application
Reason 4 states the Council's " firm position that no further breeding approvals will be granted while the district's cat population exceeds target", reflecting the Whitestone Responsible Cat Ownership Policy 2025. Applied as an absolute bar, it prevented genuine consideration of Ms Anand's individual application — the vice in Green v Daniels . The policy is also inconsistent with the Act : s 34(2) exhaustively states when an approval "may" be refused, and a blanket no-approval policy keyed to population targets introduces a refusal criterion the Act does not authorise ( Drake (No 2) ). The Council may argue the policy merely guided it; but "firm position" and the blanket terms indicate control, not guidance.
Conclusion

Strong fettering ground, reinforcing 4.1 and 4.4.
4.12 Ultra Vires Delegated Legislation (the Regulations)
Issue
Whether regulation 12 (and regulation 8) of the Cat Regulations 2025 (Vic) are invalid, and the effect on the decision.
Rule (ratio)

Delegated legislation is valid only if capable of being reasonably considered appropriate and adapted to achieve the prescribed purpose of the empowering Act — a stringent test, satisfied only in an extreme case ( Vanstone v Clark (2005) 147 FCR 299 (Weinberg J)). Weinberg J's catalogue identifies five bases of invalidity: the regulation (i) deals with a subject outside the scope of the empowering power; (ii) deals with a permissible subject but exceeds the prescribed limits; (iii) is inconsistent with or repugnant to the empowering or another Act; (iv) is made for an impermissible purpose ( R v Toohey; Ex parte Northern Land Council (1981) 151 CLR 170 ); or (v) is so unreasonable, or so lacking in reasonable proportionality to the empowering provision, that it falls outside the legislature's contemplation. Two caveats: the full range of judicial review grounds is not available against delegated legislation (it cannot, for example, be impugned solely for failure to consider relevant considerations); and a regulation that expands the operative statutory criteria needs a suitably specific head of power — a general "necessary or convenient" power will not ordinarily suffice ( Vanstone ). The analysis is two-step: (1) is the regulation invalid; (2) how does that affect the decision (typically as an irrelevant consideration or legal unreasonableness). At the State level, validity is challenged in the Supreme Court's inherent jurisdiction.
Application

Regulation 12 prescribes, as a refusal ground, that the applicant has "made public statements critical of the local government's animal-management policies". That bears no rational connection to the Act's objects (s 3: identifying and recording cats; promoting responsible ownership; avoiding cruel confinement). Nor is there a sufficient head of power: s 55(1) is a general "necessary or convenient" power, which on Vanstone cannot support a regulation that expands the substantive criteria for refusing an approval, and s 55(2)(g) ("the regulation of approved cat breeders") regulates the conduct of existing breeders, not the criteria for becoming one. It is neither appropriate nor adapted to those purposes; it is, in substance, a power to punish criticism, which is an improper purpose ( Toohey ) and is not reasonably proportionate to the empowering provision ( Vanstone ). It is therefore ultra vires s 55. The Council's best contrary argument is that reg 12 promotes "responsible ownership" (s 3(b)) and falls within the regulation-making power as to "the regulation of approved cat breeders" (s 55(2)(g)); but penalising public criticism of the Council has no rational connection to responsible cat ownership and is not a genuine regulation of breeding, so the argument fails ( Toohey; Vanstone ). Regulation 8 (a cap of ten approvals per year) is more defensible as population management, but to the extent it compels refusal of an otherwise qualified applicant on a basis outside the exhaustive s 34(2) grounds, it too is arguably inconsistent with the Act. Regulation 8 also invites a narrower, and forensically more valuable, answer: it caps the number of approvals a council may " grant ", but the Act consistently distinguishes the grant and the renewal of an approval (ss 33(1), 34(1)(a)–(b)). On its text, reg 8 does not reach renewals at all — so it could never have supported refusing Ms Anand. This construction also hands the respondent its best fallback (reading reg 8 down to new grants, where it is defensible, rather than holding it wholly invalid), and Ms Anand should embrace it: she needs reg 8 distinguished , not destroyed. Reg 12, by contrast, cannot be read down or severed — its vice (penalising criticism) infects the whole provision. Step 2: reason 3 rests entirely on reg 12; if reg 12 is invalid, the Council acted on an irrelevant/legally erroneous consideration, reinforcing 4.1a, 4.2 and 4.4.

Conclusion
Regulation 12 is very likely ultra vires; its invalidity removes reason 3 and strengthens the error-of-law and improper-purpose grounds. Regulation 8 is more arguable.
4.13 Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities Act 2006 (Vic)
Issue
Whether the Council, as a public authority, acted unlawfully under the Charter, providing an additional ground of review.
Rule (ratio)

By s 38(1) of the Charter it is unlawful for a public authority to act in a way incompatible with a human right, or, in making a decision, to fail to give proper consideration to a relevant human right. Section 38(1) has a procedural limb (proper consideration) and a substantive limb (compatibility), which are cumulative ( Bare v Independent Broad-based Anti-corruption Commission (2015) 48 VR 129; Certain Children v Minister for Families and Children (No 2) (2017) 52 VR 441; Thompson v Minogue (2021) 67 VR 301 ). Proper consideration requires the decision-maker to (1) understand in general terms which rights may be affected and how; (2) seriously turn their mind to the possible impact on the person's rights; (3) identify the countervailing interests; and (4) balance the competing interests. 'Proper' consideration sets a standard higher than that generally applicable at common law ( Thompson ; see also Bare; Certain Children ). Any limit on a right must be demonstrably justified under s 7(2)(a)–(e) ( Certain Children; Thompson ), the Court approaching s 7(2) with a limited degree of deference. There is no stand-alone Charter cause of action : by s 39 , Charter unlawfulness may be relied on only where the applicant already has an independent ground of review (the "piggy-backing" requirement: Bare ). Breach of s 38(1) does not of itself give rise to invalidity or constitute jurisdictional error ( Certain Children ).
Application

The Council is a "public authority" (Charter s 4). Relevant rights are engaged: freedom of expression (s 15) — reason 3 and reg 12 penalise Ms Anand precisely for her public criticism; property (s 20) — the seizure of her cats and the threatened destruction of Maximus deprive her of property; and privacy and reputation (s 13) — the "not fit and proper" finding, based on untrue rumour, and the entry to and search of her home. Nothing in the reasons suggests the Council undertook the four-step proper-consideration exercise in respect of any of these rights; the procedural limb is therefore breached. The substantive limb is also engaged, and the s 7(2) factors should be worked through rather than asserted. (a) Nature of the right : political expression — criticism of a council's policy at a public meeting and in the press — sits at the core of s 15. (b) Importance of the purpose : cat population management is a legitimate, if modest, objective. (c) Nature and extent of the limitation : reg 12 does not regulate expression incidentally; it attaches a livelihood-destroying consequence directly and only to the fact of criticism, with a two-year reach-back. (d) Relationship between limitation and purpose : none — silencing critics does not reduce the cat population; the rational-connection limb fails outright, which is ordinarily fatal. (e) Less restrictive means : the Act already supplies them (facilities, premises, fitness, convictions: s 34(2)(b)–(e)). The limit is not demonstrably justified at the first proportionality step, so the Court need not reach fine balancing. The property limitation (destroying a docile cat without alleging the statutory serious-injury limb) fails for the same threshold reason. The Council may argue (i) that, under s 38(2), it could not reasonably have acted differently or made a different decision because of a statutory provision (reg 12); but that defence fails if reg 12 is itself invalid (4.12), and the obligation to interpret compatibly (s 32) does not assist a regulation that cannot be read down; and (ii) that, per Certain Children , breach does not itself amount to jurisdictional error. The Charter ground must therefore piggy-back an established ground of review (s 39) — which is readily available here (4.1–4.12) — and operates as an additional badge of unlawfulness rather than a free-standing path to relief.

Conclusion
The Charter adds a real additional ground: the Council failed to give proper consideration to Ms Anand's rights to expression, property and privacy/reputation, and the limits are not demonstrably justified. By s 39 it must be run with an independent ground (of which there are many), and by Certain Children it does not of itself establish jurisdictional error.
4.14 The Cancellation (s 35) and the Seizure of the Four Cats (s 50)
Issue
Whether the cancellation and the seizure are independently reviewable and what relief follows.
Rule (ratio)
Each discrete exercise of statutory power is separately reviewable. Cancellation under s 35 is permitted only "if one or more of the things set out in section 34(2) applies " to the breeder. A warrant to seize under s 50 may issue only where the justice is satisfied of "reasonable grounds for suspecting that the cat is... the subject of an offence... against this Act"; a warrant is the act of a judicial officer and is itself amenable to certiorari/prohibition where issued without jurisdiction.
Application

Cancellation (s 35). The cancellation is vitiated by the same defects as the refusal (unauthorised decision-maker, bias, denial of fairness, no evidence, irrelevant considerations, fettering). Three points are specific to it. First, s 35 is conditioned on whether a s 34(2) matter "applies", which is arguably a more objective standard than the s 34(2) "is satisfied" formula — strengthening the no-evidence and objective-jurisdictional-fact attacks on the cancellation. Second, because cancellation extinguishes an existing approval, the procedural fairness owed was higher (4.6). Third, the simultaneity of the two decisions creates a dilemma for the Council. If the refusal validly determined the renewal application, the reg 15 continuation expired at that instant — leaving nothing for the cancellation to operate on; the cancellation is otiose. If the refusal is invalid, the application remains undetermined , reg 15 keeps the approval in force, and the cancellation falls to be tested under s 35 — where it fails because no s 34(2) matter "applies" (a formulation notably more objective than the "is satisfied" language of s 34(2) itself, inviting full curial determination). On either horn the cancellation does no independent work for the Council, and on the second it supplies the cleanest objective-jurisdictional-fact argument in the case. It remains a distinct, separately reasoned exercise of power with its own (graver) legal consequences, and should be separately quashed.

Seizure (s 50 warrant). The seizure of the four breeding cats depends on a s 50 warrant, which required reasonable grounds to suspect the cats were "the subject of an offence" against the Act. The only candidate offence is breeding without approval (s 32); but by reg 15 Ms Anand's approval continued in force until her renewal application was lawfully determined, and if the refusal and cancellation are invalid (so that there has been no lawful determination, and no valid cancellation) she remained an approved breeder , so no offence was committed and there was no jurisdictional foundation for the warrant. This argument must, however, confront a temporal objection . Section 50 conditions the warrant on the justice's satisfaction of "reasonable grounds for suspecting" an offence, assessed on the material before the justice at the time of issue — and on 22 May the cancellation had not been set aside and appeared operative. The Council will say a suspicion can be reasonable even if its factual premise is later falsified. The answer lies in Bhardwaj : a decision affected by jurisdictional error is "in law, no decision at all", so the cancellation never had legal effect and Ms Anand was at all times an approved breeder by force of reg 15 — there was never an offence to suspect. But Bhardwaj nullity is not absolute (a purported decision may have practical and even some legal consequences until set aside), and the safer pleading is twofold: first, that the suspicion was not objectively reasonable even on the material presented (the warrant application necessarily rested wholly on the Council's own assertion of cancellation, advanced by the same conflicted officer); and secondly, that whatever the warrant's validity at issue, no continued impoundment can survive once the cancellation is quashed — so the order for the return of the cats does not depend on quashing the warrant at all. That distinction matters for the respondent set: the issuing justice should be named as a respondent to the certiorari claim but would be expected to submit save as to costs ( R v Australian Broadcasting Tribunal; Ex parte Hardiman (1980) 144 CLR 13 ), while the return of the four cats is sought against the Council as the facility operator holding them. This is a discrete and important head of relief, distinct from the quia timet protection of Maximus.

Conclusion
The cancellation should be separately quashed (and is, if anything, more vulnerable than the refusal); the s 50 warrant and seizure are challengeable, and relief should include an order for the return of all four seized cats, not merely the protection of Maximus.
4.15 Grounds Considered and Not Separately Pursued
For completeness, three further matters were considered and are not separately pursued. Bad faith : although the sister's competing cattery, the PawPrint post and the "would do as the Councillor required" note could support a bad-faith argument, bad faith carries a very high threshold and is rarely necessary where improper purpose (4.2) and bias (4.7) are available; it is not separately run. Legitimate expectation : not pursued — the concept provides no basis for determining whether, or with what content, procedural fairness is owed ( Kiefel, Bell and Keane JJ, WZARH ; see also Re Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs; Ex parte Lam (2003) 214 CLR 1 ), and Ms Anand's procedural entitlements are fully vindicated at 4.6. Failure to make obvious inquiries : the failure to read Dr Lim's assessment, or to check the anonymous allegations, could support a narrow failure-to-inquire argument; it is best run as reinforcing unreasonableness (4.10) rather than as a separate ground.
5. The Strongest Ground — Comparative Assessment

Before turning to breach and relief, the grounds should be ranked: the choice of lead ground determines what must be proved (in particular, whether materiality must be shown at all), who the necessary respondents are, and what relief follows. The grounds are not of equal strength. Assessing them by the certainty of the breach, resistance to counter-argument, whether they void the whole decision, the materiality burden, and the value of the resulting relief:
Unauthorised decision-maker / invalid delegation (4.5) is, with bias, the strongest. If Mr Boyle lacked authority under s 40, the decision is a nullity regardless of its merits, and no materiality need be shown. The only real obstacle is the s 42(1) "employees" counter-argument, which is answerable (s 40(1) is the sole, specific delegation power, confined to the CEO, and there is no written instrument to Mr Boyle).
Apprehended bias (4.7) is equally powerful: the facts (a competing-cattery sibling; investigator-and-decider; a prior public condemnation) make the fair-minded-observer test easy to satisfy, and bias is jurisdictional irrespective of materiality (LPDT). It voids the entire decision.

Acting without authority / error of law (4.1, 4.1a) and the related no-evidence ground (4.8a) are clean and largely incontestable on the face of the reasons (reasons 4–5 outside s 34(2); the non-existent "conviction"; the ultra vires reg 12). The Council will argue that, because s 34(2) requires only "one or more" grounds, a single valid and independently sufficient ground (reason 1) can support the refusal notwithstanding the invalid surplus; that reflects the orthodox severance principle, but it fails here because reason 1 ("fit and proper") is itself vitiated (no evidence, bias, ignored evidence), so no valid ground survives.
Failure to consider relevant considerations (4.3) and reliance on irrelevant considerations (4.4) are strong and mutually reinforcing, and feed unreasonableness (4.10) .
Ultra vires delegated legislation (4.12) is strong as to reg 12 and decisively removes reason 3.
Improper purpose (4.2) is unusually well-evidenced but remains fact-sensitive; subjective jurisdictional fact (4.9) faces the high SZMDS threshold — though, as argued at 4.9, this case satisfies even the stricter outcome-focused formulation — and is best pleaded alongside the no-evidence and consideration grounds; the Charter (4.13) adds an additional badge of unlawfulness but must piggy-back and does not itself establish jurisdictional error.

The ground chosen for the full consequences-of-breach analysis is the unauthorised-decision-maker ground (4.5). It is chosen because the breach is established on undisputed documentary facts (there is simply no instrument of delegation to Mr Boyle), it admits of no evaluative reply, and it voids the decision wholesale; apprehended bias (4.7) is maintained as the alternative lead. The two-stage analysis at section 6 is undertaken for that ground, with the others as alternatives.

Is any one ground sufficient to obtain the relief sought? Yes. Either the unauthorised-decision-maker ground or the apprehended-bias ground, if accepted, is independently sufficient to quash the entire decision (and neither requires proof that the outcome would have differed). Pleading order matters : the delegation ground should be put first, because if Mr Boyle was never the repository of the power the Court need not enter the contested factual territory of bias and purpose at all — though bias must be maintained in the alternative, since if the delegation argument fails (s 42 "employees") the bias case becomes the lead ground; and conversely, if delegation succeeds, the bias findings would attach to a nullity and the Court may decline to decide them. The first filing should be the interlocutory injunction application for Maximus , supported by the usual undertaking as to damages, with the originating motion to follow within the Order 56 period. The remaining grounds provide robust, mutually reinforcing alternatives and support the consequential relief (the injunction protecting Maximus, the return of the four seized cats, and the declarations). The cancellation (s 35) is, if anything, more vulnerable than the refusal, and the s 50 seizure falls with the cancellation (4.14).
6. Breach and Consequences of Breach
Issue

Whether the established errors are jurisdictional, so that the decision is invalid.
Rule (ratio)

Jurisdictional error is "a breach of an express or implied condition of a statutory conferral of decision-making authority which results in a decision made in the purported exercise of that authority lacking the legal force attributed to exercise of that authority by statute" ( LPDT ). Consistently with the approach required for the exam, the full two-stage analysis is undertaken for the chosen lead ground — the unauthorised decision-maker (4.5) — with the remaining grounds as alternatives. Step 1 (Project Blue Sky Inc v Australian Broadcasting Authority (1998) 194 CLR 355): whether it was a purpose of the legislation that an act done in breach of the relevant condition should be invalid. Step 2 (materiality, LPDT v Minister for Immigration, Citizenship and Multicultural Affairs (2024) 280 CLR 321): in most cases an error is jurisdictional only if material, in the sense that there is a realistic possibility the decision could have been different had the error not occurred — a backward-looking inquiry, answered by reference to the decision that was in fact made and how it was made ( LPDT ) (a low threshold); a threshold of materiality is ordinarily to be implied as a matter of statutory construction, it being unlikely that Parliament intended a decision to be invalidated by an immaterial error ( Hossain v Minister for Immigration and Border Protection (2018) 264 CLR 123 ). The contrary view — that materiality should not be a criterion of jurisdictional error at all, because Parliament cannot be taken to intend that decision-makers need comply with the law only where non-compliance would change the result ( Nettle and Gordon JJ, Minister for Immigration and Border Protection v SZMTA (2019) 264 CLR 421 ) — has not prevailed, and LPDT now states the orthodox position. Errors of certain kinds — notably apprehended or actual bias and legal unreasonableness , which deprive the decision of a characteristic the statute requires — do not call for a separate materiality inquiry ( LPDT at [16]–[17] ). A decision affected by jurisdictional error is "in law, no decision at all" ( Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs v Bhardwaj (2002) 209 CLR 597 ).
Application

Step 1 (lead ground): Section 40 is not a provision that merely regulates the exercise of functions already conferred (the feature that saved the provision in Project Blue Sky ); it is an essential precondition to the existence of authority in anyone other than the Council itself — Parliament identified, exhaustively and formally (writing; absolute majority; a register), the only person to whom the power could be devolved. A purported exercise by anyone else is precisely what the provision exists to prevent, and Parliament must be taken to have intended it to be invalid. The same is true, in the alternative, of s 34(2)'s exhaustive grounds, the implied obligation of procedural fairness, and the rule against bias: each is a condition on the existence of the authority, not a direction as to its manner of exercise, for a decision affecting livelihood, reputation and property. Step 2 (lead ground): the unauthorised-decision-maker ground requires no materiality inquiry — if Mr Boyle lacked power, there is no "decision" whose hypothetical alternative content could be assessed; the same is true of bias and legal unreasonableness (no separate materiality inquiry: LPDT). For the consideration and procedural-fairness grounds, materiality is plainly satisfied: had the Council read Dr Lim's assessment and put the false allegations to Ms Anand, there is a realistic possibility (indeed a likelihood) the decision would have been different. The errors are therefore material where materiality is required.

Conclusion
The decision is affected by jurisdictional error and is, in law, no decision at all ( Bhardwaj ); it is void and liable to be quashed.
7. Remedies (Part II) — Likely Availability
Given the strength of the grounds, the remedies sought are likely to be granted:
Certiorari to quash the 15 May 2026 decision (the decision being void: Bhardwaj).
Mandamus to compel a lawful redetermination of the renewal application by an authorised, unbiased decision-maker on the relevant considerations. Mandamus issues for actual or constructive failure to perform a public duty — and an unauthorised decision-maker means the Council has constructively failed to determine the application at all. It will compel determination according to law, not a grant: the residual "may" in s 34(2) means even a lawfully constituted decision-maker retains a discretion. But the practical position on redetermination is strong: none of the s 34(2)(a)–(f) grounds is capable of being lawfully made out on the true facts, and where no ground is satisfied the "only if" precondition for any refusal is absent — refusal would simply be unavailable, and renewal must follow. The discretion preserved by "may" is a discretion not to refuse despite satisfaction of a ground; it is not a freestanding discretion to refuse.

A prohibitory (interlocutory and final) injunction to restrain the destruction of Maximus. Given the irreversible harm (destruction of a living animal that, on the facts, is not "dangerous" within s 31(2)), the balance of convenience strongly favours urgent interlocutory relief; Ms Anand has the necessary legal/proprietary interest ( Bateman's Bay ).
An order for the return of the four seized cats and relief against the s 50 warrant: if the cancellation is invalid Ms Anand remained an approved breeder, so the cats were not "the subject of an offence" and there was no foundation for the warrant (4.14).
Declarations that the decision is invalid and that regulation 12 is ultra vires ( Enfield : an actual controversy exists).
The Court always has the discretion to refuse relief ( Enfield ). The presumption is that the Court will grant the remedies that are available and appropriate ( Enfield ).
8. Overall Conclusion (Question 1)

Ms Anand has strong prospects of success . The decision of 15 May 2026 is affected by multiple jurisdictional errors, of which the unauthorised-decision-maker and apprehended-bias grounds are each independently decisive. The application should be brought in the Supreme Court of Victoria under Order 56 (within the 60-day period), seeking certiorari, mandamus, a prohibitory injunction to protect Maximus, and declarations (including that reg 12 is invalid). Friends of Felines Inc has only an arguable standing, so Ms Anand should be the principal applicant. The Charter provides an additional ground (failure to give proper consideration to her rights to expression, property and privacy/reputation), to be run with the established grounds under s 39.
QUESTION 2 — Effect of a Privative Clause (hypothetical s 57)
Issue
How the analysis would differ if the Act contained s 57, providing that a decision "is final and conclusive, and must not be challenged, appealed against, reviewed, quashed or called into question in any court, and is not subject to prohibition, mandamus, injunction, declaration or certiorari, on any ground."
Rule (ratio)

a State privative clause cannot oust the Supreme Court of Victoria's supervisory jurisdiction to review for jurisdictional error : that jurisdiction is a defining characteristic of a State Supreme Court entrenched by Chapter III of the Constitution and cannot be removed by State legislation ( Kirk v Industrial Court (NSW) (2010) 239 CLR 531 ; cf. Plaintiff S157/2002 v Commonwealth (2003) 211 CLR 476 , s 75(v), at the federal level; and Graham v Minister for Immigration and Border Protection (2017) 263 CLR 1 : Parliament cannot deny a court the ability to enforce the legislated limits of an officer's power). The clause is read down so as not to apply to decisions affected by jurisdictional error. By contrast, a sufficiently clear clause may validly exclude review for non-jurisdictional error of law on the face of the record ( Kirk; Craig v South Australia ). A privative clause is also relevant to Project Blue Sky Step 1 , as a statement of Parliament's intention about the consequences of non-compliance.
Application

Section 57, however widely drawn, cannot prevent the Supreme Court of Victoria from reviewing the Council's decision for jurisdictional error . Critically, the grounds identified in Question 1 are, in the main, jurisdictional : an unauthorised decision-maker (s 40), apprehended bias, denial of procedural fairness, acting without authority/excess of power, failure to consider mandatory considerations, and legal unreasonableness are all classic jurisdictional errors. As a threshold point, the decision arguably fails even the first Hickman proviso: a decision made by an unauthorised officer, under dictation, infected by apprehended bias and substantially actuated by the purpose of punishing criticism is not a "bona fide attempt" to exercise the s 34 power, so s 57's protection is never engaged. Beyond that, the construction technique is textual, not merely doctrinal: s 57 protects "a decision of a local government under this Act ". A purported decision affected by jurisdictional error is not a decision "under this Act" at all — it is made outside the authority the Act confers — so it falls outside the clause's own description of what it protects ( Plaintiff S157; Kirk ); such a decision is "in law, no decision at all" ( Bhardwaj ). The clause and the grounds of review are reconciled, not collided. Two further textual points sharpen the position: s 57 speaks only of decisions of a local government , so it does not touch the challenge to reg 12 , which is an instrument of the Governor — the declaration of the regulation's invalidity proceeds wholly unaffected; and the warrant is a decision of a justice , not of a local government, so the certiorari claim against the warrant is likewise untouched. Section 57 therefore does not save the decision against the principal grounds, and certiorari, mandamus, prohibition, injunction and declaration remain available in the Court's inherent jurisdiction. There is also a distinctively Victorian obstacle the Council must overcome before Kirk is even reached: s 85(5) of the Constitution Act 1975 (Vic) provides that a provision repealing, altering or varying the Supreme Court's jurisdiction is of no effect unless the Act states that intention expressly and the prescribed parliamentary statements are made. Nothing suggests s 57 was enacted in compliance with that manner-and-form requirement, so it may be wholly inoperative as a matter of Victorian constitutional law without any need to read it down.

The clause would have two narrower effects. First , to the extent any error is characterised as a non-jurisdictional error of law , review survives only if the error appears on the face of the record — and here the applicant is doubly constrained. At common law the record does not include the reasons unless incorporated ( Craig ); the statutory cure, ALA s 10 (deeming reasons part of the record), is unavailable because the ALA's 30-day period has expired — and s 57 would in any event present a separate question whether the ALA route survives a privative clause at all. The s 37 notice arguably is incorporated in the record as the instrument recording the decision, but the prudent course is the one already taken: frame every error-of-law argument as jurisdictional (misconstruction of the s 34(2)(d) and (e) preconditions goes to the limits of the power: Craig; Kirk ), so that nothing turns on the record. Second , at Project Blue Sky Step 1 , the Council would invoke s 57 as evidence that Parliament did not intend non-compliance to invalidate; but Kirk confines that argument to non-jurisdictional matters, and cannot convert a jurisdictional error into a permissible one. The Charter analysis is unaffected (s 57 does not touch the Court's supervisory jurisdiction, and Charter unlawfulness is in any event raised via s 39 alongside the surviving grounds).
Conclusion

Section 57 would not materially change the advice. The Supreme Court of Victoria's supervisory jurisdiction over jurisdictional error cannot be ousted ( Kirk ; s 85 Constitution Act 1975 (Vic)), and the principal grounds are jurisdictional. At most, s 57 would bar review of any purely non-jurisdictional error of law on the record — which is a reason to plead the error-of-law points as jurisdictional. The remedies (including the injunction protecting Maximus and the declaration of reg 12's invalidity) remain available.
— End of Memorandum —
E. Matters Flagged for Your Review
Isbester v Knox City Council (2015) 255 CLR 135 is the leading apprehended-bias authority directly on point (a council officer who investigated and then decided an animal-destruction matter). It is not in the supplied course materials , so it is flagged 'FOR REVIEW' in the answer (ground 4.7) and should be confirmed before use; the answer's bias analysis stands on Ebner and Jia Legeng regardless.
A handful of other authorities relied on for completeness (for example Pattenden; Samrein; Carltona; Re McBain) should be confirmed as within your course list; the core analysis rests on the listed cases.
The hypothetical is a practice instrument authored for this exercise; it is not a past exam paper.
F. Structural Note (this version)

At the principal's direction, the Strongest Ground — Comparative Assessment has been moved front and centre as section 5, immediately after the grounds and before the breach-and-consequences analysis, because the choice of lead ground drives what must be proved (including whether materiality is required at all), the respondent set, and the relief. All cross-references were renumbered and re-verified. The exam time guide (9:00am start, strongest ground at 1.5x) now appears on the first page of this document.
